Name: P3
Informed consent: In the design of the smart cabin, the comfort levels of the passengers are measured. It is important that passengers are aware of this and can turn off this feature when they don't consent with it. This can be achieved by displaying a message on the touchscreen in fron t of the passenger when the flight starts. A simple message is shown that anonymous data is collected on the passengers comfort to make their flight even more enjoyable. Users can press Yes or No to the question whether they want this feature enabled or not. In addition, passengers can be adviced they book a flight in a plane that hase a "smart cabin", but are able to disable the smart features when they want when booking the flight.
Privacy: To make the machine learing of the smart cabin more effective, some information about the passenger might be needed, for example the weight and age of the passanger (older passengers are more likely to be cold and heavy passengers might wiggle on their seat more often). This data must be filled in by the passenger, but is able to be anonymized. What is also important is that heat camera's used have very low resolution, so that the passenger is not recognizable in the picture. Another thing to consider is that a passenger that is very uncomfortable in a flight (e.g. due to an illness) gets visited by the flight attendent a lot because of how the system works. This might be noted by other passengers and can cause the uncomfortable passenger to feel like he/she is watched and/or judged bu other passengers by exposing his/her potential illness to the whole cabin.
Information security: It is important to make use of a closed system within the airline company for sharing the data with the main server and not use publically accessable services. In flight systems should not be accessible to passengers and be password protected or with a changing code (e.g. a new code for every flight) or even multi level verification (e.g. with a physical key). Another possibility could be to make the comfort meassuring system seperate from the pasenger registration system so that data on comfort level are harder to link to a certain passenger in case the system gets hacked anyway.
Physical safety: Passengers must never be harmed by the smart systems in place. For example in the case of pressure sensors in the seats, a insulating material must be used to shield the passenger from the electronical components in case of a short-curcuit. In addition, wireless communication between seets, computer and flight attendence must not conflict with other communications the plane makes with the ground or other planes (e.g. air traffic controlls) to ensure safety of the flight.
Trust: In the case of a smart cabin in an airplane, trust mostly concers the trust passengers have in the airplane and pilots. Therefore, the trust of passengers in the smart cabin system can get overlooked. Trust in the system can be increased by explaining what is collected and why and to ask permission for this. In addition, when passengers are found to be 'uncomfortable' by the system, passengers must have the ability to not ask for a flight attendent to come by, so they don't have to lay their comfort levels all by the AI system (but still have a say in how they think they feel).
Sustainability: Flying is bad for the environment. Adding even more smart features and thus electronics does not make it less bad. In addition, more electronics means more energy consumption. This has to be taken into account and maybe partly mitigated in the design of the system (e.g. using greener materials or make sure the electronics can be recycled easily). A potential benefit of the touchscreens in front of the passengers could be to display information on emisions caused by the flight to make passengers more aware of the damage they do to the environment.

Name: P4
Informed consent:
Informed consent for a smart home is very complex, since in basically all smart homes many different products made by many different companies can be found. This means that each product will have its own functionalities and its own terms of use. How much each product tracks of the user, how much it sends to a cloud somewhere or even when it is turned on or off will differ per product. The intentions of each company can also be different which will affect how much the product tracks or does not track. For example, you could have a TV made by Sony that only sends data about when it is turned off or on and in the same house have a fridge that tracks all of its contents and shares your food preferences with Samsung. So, within a house many different terms of use can be relevant which means the user has to give many different informed consents to be able to use all of his products and KNOW what they do. This is currently exactly the problem, since terms of use are incredibly long texts and virtually no-one has the time or motivation to read all of the terms of use of their products. Most people would not even read 1 term of use. 

This is a problem because companies track more than you are aware of if you don't read the terms of use. Even IF you read the terms of use, they are often hard to understand and are formulated in a vague way where a company can have their intentions legally in the terms of use but sometimes so hard to understand that you wouldn't understand if you would even read the terms of use. This totally defeats the 'informed' part in 'informed consent'.

To improve informed consent, it would be best to have 1 company deliver all the smart products in your home, similarly to how Google has many apps simultanously on a person's phone. Only having to deal with 1 company makes it easier to understand the companies intentions and you will have research to do as a consumer. A BIG downside to this however, is that you are very much dependent on this 1 company for the functionality of your smart home. If the company suddenly decides to start tracking more of you than you are comfortable with, you are forced to switch to different products which can be a BIG task if you have to change all your appliances at once. 

Informed consent gets even more complex when you have guests over. Maybe you agree with your Amazon Echo listening to you all day, but what if a friend comes over and start sharing a sensitive story of how his wife cheated on him? He probably would not want Amazon Echo listening to that, but he might not even know the Amazon Echo is in the house (if he even knows what the products is and what it does). Informed consent goes beyond the product owner and is incredibly hard to keep intact.

To conclude, the responsibility for good informed consent lays with the companies producing the products, which is bad, since they is really no one to stop them if their intentions are wrong, ESPECIALLY if it is in a product category where the company has no competitors the consumer can switch to (for example: Facebook owning the VR market with the Oculus Quest 2)
Privacy:
Privacy is a very big issue with IoT devices, especially in smart homes since these are places where people spend a lot of time with their close ones. Your home is the place where you can feel safe and where you can do what you want without others noticing. The more IoT devices added, the less you will be 'unnoticed'. For example, if you like to talk to your smart TV to change the volume or change the channel, your TV has to always be listening to make sure it hears you when you are speaking to it. This means the microphone is always on. This can raise very big privacy issues, especially when your smart TV is linked to your personal Google or Facebook account. If you are very passionate about a sports game and start shouting racial slurs, your TV will hear this. As a consumer, you just have to hope your TV does not send this audio to a cloud somewhere. The same problem arises with smart cameras in the home. With smart cameras connected to the internet, the feed from the data is streamed to a cloud server somewhere, so that you can for example watch it on your phone. But what if you just happen to like to walk around naked in the house? Would you want Google to know the colour of your underwear? Very often, people forget these things or simply don't want to think about it because the added benefits of their IoT products are just too good. But this is dangerous, because it means we get used to it. And when we get used to Google knowing when we come home and when we wear which boxer shorts, the boundaries get more vague and companies like Google can start to do more.

A good option for more privacy in the home would be switching to local data processing instead of working cloud services. This means all your data gets stored and analyzed within your own home and stays inside its 4 walls. This however is a more tech savvy option, since you will need to buy more hardware. You will need a data server that is always running and a PC that can always analyze the data and send instruction to all your IoT devices through your WiFi router. Also, the fact that e.g. Google has so much data from households all over the planet means they can easily improve their services. If all of your data is only processed in-house, it can only access your own data and cannot learn from other people's data. This means your system will be less functional.

To conclude, the more privacy you want, the more tech savvy you have to be and the more sacrafices you have to make. Loss of privacy usually goes hand in hand with added functionality, so the mass of people will chose the increased functionality, while losing their privacy in the process. Since most people willingly chose to give up their privacy or are even unaware they are doing it, it is up to the big corporations to responsibly respect the user's privacy. However, since they can achieve big financial gain with people's data, this will probably never be the case.
Information security:
A smart home comes with many connected IoT devices. All these devices collect and send data. These products are usually made by companies that are very new to technology and do not have much experience with data security. For example a company like BOSCH who makes washing machines is just now applying hardware that can send user data over WiFi. If these big companies produce widely used products without applying good cybersecurity, many households are automatically targetable for cyber attacks. Already, many problems have occured with ransomware-attacks that block hospital equipment until the ransom is paid. This practice could also be applied to household devices. Imagine a hacker turning off your freezer and fridge and only turning them on again when you pay ‚Ç¨20 in bitcoin. What would you do? Pay ‚Ç¨20 or let your groceries die? If even Windows machines in hospitals get hacked by ransomware, it is incredibly scary to think how easily hackable our household appliance made by BOSCH and Miele will be. If a tech giant like Microsoft can't protect it's software, why would we expect a kitchen-appliance company to have good cybersecurity?

These possible attacks are scary, but there is so much more that can happen to your smart products. A tech savvy person with bad intentions could easily track what you are watching on TV and perhaps even access the camera that is built into your TV. He could then record you and possibly blackmail you for money. Or, since all smart products have chips inside of them to process requests etc, these chips could collectively be hacked and used to perform DDoS attacks or to mine bitcoin. Imagine your smart fridge getting slow to use because a hacker in Russia in mining bitcoin on it. It's absurd, but already happening.
Physical safety:
A smart home can have many products that have mechanically moving parts. Think of a smart lock, a smart dishwasher, a smart washing machine or curtains that open and close automatically. Even products without moving parts can have an effect on the physical world. Think of your thermostat turning on the heating or your fridge turning on an air compressor. Things can go very wrong when these product start doing things the user didnt intend them to do. Imagine your smart lock automatically opening when your phone is in 3 meter range for 10 seconds. What if your child plays with the phone and leaves it somewhere near the door? Now your front door is unlocked. Or what a sensor in your thermostat breaks and the heating is turned on indefinitely while you are not home? Or what if your washing machine turns out while you cut off the water? This could create a fire hazard when the heating element is turned on without anything to heat up. Or what if your smartdoor doesnt function because the electricy cut off because the house is on fire? This would lock you up in a burning house.

Besides accidental dangers, products could also be taken over by someone with bad intentions. Similarly to how ransomwhere could block a products functioning, a hacker could also turn on your heating system to the maximum setting until you pay him ransom.
Trust:
The more IoT devices you own and use, the more trust you must have in the companies that produced the products you use. So, to optimally make use of a smart home, your collective trust in these companies must high. Also, like mentioned before, since it is unreasonable for someone to read the terms of use of all of his products, you have to resort to trust in the company. You hope they have your best interests protected and that all other people that bought the same product did their research so you don't have to. After all, if the Google home has more than 10.000 5 star reviews I can trust it right? We live in a society where technology has gotten so complex and anti-transparent that the average consumer can only hope the product respects the trust the user has in it. And since most of us are average consumer, we will do what everybody else does, which is buying the most functional products, regardless of whether we know what we are getting ourselves into.

How much you need to trust your products also depends on how many different product producers you are dealing with. For example if you own 8 smart devices, all from a different company, you are dealing with 8 different companies and you can trust these companies in different amounts. When one company starts to let you down in functionality or policy, you can exchange the product for a competitor's product. This gets more complicated if you have more centralized systems like the Google Home app that links all your devices. Generally, the more centralized the higher the ease of use. So again, it is a trade off of independence vs. functionality.
Sustainability:
Sustainability is an incredibly difficult subject for a smart home with IoT objects. There are many arguments that IoT products improve sustainability, but also any arguments that say the exact opposite.

IoT improves sustainability:
A smart home with IoT devices collects data about the user's pattern and preferences. This data could then be used to know when the user gets home and leaves home and then automatically turn on and off the heating system. This is what Google Nest does. This saves energy.
It is also true that the data can give the user insight in his rituals and patterns, and when this information is visualized in a good way, the user can easily determine how sustainable he lives and in what areas the most sustainability can still be gained.
Also, IoT products can measure their own performance and measure if anything is out of the ordinary. When an IoT products knows enough about itself it can diagnose its own problems and maybe order spare parts before the original part breaks. Better maintenance provided by sensors and data allow products to last longer, which is good for sustainability.
Lastly, IoT products know a lot about their own usage and performance. This allows the product to automatically improve its energy usage. Since they are connected to the internet, your machine can also learn from a similar machine used on the other side of the world. All this data allows the product to function more efficiently.

IoT is bad for sustainability:
Of course, IoT products have more eletronical components and therefore cost more energy to produce. Electronical components are very hard to recycle which will create a lot of e-waste in the near future when all currently produced IoT devices start to be thrown away. Also, many materials needed for these components are rare materials like neodymium and cobalt which are found in very politically-unstable countries and areas.
Also, IoT products generally go hand-in-hand with capitalism. Your fridge isnt good enough if you cant play World of Tanks on it, so you need to buy the newest one. Because of rapidly improving technology, appliances will be more quickly swapped for the latest versions. Where back in the day a fridge was used for 30 years because all it did was cool things, nowadays it will be used for less time because you will need the latest technology to be able to live a 'modern life'.
Other: I would like to emphasize that ethics in a smart home go beyond the person who lives in the house, and that this should be recognized more. Currently, the vast majority of society is unaware of all of the IoT products on the markets and of their capabilities. When these people visit a smart home, they should be aware they are potentially filmed or recorded. If I visit a friend's house, I do not want to worry about his Google Home listening to everything I am saying. A smart home with IoT products should be centralized, so that there is 1 control panel or app that easily allows the user to switch to different smart-modes. For example, 1 is no smart systems, 2 is basic smart systems, 3 is full tracking and autonomy. Whenever a guest comes over, you could easily lower the amount of tracking happening (but also losing some functionality in the process).

Name: P7
Informed consent: For the smart cabin assignment we need to monitor the passengers comfort/discomfort. In every way possible data on the passenger needs to be collected. Introducing a smart seat would mean that somewhere in the process, when booking the flight or at check-in for example, the passenger would need to agree that this data is being collected. Would there be a possibility to deny this data collection and in essence switching off the smart seat? What would be the consequences of choosing this, would you get lesser service from the flight attendants?
Privacy: Depending on the data collection method(s) privacy will be more or less of a concern. When using cameras that detect facial expressions, or using microphones to record sounds to identify discomfort, this is obviously a large privacy concern. Using infrared or sensors in the seat to detect temperature, frequency of movements or pressure points, privacy seems less of a concern. When using cameras, including an Edge AI system could be beneficial, in this type of system data is processed locally and the images aren‚Äôt stored, only the data that needs to be collected. In this way cameras can be used to count for example. To minimize privacy concerns it is chosen to continue with IR cameras, also because these can be used for multiple purposes.
Information security: Hacked into the server, people can process and collect data regarding whether someone is sleeping, their body temperature and movement patterns. The data is linked to a particular seat and because of that this could be linked to the identity of the passenger when ticket data is also retrieved. An interesting part of this, is that the devices/sensors that are collecting data are part of the seat and therefore not user specific or secured with a user password. This means that the handling of the information and security of the system is the responsibility/task of the airline.
Physical safety: Physical safety in Internet of Things context regards the effects that the actions of actuators can cause. In the proposed system, the actuators will not automatically cause a change. The needed information regarding the reason for discomfort and a suggestion for a suitable solution are shared with the flight attendant. The flight attendant is the one that provides a proposal to the passenger (e.g. additional water, a pillow, a blanket, etc.). Not many physical safety implications occur in this system. Suggesting a certain type of medicine to the passenger for air sickness could be a physical safety concern, depending on the dose and possible allergies or side effects of this medicine. Apart from the possible physical safety hazards for the passengers, providing or suggesting medicine as an airline could cause the airline huge problems in case something should happen to the passenger.
Trust: As mentioned earlier, all data collection devices for the smart seat are provided and used by the airline and no use from personal devices is made. This fact might make it harder to make the passenger trust the system. Not only the passengers but also the flight attendants need to trust in the whole IoT system. The AI detecting discomfort at a certain passenger and deciding upon the type of discomfort needs to be accurate enough for the flight attendant to be able to assume the passenger is actually experiencing that type of discomfort.
Sustainability: Implementing a smart cabin and smart seats in aircraft would add even more electronic devices and sensors to the cabin of the aircraft which obviously is not great from a sustainability standpoint because of the large embodied energy of electronic components. However, the goal of the implementation of the smart cabin though, is to aid passengers and improve their comfort. Helping people hydrate, rest sufficiently and help in sickness, in some way contributes to SDG (Sustainable Development Goal) 3 - Good health and well-being.

Name: P8
Informed consent: It is important to understand that the medical data is very personal. When accepting a website cookie, you want to visit the website fast so you click OK. But for the wheelchair you have to read the consent form carefully because the data is so personal and medical. It should also be clear it will only be shared with you and your caregiver by choice. Not with third parties. To be sure the user understands the Informed consent I advice that the consent is discussed with a Permobil expert in person.
Privacy: The wheelchair is used both inside and outside. So also in private situations. To limit privacy risk I advice to not use cameras, microphones and the GPS. The advantages do not win over the privacy invasion. Again it is medical data, that can not be shared with third parties.
Information security:
For the hardware the risk is that other people will use the chair, that will mess up the medical data of the user. To limit that risk I advice to put a sensor in the wristband, that has to be within a small range of the wheel chair, for the wheelchair to log the data.
For the Network level, the wheelchair has to have it's own 5G network, to limit risks of fall out, missing data and the risk of being compromised by unwanted others.
Physical safety: The Physical safety risk is very present. When the IoT system fails to work, the medical data will not be processed right and potential medical danger might be missed. That can mean harm to the user. And other risk of the IoT failing, can be that the wheelchair unexpectedly stops working which can be dangerous when it happens for instance on the street. To limit the risk I advice to add extra maintenance sensors that are connected with Permobil and periodical maintenance check-ups are to be done.
Trust: Trust is the most important concern. The user has to trust the IoT system that data is processed accordingly. And trust the system with their personal medical data. To gain trust  I advice that there are periodical check-ups with the caregiver, to make sure the data is still received accordingly and the care giver should walk through the data with the user, to show the user the data is received correctly. The personal contact will help with gaining trust from the user. The risk is that when the system fails one time, the user will have a very hard time to trust is again, since the information is so important for the user's health. And what is more important than health?
Sustainability: The IoT system will contribute to sustainability, because it makes the wheelchair a lot more personal and adds health benefits to the user. Thus the ownership feeling will increase and the user will be more careful with their wheelchair. To make sure this feeling continues when ever the user wants to sell/give their wheelchair to someone else, it should have a reset function. So the next user can make it personal again.
Other: These were all the concerns I can think off.

Name: P9
Informed consent: Since there will be sensors recording the voices and behaviour of the hotel guests, it is important these guests are aware of this and have consented. This Is something that should be checked by the responsible person at check-in, not via a form but in conversation for clarity. If the guests don‚Äôt agree there must be a way to completely isolate the one room they are staying in from the system and turn the sensors off during their stay. It also should be stated clearly that this system is used at the description of the hotel room before renting it.
Privacy: This system monitors human behaviour behind closed hotel doors. If this behaviour is monitored it must only be used for the intended function of the system. This means that the sound recordings are only stored in a way that gives information on the user and the sound volumes, but cannot be listened to afterwards to determine what conversations were held in the hotel room. If an infrared camera is used to determine if there are actually people in the room and the sound is not just from the TV, the camera should be very low resolution to keep privacy in check.
Information security: There is always a risk of privacy leaks in the hotel sector because guests provide a lot of information. This includes their contact details, payment method, passport details, roommates and with this system the behaviour of the guests. The hotel must take responsibility for these data and store them locally. If a cloud collection is necessary for the functioning of the system, they must take precautions to prevent leaks.
Physical safety: The IoT must function properly to provide physical safety to the people inside the hotel. If there is a danger like a fire in a hotel room that is not picked up properly by heat and smoke sensors, the alarms will not go off and the people might not get to safety in time.
Trust: Trust is very important in the field of physical safety, the alarms need to function properly. But it is also important (but less) for the hotel management. They need to be able to trust the system to conserve energy when there are no guests in a room, they need to be able to trust that the system will tell them if anything is wrong inside a room and they need to be able to trust that the system will tell them when it is time to clean the room because guests have left. If the trust is not there the hotel employees will check all of these things themselves and this will take a lot of extra time and energy from everyone. Also it might lead to worse customer satisfaction; an employee could walk into a room thinking it is empty when it is not.
Sustainability: This system will improve the sustainability of the whole hotel. It senses when the hotel rooms are empty and will then turn off any lighting. It can also adjust the room heating according to guest behaviour and alert staff when windows, doors or fridges are left open.

Name: P10
Informed consent: The passenger agrees to the airliner‚Äôs terms & conditions upon purchasing a ticket. However, these are hardly read because terms & conditions are lengthy and repetitive documents. Therefore, the passenger‚Äôs infotainment system in the smart cabin could inform them once again before the flight starts.
Privacy: The smart cabin is constantly monitoring information of the passenger. But instead of linking the data to a passenger, it can be linked to a chair number (storing the data separately). And instead of recording recognizable images of the passengers, the motion sensors may only record specific areas in a lower resolution.
Information security: The data can be stored temporarily, as it is only relevant for the duration of the flight. Furthermore, the data can be stored locally on the aircraft, because it is only required there. The advantage of local storage is that hacker are not able to access it via certain networks, reducing the risk of data leaks.
Physical safety: Errors in the system can cause sustained discomfort to the passengers. The risks are generally not severe, but uncomfortable nonetheless. Regular check-ups by the stewards in addition to the smart system‚Äôs care is able to control inaccuracies and failures in the system.
Trust: Besides using non-human actuators in this system (fans, lights, seat adjustment), the smart cabin can include the airline stewards to comfort the passengers. If instructed properly, they can follow up incoming data and personally help passengers. Flight anxiety is not an uncommon phobia and personal attention can support passengers in this regard.
Sustainability: If the smart system detects a passenger is out of consumables, that does not mean he / she requires a refill directly. The same goes for actuators such as temperature control. Non-electronic solutions are viable options for heat regulation as an example. Think of a blanket if cold or a drink if hot, instead of air-conditioning or seat-warming. Therefore, the system may give the passenger suggestions instead of direct action, giving the user a choice in the end.

Name: P11
Informed consent: Recording someone's body position might not seem like a big issue personally, but I can imagine that people might not feel comfortable when their body position is monitored. Therefore informing them beforehand and asking if they allow it & want to get feedback is a way to inform them and ask for consent.
Privacy: If the seating number, with the name of the passenger is connected to their body posture there might be a privacy issue. However, if this information is only used on flight, e.g. to provide feedback to the user, and is deleted as soon as the plane lands, the privacy is better.
Information security: If body position information istays within the airplane and is deleted after the flight the information can be considered safe.
Physical safety: The feedback might make someone highly aware of their posture, creating tension. I'm assuming the sensors are liquid proof, but the whole system ofcourse has to be in order to be safe.
Trust: Users have to be able to trust that the information and feedback provided is true and will not be used in any other form than providing feedback on body posture to them personally.
Sustainability: If the product increases comfort during a flight, people might take an airplane sooner, which is very bad for the environment. It also makes the chairs more complicated, which might be an issue in afterlife of the chairs.
Other: Maybe people would not like it because they are scared they will be weighted? Also, airplane companies could misuse the information and the product to wave off complaints about airplane comfort.

Name: P12
Informed consent: When it comes to collecting data, especially personal and health-related data, it is important that the user is fully aware of 1) how the data is collected, 2) what the collected data is used for, 3) any potential effects or risks associated with the data collection process and 4) their right to stop this data collection process at any time. In the case of the Nike gym shirt the user should know where the devices are located and what data they collect, as well as how this data is used to predict whether they are diabetic or not and who has access to these results. Only when the user is aware of this process can they reasonably give their 'informed consent' to be monitored.
Privacy: For medical data it is paramount to ensure patient and user privacy and to prevent any irrelevant party from accessing patient information. In many cases, like with the Nike gym shirt, it is unnecessary for anyone other the patient to view their data and results. This can be done through a password-protected cloud storage, where patient information would be inaccessible even for those that run the server.
Information security: Just in the same way that data should not leak from the IoT infrastructure due to patient confidentiality, data should not be able to be inserted into the infrastructure either. Inserting false data could affect the accuracy of the prediction algorithm and lead patients to be alarmed over false positives or even worse, believe false negatives, thereby running the risk of leaving their diabetes undiagnosed.
Physical safety: As mentioned before, false positives and negatives can be detrimental to the health of the users. Besides false data, an inaccurate prediction algorithm can have the same result. The designers should thus consider whether their algorithm is accurate enough to help users diagnose diabetes, or whether it unnecessarily puts the health of these users on the line. Similarly, any electronics should be fit for everyday use and not be detrimental to user health. If there are any risks associated, the user should be fully aware of this.
Trust: Even when all the previous areas of ethics are addressed by the designers of the Nike gym shirt, the patient could still feel wary and vulnerable due to the high amount and sensitive nature of the data that is collected. One of the most effective strategies to make users trust data processing methods is to design and explain a way in which even the designers, data analysts and any other person involved in the IoT infrastructure is unable to access the user data, except for the user itself. User data could be encrypted in such a way that only the user can decrypt this information. In this case, even if the data analysts can access the raw patient data, it would be a meaningless, unprocessed set of data from which no useful information can be derived.
Sustainability: A lot of data that is collected ends up never being used. Making sure that only relevant data is collected, processed and communicated not only makes sure the overal infrastructure more time-efficient but also more energy-efficient, requiring less computational power to run and therefore limiting unnecessary energy use. Although this mainly concerns large data processing facilities, the same principle applies to the case of the Nike gym shirt.
Other: N/a

Name: P13
Informed consent: For my designed application some medical data of the user is needed. This will be the vaccine status of the user, the recovery status of the user and the possible test results. This data is subtracted from municipalities and so servers. This means that for this information the user has already given informed consent to not only undergo these medical treatments/ testing but was also in agreement with the data being stored. Since the app does not cause the user to undergo any medical or physical actions, no informed consent is needed to use the application. The app should however let the user give consent for processing their data and using it to construct a QR code certificate.
Privacy: For this application the user must consent in the app for subtracting personal data to create a travel certificate and track location data of the user. This means the application knows where the user is located and what his or her tests status, vaccine status and recovery status is. The application also is able to show infection risks situated nearby. At any cost the application should prevent giving personal data such as names when it shows infection risks nearby. It should simply alarm that "a positive tested person"is nearby. Next to this the location data and medical data should not be stored for a longer period of time, and if this does prove to be useful for research purposes, the user should give consent for this. The certificate uses an QR code. The advantage of this code is that no personal data needs to be on the screen. It can just be the QR code. This means the workers at the airport do not see any medical data of the user and this concern is taken away.
Information security:
The ethics concern regarding information security is very real. It is medical personal data that is dealt with. In recent months there have been multiple data leaks from the GGD (
The Municipal Health Service). Since the app I designed gets its data from these organizations  it is important that their data system is secure and that the app is not easy to hack to guarantee the users their data is in safe hands. The user should be aware of how, when and for how long their data is stored. A risk analysis should be made and the needed measurements taken before the app is launched.
Physical safety: The application shows infection risks located near you. This means that when the application is not working correctly, but the user is not aware of this, the user might locate himself in places with high infection risks which can endanger physical safety. The app should always show that the infections nearby are just an indication and cannot be fully trusted in accessing in whether or not going somewhere is infection-risk-free.
Trust: The ethics concerns regarding trust are mostly that the application might not always work as intended while the user might blindly trust its data. This should be prevented by telling the user the application only serves as an indication of a country's infection risk and travel measurements.
Sustainability: The application allows for users to travel again. This might create an increase in flights and CO2-footprint. The emission of greenhouse gasses has been significantly lower during the pandemic. The question is if traveling should be stimulated with an application like this in the first place. When looking at the sustainable development goals we find that good -health and well being is also a priority. Travelling with an app like this is still experimental and the effects on health and well being of the travel industry starting up again are not yet known.  Another goal is to reduce inequalities. People in prosperous countries are more likely to get a vaccine / test  first and are therefore allowed to travel. With an application like this it might actually increase inequality.
Other: A great ethical concern would be if the application should be obligated when travelling or not. It could actually be against some human rights to say a person cannot travel if he or she cannot show that he or she is vaccinated or tested. It could easily segregate a society. This has already caused a lot of fuss in politics right now.

Name: P14
Informed consent: When i want to access a website, there is often a pop-up demanding that i agree to certain terms and conditions or cookie settings before i can have access to a website. More often than not I do not take the time to read through the pages long document that is linked in the pop-up, I just click ‚ÄúI agree‚Äù. Can this be considered ‚ÄúInformed consent‚Äù? ‚ÄúTo consent‚Äù is something different from ‚Äúto assent‚Äù. The first having more weight to it than the second. There is pressure on the user to click the ‚Äúi agree‚Äù button because you can‚Äôt access the website without doing so. However, there is a significant informational asymmetry. The legal documents attached to the pop-up are often filled with densely packed legal jargon and technical details that are not easily understandable to most of the end users of the product. In short: you don't know what you are agreeing to. The same issues are present with the use of IoT devices. You agree to the terms and conditions in the app, there is a text on the box stating: ‚Äúupon opening this box you are agreeing to‚Ä¶.‚Äù or even worse, you are never informed that your data is being stored and potentially used for different uses than you had in mind personally.
Privacy: To some extent, the harvesting of our data will help us in everyday life. My phone will automatically calculate the time it will take me to reach an appointment I have, calculate traffic delays and (without me specifically asking it to) prompt me when it is time to leave. The darker side of the coin is that apparently, my phone (and therefore my phone‚Äôs manufacturer and probably a plethora of other organisations) know exactly what I am doing the whole day. I can‚Äôt remember the source but I remember the following anekdote: ‚ÄúI once had a discussion with a friend who claimed this was no problem since he had nothing to hide. I asked him to install a webcam in his shower broadcasting live to facebook, and give me his credit card credentials while he was at it‚Ä¶‚Äù. When integrating IoT devices into our houses we are voluntarily bringing more and more sensors into our house that collect private data and send it off to be processed. We just have to trust that this data is handled safely and with integrity.
Information security: There are many current examples of harm being done by malicious hackers who are after your passwords, data and money. Some examples are the ransom software that forced the University of Maastricht to pay nearly ‚Ç¨200.000 in Bitcoin to regain access to their online environment, or several webshops that had all their user logins and passwords stolen and sold online. This can be extremely embarrassing for the organisation that is targeted directly but can be even more detrimental to people who are using the organisation's services. A lot of people use the same credentials for multiple services like subscriptions, bank accounts, government logins etc. Imagine what harm any low level criminal could do if they get their hands on those logins and passwords. Even if you use a password with multiple capitals, numbers and symbols, it is only as strong as the location it is uploaded to.
Physical safety:
The IoT brings connectivity to our everyday, physical world. It provides the internet with a body to exist in places other than on our computers. We are entrusting more and more technologies to be controlled over the internet without a person actively monitoring with an emergency stop button nearby. Some examples are smart pet doors, security systems, smart door locks and apps that can open your car. There is also the possibility that people can use your IoT appliances data to see when you are home, or when you are not home. 
If you are a high-profile member of society you may find it unappealing if your location is traceable by anone 24/7. This could be the case if you are using a fitness gps tracker or google timeline for example.
Trust: ‚ÄúAll the world is made of faith, and trust, and pixie dust.‚Äù -Peter Pan-. It is hard not to be sceptical about ‚Äútrusting‚Äù anything that happens online. We live in the Fake News era, have to use more and more complicated security tools and have more shallow interactions per day than ever before. However, our entire lives are built around trust. We trust that if we break a leg, a doctor will patch you up. We trust that if i work for an employer, that he will give me money at the end of the week. We trust that we can receive goods and services in exchange for an imaginary value of some numbers on something called a bank account. Somehow we need to figure out exactly how much trust to put in a system so that we can use it to its full potential, but still keep a skeptical eye out for the downsides. Trust, but verify. Right now people seem to trust IoT applications enough to let them drive their cars for them, and listen to their private conversations in their houses all day long. When trust in a certain concept is lost, it is very difficult to regain this. It is the duty of the designer to treat the trust received by the user with the utmost respect, or risk losing it forever.
Sustainability: Plenty of IoT gadgets claim to be a more sustainable version of a product of which we did not even know we needed an update for. In some cases they are right, in some they are not. If you can place a sensor on your outdoor water faucet that sends you a notification when it thinks you unintentionally left the tap open, this could potentially save liters of water from being flushed away. However, it takes energy and materials to produce, ship, sell and run these gadgets. You would probably need to leave the tap open a lot to offset the emissions and footprint of the product before it can justify its existence. There is also the issue of replacing products that are not broken with a version that is ‚Äúconnected‚Äù and therefore most likely using more power to run. If not only by the device itself, consider the massive data centers required to process all the collected data.

Name: P19
Informed consent: ‚ÄòEUSafe‚Äô has a few ethical concerns with regards to informed consent. The main concern lies in the fact that a lot of the user‚Äôs personal information is needed to make this arrangement possible. Additionally, there are multiple actors in this architecture through which information exchange is taking place. These include the governments, hospitals, vaccination centres, test manufacturers etc. The user must know exactly how a particular actor is processing their personal data. Since all this data comes together into one unified platform ‚ÄòEUSafe‚Äô, there is a lack of clarity on how a particular actor is processing the user data. Lastly, all these actors have significantly differing needs for the same data from the users. Governments might use it for citizen surveillance while test and vaccine manufacturers might use it for targeted sales. These are quite different in their impact and scale and thus, pose a problem for the user who might have to agree to share the data just to be able to avail the vaccine certificate and travel freely. Offering a bare bones vaccine certificate with the least amount of identifiable personal data processing and adding extra options where the users can opt-in to make their data available would be the best solution in this case. The users should be able to see how a particular actor will process their data and choose to selectively restrict their data to certain actors. With respect to the exposure notifications, the user must consent to the use of specialised hardware on their smartphones to be able to exchange sensitive data (location). However, the EUSafe app is not a stand-alone application. It is installed on the smartphone with various other apps which it can communicate with through shared API‚Äôs.  These other apps can use the sensitive information for their own malicious needs. There should be clear communication to the user that the EUSafe app runs in its own sandbox and thus, cannot communicate with outside apps. Lastly, with respect to trend prediction, the use of non-identifiable numbers received from the health agencies do not have clearly defined informed consent issues since the algorithm just runs on the numbers and cannot identify individuals. However, the method of processing of the data before it reaches the appointed health authority needs to be clearly communicated to the user at the point of contact (test location/vaccine centre etc).
Privacy: The ethical concerns regarding privacy for ‚ÄòEUSafe‚Äô have the same roots as that of informed consent. Due to the sheer amount of user data that is needed for such an endeavour (e.g.: name, ID identifier, test/vaccine date, dosage status, results, and much more), there are bound to be concerns about the privacy of this user data. On the one hand, it is impossible to create this system without this data and difficult to make it reliable and secure if it is not linked to a particular user because the system needs to be interoperable at all levels. However, the nature of the data makes it quite sensitive. For e.g., the fact that a certain individual is not vaccinated might lead to discrimination in service in accordance with the global sentiment without realising why that individual is not vaccinated. Moreover, this might happen covertly at the verifier level and the user is left unaware of such discrimination. In essence, the private choice of the individual has been invaded upon. With the proposed architecture of national database and public EU keys, the personally identifiable data is protected at a system level. However, since this data is fed by humans into the system, there is always a possibility of data abuse and manipulation which the user is left unaware about. Thus, enforcing strict privacy regulations like the GDPR and appointing an independent 3rd party watchdog to conduct privacy audits is the solution to ensure user privacy. With respect to the exposure notification as mentioned before, the EUSafe app is not a stand-alone application. It is installed on the smartphone with various other apps which it can communicate with through shared API‚Äôs. These other apps can use the sensitive information for their own malicious needs. Facebook is notorious for conducting such practices. Recently, Apple released an update that prevented cross-tracking of applications much to Facebook‚Äôs anger due to lost ad revenue. Such an arrangement must be made commonplace before the privacy of the data can be ensured as 3rd party actors can always use the data due to the platform sharing. Additionally, regarding the exposure notification, the most sensitive data is that of user location. Logging this location and keeping a track of the user is an absolute violation of user privacy and thus, this is something that must be made absolutely clear that this data is not being collected or deleted after a specific time period. Using the DP-3T protocol is mandatory for this.
Information security: The ethical concern regarding Information Security of course comes from the fact that there is a lot of sensitive information that needs to be protected. In this case, the sensitive information ranges from the user‚Äôs personally identifiable information such as address, name, date of birth and other unique identifiers but also from the medical angle of health status such as vaccinated, recovering and or negative tests.  As mentioned in the privacy section, if this information falls into the wrong hands, there are a lot of avenues for user profiling and discrimination. As mentioned in the privacy section, the app can also communicate with other apps in a non-sandboxed environment thus leaking sensitive information. The use of AES256 bit encryption and MD5 hashing digital verification does alleviate some of the security concerns but the read-write permissions at the hands of the national authority always creates challenges due to the decentralised nature of the data across the EU. Individual member states may make deals with organisations for profit at the expense of the users. This is especially influenced by geopolitics in an increasingly polarised world. However, not having this interoperable nature could lead to operational inefficiency in the form of detailed fraud checks etc and this would lead to increased compliance cost. Thus, there needs to be a balance in the protection of information and the freedom with which it can be shared. Additionally, the location of physically storing this information is always a point of concern and should be secret information. Specialised data centres and high security are a pre-requisite for such massive operations. Ensuring this is always a difficult task and data breaches are quite commonplace. Thus, the user should have the option of deleting their data from the database if they choose to and periodic refreshes and checks must be done to check the security of the databases.
Physical safety: At first glance, it might seem that there is no specific concern regarding physical safety since this is just a verification and information system for the status of an individual. However, on a closer look, the concern for physical safety is massive because of the type of data that is being verified: health data. If by any means, hackers and/or miscreants can clone the system and break through the databases and encryption layers of the system, there is an avenue for data manipulation. With this data, it is possible to present a false status and wreak biological havoc. For e.g., it is possible for an infected person to manipulate that they have a negative test result. On their travels, they can be a super spreader and thus, begin a wave of infections that would have been prevented otherwise. This could lead to strain on hospitals and economic strain on countries. Especially, in today‚Äôs geopolitical environment and rising nationalism, this situation does not seem too dramatic or crazy. Ensuring robust information security is the only way to prevent such a threat to physical safety. Additionally, since location data is being tracked, the logs can never be breached since this gives miscreants the location information for targets whom they can physically target and exploit.
Trust: As mentioned in the reference paper, trust is a function of all these specific sectoral concerns. The main ethical concern regarding trust comes from the fact that it needs to be made clear that this is a positive good move to ensure the better health of all EU residents. If there is trust among the people that this is for their betterment and the supporting infrastructure possesses the same qualities such as reliability and security, the trust in the system is built. However, the concern that this is a ploy by governments to control and create classes of people since people generally have an unfavourable view towards governments. This could also lead to the creation of two classes of people and infringe on the fundamental right of movement within the EU. These could completely erode the trust people have ion such systems as well as trust in the EU as a supra-national organisation. On a smaller scale, the users must be able to trust the data the EUSafe app showcases. These are linked to the exposure notification and trend analysis. If the data mirrors these predictions, then the trust in the platform will increase and more people will be willing to use it increasing accuracy. On the other hand, poor results will ensure users lost trust and dissuade users from the entire program and consequently, the governments and EU. This can ultimately boil over to dissent.
Sustainability: Sustainability must always be seen from three lenses: people, planet, and profit. With this system, the concerns lie in the fact that there are profit motives for the companies involved to keep this system going especially the vaccine and test manufacturers. The vaccine production process is an extremely technical and difficult process and thus, needs a lot of care to ensure safety in the product. With ingredients such as Active Pharmaceutical Ingredients concentrated in some countries, there is a possibility that this system encourages vaccine hoarding in developed countries and thus, a lack of supply in other countries since total production is limited. This leads to inequitable distribution further lengthening the time-period of the pandemic and in turn, filling the coffers of these companies. Moreover, it also grants the governments a probable reason to keep this going and slowly but steadily infringing on fundamental rights of citizens even when the situation does not demand such a restriction. And finally, by promoting heavy testing and vaccination, the strain on the supply chain to provide and keep up will eventually lead to some accidents, human rights issues, and environmental destruction in some parts of the supply chain just like every other industry. These are serious concerns to sustainability and thus, must be kept in mind when propagating this system as the saviour of the times. The goodness of the system lies in the temporality of it.

Name: P20
Informed consent:
The Permobil Wheelchair IoT system would be regulated by means of a EULA, ‚Äúend user license agreement‚Äù, in which the terms and conditions of the data prediction service would be stated.

The main concerns came for both user and vendor sides. On the one hand, the user should be aware of the risk that accepting information implies.  On the other hand, the vendor should provide a clear and precise statements of the implications of using the product, rather than an extensive or ambiguous description (Allhoff et al, 2018).
The probability the client to permit the access to unwanted private data depends on the vendor‚Äôs contractual document characteristics. Below, a possible example of a short EULA is proposed:

‚Ä¢	Permobil declared itself as the server administrator. As the administrator it must guarantee the proper operational functioning of the and advocate for the client‚Äôs privacy.
‚Ä¢	Enterprises interested in accessing the data are referred as other parties.
‚Ä¢	The client has the right to access this service or use the product with exclusively the local benefits. (heart rate monitoring and statistics)

The following conditions are applied to clients using the prediction service:
‚Ä¢	Other parties (Public administration and private health care entities) can request the access to a client‚Äôs data, which needs to be accepted for both the administrator (Permobil) and the client.
‚Ä¢	In case any of the afore mentioned deny access to the data, the other party will not have access to the specific client‚Äôs data. 
‚Ä¢	The right to modify client‚Äôs information is prohibited to all the participant entities to guarantee the veracity of the data. 

This EULA makes clear in a few lines the acquisition, retention, and sharing of personal data.
Privacy:
The first ethical concern related to privacy is how is it guaranteed the privacy of a client‚Äôs data. 

The second ethical concern related to privacy is who should be the owner of the data. As I stated in the previous answer, Permobil could be the administrator, who could give authorisation (if the client also agrees on that decision) to the health care public and private entities to access client‚Äôs data. 

Permobil should inform about the objectives of the information sharing and not use the information for economical purposes.

The afore mentioned(in informed consent) both side verification (client + administrator) is designed with the intention to have an accurate control of who can access each client‚Äôs data and give the client the right to decide.
Information security:
Looking at the security of the information, it's useful to remind how the architecture I proposed worked. There information flows from the wheelchair, to the smartphone and from the smartphone to the cloud server. The first transfer is considered more secure because of  the distance limit of the BLE technology and the single connection of 2 devices. 
(https://vpnoverview.com/privacy/devices/bluetooth)

The second connection brings to play risk during the upload and during the access to the online data. Starting with the access protocols, the most common way to tackle this issue is the client-password log in. The online server is designed in such a way that each client-password request access to the online server to access certain amount of data which contains the client private information.

With regard to the client-password security layer, it brings to play some responsibilities for the user. For example, the complexity and frequency of changing the password is advisable to keep your data secure.

Besides that, to guarantee the information security during the transference of data to the cloud server encryption-decryption techniques are proposed to avoid possible interceptions of the data during the transference.
Physical safety:
As stated in the Allhoff et al work, there are several concerns related to actuators malfunctioning or sudden failure that can suppose a notorious hazard (2018):

‚Ä¢	Seat positioning actuators failure. Can affect both the comfort during the transport and the cognitive ergonomics. The user could feel mounted in a device that cannot be controlled and he could feel observed by bystanders, leading to negative emotions.

‚Ä¢	Brake‚Äôs failure can derive in an impact or accident with other vehicles on the road. In case the user would fall from the wheelchair he might not be able to sit again, supposing a big deal in his autonomy. 

‚Ä¢	General failure of the system. The user will not be able to autonomously go back home.

Although to generalise is politically incorrect, for a great majority of wheelchair users the autonomy or independence is highly valuated, and they challenge themselves to be autonomous.
Trust:
Trust per se is a complex term to identify. They can be identified several trust relationships:
‚Ä¢	User's Trust in the company. The openness when communicating with the user is key, but also the attitude. The company's attitude has an effect on the trust in the data security. Therefore, professional communication is key.

‚Ä¢	User's Trust in the data. Possible mistakes in the local storage due to storing errors, can lead the user to think that the data is randomly displayed, deriving in a trust loos. 

‚Ä¢	User's Trust in the product. The reliability highly affects the perception of the quality of the product. The quality likewise affect the trust that the user deposit on the product.

‚Ä¢	Caregiver's Trust in the predictions. The accuracy of the algorithm is key to provide the caregivers the correct predictions, that trust is built with the sand of the time.
Sustainability:
For assessing the sustainability of my proposed solution, the sustainable development goals are a good base to start with. (United Nations, 2015) The following two goals are the core essence of the Permobil new chair: 
3.Good health and wellbeing. 
10. Reduce inequality within and among countries

The new modifications Incorporated try to help the user to keep in a healthy lifestyle and decrease the inequalities that such a vulnerable collective as reduced mobility people suffer.
 
However, other goals could be affected such as the number 12: responsible production and consumption. I conducted a 3-year research with reduced mobility users during my bachelor and one of the main challenges I noticed in the wheelchair design was how to guarantee the functioning of the controller. It was detected that component sometimes needs to be replaced excessively if the user exposes the wheelchair to the rain. The durability and reliability should be a key requirement for a product that aims at offering autonomy.

Finally, I think that other aspects related to recyclability of materials, production offshoring model, critical material extraction, batteries pollutants should be analyses critically to  assess the sustainability of such a necessary product.

https://sdgs.un.org/goals

Name: P21
Informed consent:
My design proposal for the COVID-app uses connectivity with the internet to transfer data from the GGD servers (Dutch Health Organizations) and Rijksoverheid servers (Dutch Government). Users should be made aware that this data transfer takes place and that it is connected with their DigiD (National Digital Identification) of the government. This data transfer will only be used for vaccination confirmation (to avoid fake vaccination certificates), sharing statistics about COVID-19 in the Netherlands and to generate the unique certificate QR code. Without this connection the user won't be able to load statistics, certificate and thus also QR code. The user needs to be informed about the necessity of the internet connection with the servers, to provide them their statistics and confirm their personal vaccination certificate.

Another aspect that users need to be informed on is the use of Bluetooth data exchange with other COVID-app users. When installing the app the first time, users have to be informed and asked to enable Bluetooth access by the app. Only this way the app will be able to warn them from any infection dangers they have been exposed to in a certain area. It won't track their identity or location, only exchange a randomly generated code with other app users when in range. This way the app can send the user a notification when an app user in range was tested positive on COVID.
Privacy:
Privacy is an important topic, especially regarding governmental data sharing. Therefore the app is specifically designed with Bluetooth sharing rather than GPS location tracking. Bluetooth code sharing would be anonymous and no specific location data will be gathered. It will only remember a random generated code of app users that come in a certain range of each other. Once an app user is test positive with COVID the GGD will send everyone with shared Bluetooth codes a notification about infection risk. 

The QR code for showing your vaccination certificate works similar, as it doesn't exchange any details about the individual besides if he or she is vaccinated. The employee that scans the QR code will therefore only see a "check" or "cross" regarding the confirmation.
Information security: Most of the medical and identity data is highly personal and should definitely be kept secure at all times. Therefore the app won't use any user created account, but link it to their DigiD (National Digital Identity of the Dutch Government). Once the app is linked with the user can request their vaccination certificate via the app by scanning their fingerprint on their phone. If fingerprint scanning is not an option the user has to log in with their DigiD to receive it. This way the government has control over who specifically owns a vaccination certificate, while the user's info is more secure by linking it to their DigiD, like the system most Dutch banks are using with mobile payment.
Physical safety: Physical safety isn't a major factor regarding this design proposal. The only aspect that could be connected to this is the infection risk. The app would give users the indication when someone has tested positive in their area (in a recent period of time). However, this doesn't mean that users with this app won't run the risk on an infection. Probably a lot of people won't test themselves or carry the virus without even knowing it. They won't be registered as a positive COVID-case, thus not alerting app users. This should be communicated clearly in the app, to avoid users in expecting a completely risk free experience.
Trust:
Trust can be directly connected to the physical safety topic. Users should trust the app in reducing their risk of infections by providing more information, compared to when they don't use it. However, it doesn't mean that they can completely trust the system to avoid them from being infected at all. They still are at risk and they should know that. 

The DigiD is the Dutch Digital Identity Passport and has multiple verification steps via an encrypted connection. This would hopefully provide users with more trust as it is directly connected with your civil registration. 

The use of Bluetooth and QR codes would hopefully provide users trust in the app that it won't contain any background data transfer. The app can therefore be used without GPS tracking, camera and microphone for example.
Sustainability:
The design proposal makes use of existing technology integrations, therefore reducing the sustainable impact on the environment and daily routine. Using an app on a smartphone is something people are familiar with and doesn't require a new electrical device to give to the entire Dutch population. Every modern smartphone has Bluetooth and internet connection, therefore it serves as a suitable platform for the software.

Additionally, a Digital Certificate will be much more sustainable compared to Physical Certificated. Printed certificates for example, could provide unintentional paper pollution in the cities or require physical resources to manufacture. Smartphone and servers do use electrical power, but won't compare regarding sustainable impact to manufacturing a physical certificate for the entire Dutch population. Therefore the impact of a digital certificate is much lower.
Other: This design area is especially ethically sensitive as it both deals with governmental, medical and societal data. Being marked as "infected" has a really bad taste to it and having the virus is a very personal thing for people. Therefore it is highly probable that finding an "ethically perfect" solution is impossible. The Dutch government has to test various ways of integrating vaccination certificates into our society and opt for a solution that creates the biggest impact in reducing the COVID-impact.

Informed consent:
This IoT system does not collect any personal data. Its only ethical concern based on informed consent is that the system knows when someone is actually in their room or not. Which could indicate to people who want to steal their belongings when they are safe to enter. Without such an IoT system, only the staff that is present would know who are in, because they might have seen them enter/leave the building. 
In principle, the data that is collected will only be connected to the room number and should stay disconnected from the people who hired the room to improve privacy concerns. Based on these I think it is not necessary to ask for informed consent of the hotel guests.
Privacy: The smoke detector is looking at you 24/7. However, it only can see you through a low-resolution infrared camera. This makes sure it can detect temperature and human presence, but not what you are doing, or what you look like. For the sound detection also there has not been used a microphone but a sensor that can only detect sound level. Both to increase privacy concerns. Like I said before the only privacy concern is that if the data is accessed by someone it is not meant for they can see whether or not you are present in your room. Any patterns that emerge from this data are hard to analyse because they are not coupled to a specific person. Furthermore, patterns are hard to determine because people only stay for a short period of time. The only patterns that can be found will be based on specific rooms, which could lead to gaining more knowledge about the user behaviour of that specific room. For example, learning that people spend more time in a room that has a window in the southern direction.
Information security: To ensure information security the smoke detectors communicate over a separate secured wifi network coupled to the local server. After entering the local server the data will be encrypted when sending it anywhere else. Still, this does not forgo the warranted concern that the data may be sent encrypted towards the manufacturer of the smoke detectors. It also doesn‚Äôt overcome any unknown use cases of the data by the hotel or the manufacturer of the smoke detectors. Ideally, after production, the devices don‚Äôt send any data back to the manufacturer.
Physical safety: This IoT system has an influence on the guests by being able to sound an alarm and to activate LED‚Äôs on the device to indicate the type of danger. If these features can be accessed or activated when not intended they can influence multiple things. They can create chaos and stress because people get scared and need to exit the building, leaving all their valuables. Furthermore, if the system is accessed remotely the fire department may be called by the system, which can indirectly harm others because they come to a hotel that‚Äôs not in danger while they might be needed somewhere else.
Trust: The trust for this service has to lie mostly with the hotel chain since they will interact with the system. The guests don‚Äôt interact or ‚Äúuse‚Äù this system. They only need to trust that it works reliably as a warning system for any hazards in their room/hotel. Keeping the trust and confidence of the users is key since for IoT systems it is very hard for the users to know what exactly is going on in the background. You often don‚Äôt know where your data goes, how it is processed and only a small part of what they do with the processed data.
Sustainability: The ethical concerns regarding sustainability have mostly to do with the increased power use this system introduces in comparison with normal smoke detectors. Also, the increase in used sensors and actuators increases the impact on the planet.

Name: P29
Informed consent:
Especially for smart homes, informed consent is very important. Your home is often your private and safe space. As discussed in the paper: The Internet of Things: Foundational ethical issues, where a manufacturer gathered information from a sex toy, often people do not want to share such private information. However, the terms and conditions are mostly not clear for users since they are long and written in difficult language.

Another example of what happened with Google Homes is that they are eavesdropping information from people‚Äôs smart home devices. When this came out in public, many people were shocked. This is something that did not come clear from the terms and conditions.

I think it is important that people know they are being 'observed' and in what way. Companies should be clear where they store your data, for how long and what they do with it. Then people can decide for themselves if they would or would not like to have such a product in their home.

Some systems like Android and IOS already ask permission for an app to use certain sensors on your phone, like your camera, microphone, or GPS. In IOS' newest update they also announced to make people choose whether they want companies to collect data or not. I think this is a step in the right direction. Make people choose what they want to share and still let them use the product (maybe with less features)
Privacy:
A smart home has many sensors that can detect your every movement, conversation, time you are at home and much more. This information could be very valuable for companies and advertisers.

Cameras and microphones can really create privacy concerns. Especially when this information is stored on a server or even processed and used. People close their curtains when they want some privacy. But there is no curtain in front of your security camera. Companies should make sure that it does not end up in the wrong hands.

But there are not only privacy concerns for the people living in the house. People visiting the house get recorded eventhough they might not know it. And when a thief is entering the house, the home owner often puts images of that person online, taking the law into their own hands which could officially be seen as slander and defamation in the Netherlands.
Information security:
The news is often full of articles of companies getting hacked. As descibed in the previous topics, this could be dangerous since their is a lot of valuable information stored. 

Apple's Google's and Amazon's data is encrypted [1] meaning that there is a smaller chance that information gets stolen from their smart home servers. However, in the past, stories about Huawei and Facebookinformation leakage [2] showed that even big companies can be unsecure with their data.  

This hacking or information leakage does not only happen at the servers of companies. But also at people's smart homes. An electronic lock on your door should not be opened with a simple third party smartphone app. Or information should not be stolen from a local server. This means that the devices itself and the local communication should be secure too.

Since most people do not understand a lot about the internet of things they have to trust the engineers and designers behind it. And while there are many on information security, they are difficult to check.
Physical safety:
More and more devices in our homes are being automated and connected to the IoT. The IoT does not only concern sensor but actuators too. These actuators should be designed with physical safety in mind. 

A human often quickly sees when there is something wrong. When your lightbulb catches fire after turning it on, you may be quick enough to put out the fire. When your smart home automatically turns on the light you may not. Not reacting (quick enough) on an accident that happened on remote could be a major problem.

Another physical safety concern could be a smart lock that does not function as how it should or easily getting surpassed. Now your door is unlocked and anyone can come in.

In Europe every product should meet the European requirements for products. However, an increasing amount of products come from other regions in the world like China that has different restrictions. Products can contain materials or features that would be forbidden here.
Trust:
The smart home systems are now not only used by technology geeks, but it is comming to the masses. This means that there are more and more people using devices that they do not understand (as in underlaying principle). People have to trust the designers and engineers that make the product. This could mean that designers could get away with more unethical designs since people do not understand it anyway.

People that have smart home functions sometimes give a lot of trust to these companies. They trust that they do good with your data and for instance lock your door properly when you have a smart lock. All this data is stored, we do not know what these companies will do with this information in the future.

As explained before, companies like Facebook and Huawei have put a dent in peoples trust in them. In my opinion this is a good thing, since people are now a bit more alert and companies will react on this.

What else is a concern is that it is sometimes hard to choose who to blame. You have trusted a company with your information. But you also agreed to the terms and conditions. If a company does something with your information that you do not like, but they are allowed to, is it than your fault. Trust is probably more than they are allowed to do it, it is about is it ethical to do so.
Sustainability:
E-waste is one of the biggest problems in the world. More and more, before dumb devices now get smart. This means that they all need a PCB, battery, Wi-Fi connection etc. This e-waste is most of the time still not recycled and companies often do not take responsibility for it. Making a dumb device smart could also mean that it has more components that could break down. Also while most smart devices use low energy connections they are still on every minute of the day.

On the other hand, smart devices can be turned on at the right moment automatically. For instance your thermometer, which could make heating your house more efficient. Devices can remotely be turned of like lamps which could also save energy if you forgot to turn them off.
