Name: P31
Informed consent: Upfront the hotel will have to be clear what an autonomously safe night hotel they have. The functions of the alarm system will have to be presented to the costumer when they are booking a room in order for them to see what they are getting. This could at the same time be a major selling point, but the customers will have to agree to the terms imposed by this system. The customers will have to give some of their data willingly for this system to work.
Privacy: The main issue is the cross-referencing of the voices of said individuals. Personal information is taken from the client in this case and could impose serious privacy issues. It is therefore essential to store this data safely in an encoded way or better yet, to not record this data but only cross-reference it with an existing, anonymous database. This will however make it harder to train the model and predict more accurately.
Information security: The phone data of the staff members are stored in a database in this system. This is part of the local network and will have to be encoded to secure it. It would be best to store this data without names attached to it. As for the customers, minimal data would be ideally used to validate their check in's and outs. A bar code scanner is used at the moment and these codes could be given to orders placed online but will therefore be linked to an email address as well as a booked room. This system would ideally step away from the barcode and solely use voice recognition, although that will impose more ethical concerns on its own.
Physical safety: Precautions are taken in this system to warn customers in most cases of emergency. A speaker has been build into rooms to broadcast alarms should it be necessary to clear out rooms in case of danger. Physically it could be deemed as inconvenient or dangerous should these systems trigger and people will have to evacuate during a cold night whilst it was unnecessary. These alarms will have to be tweaked in order to not trigger too easily.
Trust: It will have to be communicated clearly that the microphones are not always listening and not storing the data that they are detecting. This is a major trust concern as people will be paranoid when they find a microphone in their room. The company will therefor have to be completly transparent about its use and show how they hadle this data. Store as little of it as possible without losing its function and showing that to the costumer
Sustainability: A lot of sensors are used in almost all of the rooms. This is not sustainable by any means. Some research will have to be done on how necessary some of these sensors are. A study might for example prove that a CO detector will not be nessesary in every room as well as the magnets on the windows or motion detectors. Analysis will have to be made to weigh the sustainable concerns vs their actual effectiveness to reduce danger.

Name: P32
Informed consent: As stated in the paper we read, informed consent require the user to be truly aware of the implications of the technology as used. Most EULAs are never read. In this application (of voice recording and recognition), perhaps we can audibly play a summary of the EULA, and in capturing the user's verbal assent, use that recording as the seed data for the voice recognition software. It would be easy to "cheap out" and do a standard EULA like most products currently on market.
Privacy: Privacy is a huge concern, and luckily for us it can be perhaps less critical than other services as each hotel stay is temporary. We would be unable to collect enough data to track habits over time, and should assign each hotel guest a random identifier for each new stay, such that different hotel stays can not be cross-referenced to determine behaviors. If the necessary data for guest monitoring can be done semi-anonymously, we have no risk of spilling personal data. We should also not store data on each guest much past their stay, Perhaps a few weeks incase any crimes were committed or damages were made?
Information security: I really don't think I'm qualified to speak on information security other than the parts that apply directly to this case (and are somewhat obvious). In the best case scenarios, we transmit as little data as possible over the internet/to cloud services. Hardwired, local-area networks are generally more secure. Of course, "encryption" would be valuable too, but those standards vary and are not useful if improperly applied. We can also seek not to transmit or output raw data, but only post-processed data "conclusions," if you will, to protect privacy and sensitive data.
Physical safety: Luckily, physical safety is not as critical for our hotel monitoring system as for some other products, such as driverless cars. I have chosen not to implement remote-lockable doors, etc. because I think not only is that risky (from a physical safety standpoint), but it also does not fit with the mission or value proposition of "Hotel premiere classe" lodging. I had considered using voice-activated keys for doors, but our voices can change situationally (drunk, smoking, hoarse from partying, etc.), so this felt unsafe.
Trust: For me, trust in this context will be a lot about personal freedoms vs. invasiveness. If the system is designed such that it guarantees the "safety" of guests and detects "possible dangers" in the room, where is the line drawn? Will it alert a monitor if the system detects cannabis smoke or other drugs? Will it call the police if sound patterns are observed that match with domestic violence? As the system is largely for the benefit of the hotel themselves, we trust the system to be non-invasive, and our trust would be broken if it disturbs one's stay at the hotel.
Sustainability: I'm not really sure where sustainability plays into this one really. One could make the monitoring system very pervasive and log water and electricity usage. Perhaps it could detect the long-term absence of a guest and turn off lights and air conditioning. One could implement sensors to determine the cleanliness of the room and reduce energy and time spent on housekeeping. All of this is purely theoretical though, as it does not well suit the scope of the desired monitoring system, or the type of hotel.
Other: Is it right to classify people by gender? Our modern understanding of gender is much more fluid than "male" and "female," so I question the validity of such classification. Even if it was extended further than just gender to a unique identifier, we would need to make that identifier devoid of any sort of gender or sex label. It is a know issue among transgender communities that misidentification via voice causes pain and triggering experiences.

Name: P33
Informed consent: Since audio of the user will be processed to ensure their safety it is important to let them know this is the case when booking the hotel. By booking the hotel they give consent to this system. It is also important this information is not in the regular privacy policy documentation of the hotel since most users don't read them. We must inform them using a separately clickable box when booking the room, essentially forcing the user to read about the safety system before booking.
Privacy: Privacy of the system will be ensured by firstly making the data unaccessable to hotel staff. No data from the microphone will be saved, but only processed and used in real-time at the back-end of the IoT structure. The audio signal picked up by the microphones is filtered through filtering hardware in the room's safety unit. This will leave only a narrow frequency range being sent from the microphones, which makes most word illegible for human hearing, but very well usable for AI in recognizing important sentences like 'help me!', 'help!' or 'I'm hurt!'. Only when the system recognizes one of a set of 'wake words' will it enable the on board high quality microphone and contact the responsible safety employee or safety services.
Information security: Firstly no active data streams from user's room are legible for humans, this makes for a fairly uninteresting target for people meaning harm. Secondly all signals from the safety unit's microphones will be made digital through a DAC (Digital Analog Converter). This makes the data encryptable when sending it to the back-end of the IoT structure over 3G.
Physical safety:
The system I propose focusses solely on the physical safety of the user. The hotel chain states it is interested in recognizing the guests in an anonymous but recognizable way. What we plan to recognize from the user at all times is their physical safety. Most accidents in hotel rooms are slips, falls, cuts and burns. At night when nobody will be around to help low audio quality microphones in the rooms will always be listening to a hotel guest calling for help. It will be triggered by previously mentioned 'wake words'. This will be the case when the user is in their room, recognized by their hotel keycard. When the system is awoken by one of these words, the high quality microphone is enabled and safety services are contacted. The system can also be awoken by using the touchscreen and calling for help in this way. With this high quality microphone some identification can be done on the user by use of AI. This could entail statistics like gender, age, distress levels; statistics that immediately help safety services doing their job. 

When the user is not in their room the safety of the guests' belonings will be ensured by having the high quality microphone enabled at all times, monitoring for burglary.
Trust:
Since the user's audio will always be monitored it is important to let them know they can trust that their conversations are not listened to. This will be done by informing the user about the way their data is processed in an anonymous way. This is done previously when booking, but the room's safety unit's screen could also show a QR code leading to a video explaining the system to the users. 
The safety unit's microphones will be visible and accompanied by small LEDs. These LEDs indicate whether or not the microphones are enabled, showing the user only the illegible microphone is enabled, and the high quality one is not. 
If the user still does not trust the system the microphones can be turned off by a physical switch on the safety unit.
Sustainability:
The system we propose use a minimal amount of hardware to ensure the guests' safety. Two microphones, a speaker, two leds and a small touchscreen is all that's needed to enable all safety features proposed for this system. The choice was made to make this system non-wireless, but choose highly sensity microphones, giving the safety units a lot of range. This also means no batteries have to be used, which are notoriously unsustainable. 
In terms of energy consumption only an extremely low energy microphone and an LED will be enabled at all times. The touchscreen and other components will essentially always be turned off.

Name: P36
Informed consent: The app must ask for permission to analyse the data and store it, indicating what the data will be used for, and perhaps more importantly, what it will not be used for. This is to prevent data from users being shared without their knowledge or consent. The shirt itself cannot be connected to an informed consent so for every system or device that tracks the data the user must be able to give consent.
Privacy: Some of the information stored in the device like pregnancies info, BMI and  diabetes pedigree is very personal. You may not want to share data with others or only with your doctor, for example. Therefore the user must be assured that info is not shared with others, for example by signing an informed consent form.
Information security: The app could have its own security system so that not every user or visitor on someones device can access personal data without asking. For instance in the form of a passcode that needs to be entered when opening the app.
Physical safety:
Sensors are located on the shirt and in contact with the skin. Therefore, there must be no irritation and, above all, no risk of explosion or spontaneous fire. Any wiring must also be secure.
Diabetes must be measured properly and no false conclusion may be drawn. So products must be tested carefully and info must first be sent to the doctor who can then also do his own tests. So no conclusion: you have diabetes, but a recommendation to have it examined by a doctor.
Trust: There could be a risk that the user does not trust the outcomes of the data and does not believe he/she is over-training or has diabetes when this actually is the case. Name: P37
Informed consent: In order to accurately identify discomfort in a passenger, the Smart Seat must collect biometric data. This data can be considered sensitive, as it contains information on the physical/mental properties of the passenger; some passengers get uncomfortable in a warm seat, while others enjoy it. By travelling with airlines using the same Smart Seat system, a user profile can be constructed which identifies the conditions that the passenger enjoys or does not enjoy. A more accurate prediction leads to a more comfortable flight and better user experience. That being said, the user must understand what is happening to their data by using the system. A simple opt-in/opt-out option is not enough; the passenger must be educated. As described by Allhof and Henschke, a long and detailed EULA is likely not going to be read or understood by the user. The challenge here is to provide knowledge to the passenger that they can understand. My advice is to do this during the pre-flight instructions. Usually, the flight attendants will instruct the passengers about how to use the seatbelt, what the light indicators mean, and what to do in case of emergency. After this is done, a short explanation of the system can be given by the attendants, in combination with an illustration on the IFE screen. The passengers are asked if they want to make use of the system or opt-out. This screen should also provide a link to a full EULA for those interested, but it is important that the core information is provided by the attendants and the illustration. This way, most people should be able to make an informed decision on whether they want to share their data. The topic of informed consent is likely going to be a source of discussion if the Smart Seat system is released, as the collected data can be considered medical information. It is therefore under more strict rules and guidelines from the EU and other organizations with the people‚Äôs best interest in mind.
Privacy:
As discussed in the previous section, the data gathered by the Smart Seat can be considered sensitive. A person‚Äôs emotional state or nervosity could be derived from the sensor data (sweating, micromovements, high body temperature), especially when combined with more information regarding the context. This information needs to be protected from people with malicious intent, but also from possible risky parties. For example, if all user profiles are stored on a server form a third party without much protection, there is not much the airline can do about the safety of the data. Anonymizing the user profiles can improve privacy, but that makes the system less efficient as the comfort prediction is based on individual preferences.
Instead, the airline should focus on getting the least amount of ‚Äúmiddle men‚Äù between the Smart Seat and the server containing user profiles. This lowers the amount of points of possible security failure. Also, the data should obviously be encrypted in some way when it is being transferred between nodes. It will be challenging to separate this data stream from the rest of the passengers, as the airplane theoretically has a single wireless network to which all user devices are connected. This can of course be partially solved by making an inaccessible network to which only the Smart Seats are connected, but for maximum safety it is wise to employ an active security team which monitors connections and identifies possible data breaches. These privacy concerns and the airline‚Äôs actions against them should also be mentioned in the EULA or pre-flight instruction.
Information security: Optimally, Smart Seat data is used during multiple flights to improve the effectiveness of the comfort prediction algorithm. If the passenger only travels with a single airline, that might be possible. But what if the passenger decides to use another airline? Would they have their own Smart Seat system? In the best case, all Smart Seats work with the same database. This brings some concerns, however: Multiple airlines using the same system increases the possible points of security failure massively. If a single airline company has a data breach, all others are affected. A possibility would be to have all Smart Seats be part of a single, separate company/organization that manages their security. This comes with the problem that only one company holds a monopoly over all Smart Seats, leading to possibly unmanageable responsibility and an unfair market for competitors. As mentioned in the previous section, airlines should take extra care to make sure the security of the data is guaranteed. This will take a lot of effort, though, with higher costs and therefore ticket prices as a consequence.
Physical safety: Because the Smart Seat uses only sensors and no actuators, it can be said that it is a relatively safe IoT product. However, because of the presence of electronics in otherwise normal plane seats, there is an added risk of failure such as short circuiting, which can lead to things from the system breaking to electrical fires. Aside from the seat itself, there is also the question of liability regarding comfort. If a passenger is highly uncomfortable, but somehow does not communicate this to an attendant, and the Smart Seat also does not work, this can lead to physical complaints like back pain. In that case, it is difficult to point a finger and blame a certain party. The Smart Seat should have detected the discomfort, the attendant should have alleviated the discomfort, and the passenger should have noticed the discomfort. Of course, in general, discomfort is not immediately a risk of physical injury. It should be taken into account though, that some passengers, like the physically handicapped, understandably put their trust in the system to identify their needs. This would give them more autonomy, but the question remains whether this is a practical approach. If their needs are ignored too long, they might experience physical pain, or just a very unenjoyable flight.
Trust: Taking all previous sections into account, the Smart Seat system is clearly something which needs to be trusted in order to work effectively; if users opt-out of the function, less data can be used to train the algorithm and provide accurate predictions. If people don‚Äôt trust the security of the Smart Seat, they may avoid airlines which use them. Many people already claim to distrust connected technologies, and integrating them even further into daily life might raise questions regarding trust. It is important that passengers are therefore fully aware of the function of the Smart Seat, and can make an informed decision for themselves. Airlines will undoubtedly try to gain people‚Äôs trust with marketing, but it is essential that this trust is based on truth. If an airline damages the general public‚Äôs trust by selling, leaking or otherwise misusing personal data, it could impact the Smart Seat system to a point where it is no longer allowed on flights. Trust determines whether the Smart Seat is a success or not.
Sustainability: Flying has been a controversial topic in the context of sustainability. Some might argue that adding a Smart Seat to improve comfort on a flight further promotes unsustainable transport. It can also be hypothesized that the added electronics of the system not only cost more (critical) resources to produce and electricity to power, but also increase the plane‚Äôs weight, leading to more fuel consumption and thus emissions per flight. It is important to remember, though, that social sustainability is also a part of sustainability. If passengers are happier during their flight, the positive impact of the Smart Seat is clear. There is a balance between material use, energy use, emissions, and positive social impact. It is very difficult to determine where that balance lies, but the designers of the Smart Seat should and likely will consider this during their development of the system.

Name: P38
Informed consent: Informed consent in the context of this IOT system, would mean that the user of the smart shirt and app understands how his/her data is used in this product/service system. It is important, that the user is still able to make their own decisions about how much of their data is shared and with whom. For the system to work perfectly, most users would need to share their data (anonymously), so that the algorithm could use this data as new training input, and learn to identify more risk factors for more medical conditions. However, users should also have the option to not share (parts of) their personal data and only store it on a password protected server. It would be highly unethical for Nike to want to sell the data of its users to third parties, as it concerns medical data, but would they choose to do so anyway, they should clearly state this in the consent forms and allow the user to not agree with that clause specifically, without removing functionalities from the product-service system.
Privacy: As mentioned before, the user should always be able to control which data is shared with the company and other companies, and which data is not shared. Furthermore, when data is used to improve the algorithm, this should always be done in an anonymous way, and non-retraceable to the specific user.
Information security: Many IoT devices, such as this smart-shirt, depend on other devices in order to work. The shirt connects via Bluetooth to the users phone, which connects the users data to the server. This means that this the security of the product and corresponding shirt is only as strong as its weakest link. This could quite possibly be the users old phone or computer, or also their passwords.  The product service system can advice the user what devices to use, or how to create a safe password, but apart from this they cannot influence this. What the Nike could do, is provide a safe and personally encrypted server, and only process users personal information in an anonymous way into the algorithm.
Physical safety: Although the shirts goal is meant to encourage and increase peoples fitness, the product should beware that the user does not overtrain, and any fitness advice should be tailored to that users specific level of health and overall fitness to prevent injuries. Furthermore, the shirt should include safety measures for training outside, like reflective surfaces.
Trust: The product service system works optimally, when users decide to share their data with the company, in order to improve the algorithm. Therefore, Nike needs the users to trust them with their data. To earn that trust, they need to have a transparent policy about how the users‚Äô data is processed, and live up to this policy; they should be diligent about how they handle the users‚Äô private data.
Sustainability: Although smart fabrics often have many benefits and opportunities, sustainability is not necessarily one of them. Fabrics are notoriously hard to recycle, but especially, when electronics have been woven into the fabric. To ensure that the product is as sustainable as possible, the electronic parts should not be completely woven into the fabric, but should be more like removable modules, that can be exchanged easily when broken. Furthermore, the shirt should be made durable, as to not wear out before lifetime of the electronic parts has been met. The fabric should also be recyclable. Nike should implement a service to repair broken shirts, and collect worn out/discarded smart shirts, so hardly any material goes to waste.
Other: Although the shirt could theoretically be able to warn the user about risk factors for certain medical conditions, the user should still have the option not to receive these warnings, as they could also result in a heightened stress level. If a user is, for example, a hypochondriac, receiving warnings about possible health risks, could worsen this condition. For this, or whatever other reason a user would not want to know if they might be ill, the user should be able to deactivate health warnings.

Name: P39
Informed consent:
Regarding informed consent, it is important that only necessary information of one stakeholder (for example an inhabitant) is shared with other stakeholders. Transparency about what information is gathered and with whom it is shared is key here. 

For the stakeholders to be properly informed, it is important to both explain the reasons why it is important to use this system, as well as all the data collected from that stakeholder and the data that the stakeholder will receive. Whenever possible things should be anonymized.
Assuming inhabitants would use a sort of application, it should be informed about this at the download page of the app. In addition, when the app is downloaded, it should ask the user for permission to use Bluetooth signals and run on the background. 

Examples of what other stakeholders can receive from inhabitants could be: 
      Other inhabitants / application users: applications of other users only save your anonymized Bluetooth code, which the users cannot review themselves. Only when you sent a signal with your application that you are infected with Covid, other users that have been in contact with you recently within 1.5m for more than 15 minutes get a message like this ‚ÄúOn 14.05.2021 around 14.30 you have been contact with someone that is currently infected by the Covid-virus, please call the GGD to discuss what you should do‚Äù. 
      Restaurants, public transport, event organisers etc., should have the right to ask inhabitants to show their covid certificate. When scanning the corona certificate of visitors, they only see if the user is allowed or not (based upon criteria set by the government) on their scanning device. The scanning device does in addition save the Bluetooth code of the visitor to be able to send through warnings on covid infections if needed later on. This Bluetooth code should not be visible in the application of the certificate scanner.
Privacy:
If we want to elaborate on the current system with the 'corona melder' that is already in place, we might consider linking the service to DigiD. In this way test results and vaccination results can be shared via the service with the inhabitant, and the user can subsequently share this (in the form of a corona certificate) with other stakeholders such as event organisers. However linking the service to DigiD is also a risky step, since this includes much more personal and confidential information to the system. It should be made sure that only the inhabitant him/herself and the GGD can access the confidential information, like test results and vaccination data. For all the other stakeholders, non-necessary information should not be accessible, and other information should be anonymized.
It should be made clear who is allowed to scan the covid certificates in order to provide access to visitors. And it should also be visible for inhabitants who scanned their certificates.

It should not be possible for anyone that scans the certificate to link this with other personal data of the user. Or at least the scanning device used should not save any other data rather than the bluetooth code of the scanned person to be able to sent through warnings later on if needed. Certificate scanners should not be able to review these codes. However it is of importance that the certificate scanner can check if the account really belongs to the user in front of them. The application of the user should therefore show a picture of the user as well. 

If restaurants and events scan covid certificates of visitors, it might be nice to anonymously share this data with the RIVM. In such a way that the RIVM could study the effect of for example opening up restaurants to the amount of infections. However here it is extremely important that both information on visitors as well as event organisers is completely anonymized. For the organisers to use this service they do not want to take the risk of getting a penalty for example when many infections appeared after that event. 

The way this data could be shared is for example:
-	Restaurant ‚Äì 14-05-21 ‚Äì 59 visitors ‚Äì 4/59 infections (17-05-21)
-	Outside event ‚Äì 12-05-21 ‚Äì 130 visitors ‚Äì 40/130 infections (17-05-21) 
In this way RIVM can increase their knowledge and design more adequate measures to control Covid.
Information security:
-	For the system to work, covid certificates should be reliable. Therefore it should not be possible for inhabitants to edit the certificate data themselves (like altering a test result). In addition, to assure safety and validity of the system, it cannot be possible to link self-tests to the application, since these tests are not fully reliable and it cannot be checked by a second party if the test result really belongs to the specific user him/herself. 
-	Also, if inhabitants have an application of the system on their phone, this should be protected by an password. Imagine that a person‚Äôs phone would be stolen, there should not be the risk of persons personal details being exposed or that the thief can somehow via this service get access to DigiD.
o	It should also be possible to block the application via a different security system ( in the case the scenario above presents itself).
Physical safety: To increase the physical safety of inhabitants, it might be nice if inhabitants can check the infection predictions of areas they are in or want to go to, for example on a map. These predictions should be based on the published reports on infections per municipality that the government publishes every day, and should not be based upon the input from the service itself, since this should be kept private.
Trust:
Related to privacy, to make sure people trust the system and believe that their information is safe, it is of great importance that there is transparency about which stakeholders use the service, what each stakeholder shares with the system and what information stakeholders can receive from the system.

This information should be provided to all stakeholders before they make use of the service, and should be easy to find back. 

Linking the service to DigiD might increase people‚Äôs trust in the system, since DigiD is already an established service and most often trust by inhabitants.

To increase the trust, a feedback system should be implemented as well to further improve the service. 

It is also important to consider who should be the owner of this certificate system. If it would be the government, this might not be that trustworthy since the government has a advantage of being in charge of all the information shared and gathered, but on the other hand linking it to DigiD might increase trust. Maybe the RIVM could be the proper owner of this system.  

Lastly, although not related to the system itself, trust can be influenced by the communication of covid regulations set by the government. If this is done in a unclear manner and regulations constantly change, people might feel confused about what is or is not allowed and which measures work and do not work. This might also indirectly influence the trust people have in this certificate system.
Sustainability: By making this certificate system mostly online, this means that certificates do not have to be printed all the time. This could be considered slightly more ethical towards the environment.
Other:
Ethics regarding inclusiveness:
-	Although it is the easiest to make everything online, some user groups such as elderly might be limited by this. Therefore in addition to a phone application, elderly or maybe family of elderly should also be able to access the certificate part of the system online, and print a proof of this. 
-	Although it is not fully inclusive, I do not think it is feasible to design something by which people that do not use a mobile phone can make use of the Bluetooth warning part of the system.  But this is something that should be further discussed.

Name: P40
Informed consent: Felyx should make sure that they inform the users that their data will be used for data analysis.

Name: P42
Informed consent: Informed consent is an important topic for those who are physically handicapped, and possible also mentally challenged. The company has the obligation to fully inform the user what is done with their data, how it is collected, and when it is collected. The user should be fully aware what they are consenting to, and should be aware of the consequences of this. For the users of a wheelchair, the possibility might be there that they cannot fully understand what they are consenting to, and that this consent might be forgotten after a while when the user is also suffering from mental disabilities. The company should bear in mind that this is a very vulnerable group, that might not be fully aware to what they are consenting to. A possible action that the company can take to ensure that the users are still fully consenting is to ask for consent multiple times throughout the year, and provide transparent information what the data is used for to either the users, or the caretakers for review.
Privacy: The privacy that the users will give up by consenting to sharing their data, should be respected by the company. The privacy of the user is already under stress when the user consents to all the data that is shared in the event that all sensors are used. As this is a vulnerable group, their standards of privacy could already be shifted compared to the rest of the society, but the company should still ensure that the data is not misused, and that the data is only used for the intentions that the user agreed to. They give up their right to certain privacy, in exchange that it helps them in their daily life and provides value to their life. The company has the responsibility that this privacy is not violated by unknowingly using their data for other purposes, or is shared with others outside the agreement. A possible way to ensure this is to make it transparent for the users or caregivers to see who has access to what data, and when this data is last updated. This could provide the user with some reference and reflection on what data is shared with whom, and when.
Information security: As there is a great amount of data flowing through the IoT architecture, and much of the data is very privacy sensitive, the encryption of the data is a must. Without the encryption the data of the user is vulnerable to an attack from criminals, and poses a threat to their life. As this is a vulnerable group, the possible dangers are even higher, and the possible harm to the users is also increased. These user might not have the same defences that the rest of society has, and they might be taken advantage of more easily. It can also not be expected from the user, that they are capable of maintaining the encryption passwords on the system on a regular basis. As this is a vulnerable user group, the company should have measures in place that are tailored to the user group, and still ensure that the encryption of the system is up to date. The data collection should thus not continue, when the security of the data is not ensured. As the increased security might hamper the functioning of the IoT system, in this case the focus should be on the safety and not on the smooth operation. Extra costs should be taken as a given, and the functioning should not be completely ignored, as the system can perhaps save the life of the user.
Physical safety: The physical safety of the user is of the utmost importance, as the system has the goal of protecting the user, and making sure that the user is not dealt great harm. For the IoT system, it is different than normal internet services, as these have their manifestations in real life, and not only virtual. The system has to take into account that in the case of a system failure, the physical safety of the user is never endangered. For instance, if the GPS signal fails to register, the system should automatically alert the caregivers or the user itself, to make sure that the user knows what is going on, and can act as soon as possible to resolve this. As this is a vulnerable group of users, they place a great deal of trust in the system and expect it to prevent them from physical harm, but it has to ensure functionality, and when it does not it should alert the user that action has to be taken.
Trust: Trust is an important factor when considering the relation between the vulnerable user group, and the company. Whilst these might have very different goals in their life, the company is keen on providing the best service for their users while being profitable, the user group just wants what is the best for themselves. The trust in this system can be based on the goodwill of the company, the feeling from the users that the company will act to do good. Any action that can violate this sense of goodwill, can already alter the bond of trust between the company and the user. For instance, the company Permobil is owned by an investment company ‚ÄúInvestor‚Äù, who are aiming to take over multiple more companies in Scandinavia and the North-American region.  This gives a clear sign to the users of the wheelchair that the company that is providing their equipment, and is handling their data, is interested in obtaining other companies and more profit. As the bond of trust is so important between the users and the company, these are not signs that the user would like to see, as they would prefer that the focus is on their wellbeing, and providing a service to them.
Sustainability: Sustainability should always be taken into account in the architecture, especially when it comes to material and power usage for the system. It is a balance on whether the IoT architecture can actually offer enough value for the user compared to the environmental strain caused by the exotic electric materials, and the power usage from the system (the remote cloud server). For this project a question can be raised on how much value the system offers to help those in a powered wheelchair, as opposed to the environmental cost that the system creates. It is a discussion whether the impact on the lives of the users can be held in a higher regard than the impact on the environment. This discussion can be raised for many more things in life, but the impact on the life of the users of the wheelchair can be different than for the rest of society. As this is a very vulnerable user group, they might not have the same methods of protection, and care for them that the rest of society has, and which the wheelchair can offer them. In this case can you rank this impact higher than if it would be used for someone that is not in a powered wheelchair? A compromise for this is to see the efficacy of each of the systems and use the ones that are proven to have a great effect, and discard the ones that only offer a small improvement. In this way the environmental effects can be justified by providing the users of the wheelchair more opportunities at a better life

Name: P45
Informed consent:
The product will consist of a T-shirt and a controller device to be purchased together, and a seperate app on the user's phone that connects to the controller device. I think transparency is very important when it comes to informed consent, so both in the package of the shirt and when installing the app the user will get a message about what Nike is doing with their data. Off course there is always the risk of people not reading it or only skimming through it, but they at least do have the option to know. 

For the application the informed consent message should be clear, somewhat entertaining and comprehensible. This means that instead of the long, usual form companies present you with, the use of the data should be explained through visuals with short descriptions. The user can then tap the visuals to get more information about that aspect of data sharing. This way, they know what they're actually agreeing to because they have at least read the shortened descriptions of what happens to their data. 

As mentioned in the lecture, the informed consent is dynamic. The user might choose to share their data in a certain time or context and then forget about it. It is unethical to make use of the fact that someone forgets about their data being shared, and that's why the application needs to remind people and ask again about their informed consent every time the device has not been used for two weeks. This ensures that everyone who has not used the device in quite some time is reminded again of what it does exactly.

Just as mentioned in the lecture on ethics, people are more willing to share their data when it serves a higher cause. The Nike sport shirt is purchased by people who care for their health and are looking to improve their fitness; thus serving a higher cause. As the designer for the system we therefore have to be aware of the ethical concerns tied to this fact that people might be more willing to share their data for the purpose of getting healthier and be transparent and honest about what's happening to it.
Privacy:
The application located on your mobile device will gather all the data that is retrieved from your smart shirt. This application in turns sends your data to the Nike Server to let the algorithm located there select the best workout for you, and also to improve the algorithm itself with your data.

To ensure the user's privacy the data will be in no way registered to your name or e-mail adress. Your user profile at Nike will get a random number assigned, and information such as your name and e-mail address are optional to fill in the app, and even then they are not saved to the cloud, Nike, or sent to any server. Any information that needs to come from you to the server or vice versa is contributed to that specific number, and not anything else. 

Although this system of assigning a random number instead of a name or e-mail adress enhances the anonomity of the system, the retrieval of a dataset of a person could still say a lot about what kind of person they are since it has their age, BMI, glucose levels, etc. In a database such as big as Nike's (with an app with over 21 million downloads) these datasets can impossibly be traced back to their original owners, but if you were to guess out of a pool of 10 people who a certain dataset belonged to you would defenitely run into some privacy issues. Although the scale of the system makes it more anonymous it's important to keep the inherent flaws of such a system in mind.

Also, I think it's important to give people the option to stay off the internet entirely with their data and still get the health benefits. Therefore, the user can opt out of uploading their data to the Nike Server's if they also opt out of receiving personally recommended workouts. They can still access workouts from the server since that doesn't require any uploading of personal data, but the algorithm and danger zone values will be stored locally on the device of the user, so the data does not have to be uploaded in order for the algorithm to predict their chances of health and fitness. 

Lastly, I want people to be able to share their workout with their friends via the app; this will go through the Nike server as well, but once again only via the random unique numbers attached to the people. The assigned name to the number will, once again, only be stored locally on user's devices. The user can choose which data to share with their friends so they stay in charge of what they want to share, or if they want to share at all.
Information security:
The data on the user's phone is vulnerable to hacking attempts or a data leak. This can be somewhat countered by giving the user the option to set a password for the application or to enable logging in with the biometric sensors present in most phones nowadays such as FaceID or TouchID. The Bluetooth and Wifi connections in the system between the controller device, the mobile phone and the Nike server's do not have to be of the uttermost reliablity since the data is stored locally on the controller device until it's uploaded to the phone, where it is also saved locally. From there, the data will be updated to the Nike server's when the WiFi or 4G of the user is sufficient to do so, which it probably will be in the biggest part of the Western World.

However, for the Bluetooth device it is important to note that super old phones do not work with newer versions of Bluetooth. This has to be kept in mind when the decision of which phones to support will be made. 

Information security is important when it comes to the server's of Nike. Since the data that is uploaded to these servers is encrypted with a random number the security of the individual users is mostly safe, but we still have to be wary of data leaks, inappropriate use of ACLs and lack of secure identity. In order to counter all this a good firewall should be in place, and every night a CronJob will be carried out on the server's of Nike to make a back-up of all the data and algorithm so it won't get lost.
Physical safety:
For the physical safety of the product my concern was mainly people's mental health. The prototype of this assignment was writing an algorithm that determines whether someone is at risk for diabetes. My main concern is whether people should be getting this news from an app. I think bad news like that is best received by a doctor with a person to support you by your side, not via a phone screen. My solution for this concern in my own prototype is framing the message as follows; You might be at risk for developing diabetes'. An algorithm is no way of diagnosing people, but it can make them aware of future issues and can make them contact other sources of medical advice. 

Another very important aspect of safety is location tracking in many current sport and fitness devices. With Strava it was a problem a while back that user's could tell where other user's lived based on their uploaded locations and tracking. That is why i did not put a GPS chip in the controller device; this way there is no way of tracking someone through the smart shirt, ensuring that no one will follow someone or figure out where they live based on the data from the controller device. 

Lastly I have put an actuator in the T-shirt; an iron-man-like light on the chest that the user can easily see in the bottom of their field of vision that will indicate in which Heart Rate zone they are in. I designed this because it is something I would like to have myself; many runners and cyclers train according to which Heart Rate Zone they're in; some of them meaning building cardio fitness, burning fat, or building muscle. The light indicates with a color which zone you're in, and turns bright red when your heartrate is getting too high for your own good. This way, users can train for their optimal fitness and are warned when things are about to go wrong and can stop before this happens.
Trust: I think a big part of Nike's branding has always been transparency and daring to make a statement (such as supporting the athlete who stayed down during the national anthem). This will certainly benefit them when it comes to people trusting them with their data. Also, they have had major apps such as Nike Fitness Club and Nike Running Club for years without any complaints. However, to keep up the trust from people I think they should stay transparent about what they're doing and what they're using the data for. I think a big part of this is being able to not upload your data in order to get the health benefits and chosing which parts of your data are shared with friends. Giving people the ability to opt out of things will greatly improve their trust in you.
Sustainability:
Since we're dealing with a physical product we are off course also dealing with the sustainability of the materials needed to produce these products. 

Let's start off with the shirt; the fibers needed to make the stretchy and tight material for the shirt and sensors are polyester, nylon, spandex and silver. As these materials are in itself not great for the environment to produce, they can be recycled or repurposed. However, I think the sustainable approach to these shirts is not to be found in the recycling of them but rather in the care people will take of them since they're expensive and high-tech. Their expensivess also gives them resale value, which means that they can be sold or passed down to another user after their initial user is done with them, giving them a second life and thus improving sustainability. Also, a user should be able to buy multiple shirts that the one controller device can connect to; this way only one controller device has to be purchased and the user can workout multiple times a week while wearing a clean shirt. 

Another sustainability issue is off course the data and therefore fuels that the server will use up while processing all the data and the algorithms. 

The controller device should also be made in such a way that is rechargable (saving batteries) and is easily repairable (either by the user or an authorized reseller) so that it does not have to be thrown out entirely when breaking; thus enhancing repairability.
Other:
Being confronted with your own fitness data might not be best for the mental health of some people (I sure know I can get obsessed with it), so where does Nike draw the line between healthy fitness concerns and obsession? Should they? 

What if insurance companies start asking for data like this in order to determine whether you're eligible for a certain type of insurance? What if your employer wants to know how often you work-out to determine your risk of injury? What if you decide to share your data with your friends and they judge you for it?

Name: P46
Informed consent:
In the designed IoT architecture, personal data of the passengers (such as boarding status, seat number, age, if they are traveling with children etc.) are needed to be processed and stored in the cloud database. Therefore, an open and comprehensible text or any kind of source (e.g. in the form of a video) explaining how/ when to process and store their personal data, what kind of personal data will be processed and stored in the database, disposal of data should be presented to the passengers and their consent should be asked after being sure that they understand what they are going to consent. A possible scenario might be a passenger that wants to buy a ticket from the counter for a flight due in 2 hours. The counter personnel hands out terms & conditions of the service agreement that explains processing & storing the personal data and the passenger has to sign it. However, the passenger is in a hurry and signs without reading it. Another scenario might be a passenger that gives consent while booking tickets online without understanding the terms & conditions even though the explanation is clear but has no prior experience with this new technology on the plane. 

Also, in the designed IoT system, there is a noise level sensor that can record sound levels of the passengers. One passenger might not give consent to record their sounds while the one sitting next to them might give. These kinds of situations should also be taken into account.
Privacy: In the IoT system, there are especially two sensors that might raise questions about privacy. Those are the noise level and the pressure mat sensors. For the noise level sensor, it can be said that privacy is not an issue since it does not record the sounds of the passengers in the way that a microphone does. It only measures the level of the ambient noise in decibels. On the other hand, the pressure mat sensors might cause some privacy issues. These sensors measure force applied to certain areas of the seat, which can also give information about the weight/size of the passenger sitting on it.  For example, if the same person travels twice with the same airline but there is an increase in her weight, that might be an indication of a pregnancy, which is a piece of information she probably did not consent to provide to the airline. Even though the purpose is not collecting sensitive/intimate data from the passengers with these sensors, these aspects should not be disregarded.
Information security: Regarding the information security in the designed system, there are a couple of scenarios to be considered. Since the data are gathered by sensors and sent to the local server and remote cloud via gateways, the security of the networks plays an important role. Therefore, all data gathered by the sensors and data fed from the airport should be encrypted to prevent possible hacker attacks and those data should only be accessible by authorized personnel with strong passwords. Even though encryption of data might add a high amount of costs and big responsibilities to the users, it might be crucial. One example scenario to understand the importance of protection of data might be the steal of the passenger‚Äôs personal data by plane hijackers. For example, they might discover that a person is an authority within a company or a government body, which can cause to put that passenger‚Äôs life in danger.
Physical safety: In terms of the physical safety of the designated IoT system, correct functioning of the actuators and the sensors embedded inside the seat (e.g. pressure mat sensors) should be ensured. Because at this point, IoT differs from the internet, which is mostly constrained to cyberspace, since it does not only depend on a virtual environment but also has effects on a physical environment. A possible issue that might occur within the designated IoT system is the malfunctioning of IFE earphones which acts as an actuator. When the noise level close to a certain passenger increases, that passenger might be annoyed or disturbed and therefore, the comfort level of that passenger might decrease. This can be sensed with the pressure mat sensor by realizing that the movement of the passenger is increased and this data can be matched with the output of noise level sensor data. As a result of this matched data, the cause of the discomfort of that passenger might be identified as the high noise level and the sound level of the earphones might be increased for example. However, due to a malfunction sound level of the earphones might be increased to the maximum level and this can result in physical damage in the ears of the passenger.  Also, the machine learning algorithms that are helping to identify the root causes of the discomfort might not be 100% accurate. Hence, this can also be a threat for the physical safety of the passengers.
Trust: As stated by Allhoff et. al., trust is directly related to  other ethical issues such as physical safety, informed consent, privacy and information security. To exemplify, if the user knows how their data is being used, processed or stored, the user can be aware of the risks and establish trust in the system. Or the other way around, if the user experiences a malfunction within the system, the user might lose trust or reliance on the system. Therefore, the ethical issues stated in the other sections become valid and even more important for this section. Specifically, the correct functioning of AI and the machine learning algorithms in the designated IoT system can be the main issue for this section. If the AI fails to identify the specific root cause of the discomfort of the passenger, activates wrong actuators or causes actuators to function incorrectly, not only the passengers but also the airline company can lose trust in the system. Therefore, this situation might lead to some problems for the company such as the loss of a trusted brand image or economical losses.
Sustainability: Since the IoT systems in general, regardless of a specific context, consist of a lot of electronic parts which are often not recyclable or easily repairable, sustainability is an important aspect to consider. In the designated IoT system, the biggest issue regarding sustainability is a large number of sensors, actuators and connections. Considering an airplane contains an average of 138 seats (PlaneStats, 2015) and this number is usually higher for intercontinental flights due to bigger planes, and most of the sensors or the actuators are placed for each seat, especially maintenance or end of life scenarios of these electronic parts might be troublesome. Additionally, even though some sustainable choices are desired to be made such as using recycled or reused electronic parts, cost and safety can avert these choices. Another issue related to sustainability is high energy consumption due to complex technologies, networks and connections. In the designated IoT system, AI technology is used and this might cause issues such as using high computational power and electricity.

Name: P48
Informed consent: The voice data is being collected and processed as a part of this monitoring device. The guests need to be made available what data is being collected, at what intervals and how this data is used by the hotel.
Privacy: Along with accomodation, hotels are associated with a certain standard of luxury and privacy. The big concern here is regarding the voice recordings. In the system I propose, the voice recording never leaves the room but the number of occupants in the room and their genders are recorded at certain intervals. The guests have the right to not disclose this information to any parties.
Information security: The monitoring system is equipped with hardware capable of fully recording every conversation and transmitting it. If the security of the system is compromised, malicious persons could listen in into conversations without the hotel guests' knowledge.
Physical safety: I cannot see a physical safety issue, as there are no moving parts the monitoring system is responsible for. One concern could be the hotel relying on emergencies to be reported by the system while neglecting upkeep of sensors. For example, if the microphones in one room are broken, and there is a safety issue, this will go unreported. So the hotel has decisions to make on redundancy of safety measures and regular maintenance of the system to ensure functionality.
Trust: There is already big debates going on regarding the trustworthiness of voice assistants like Alexa. Some people do not believe that the full functionality of the device is not disclosed and the creators might be using user data with malicious intent. In terms of user-facing hardware, my designed monitoring system is no different. Feeling comfortable while having a device with voice recording capability in the room requires a large amount of trust by the guest towards the hotel chain. There are other players involved for security and Cloud, but the guest only interfaces with the hotel in their stay and this is the party who they will hold responsible for any misuse of information collected.
Sustainability: In terms of environmental sustainability, concerns are in fields of generation of electronic waste and energy use. A system like they asked requires information to be generated and stored, and all of this processing requires energy use. The tradeoff between value added by the system and the environmental impact in carbon footprint needs to be considered.
Other: One reservation I have about such systems is that they are getting people desensitized to a state of constant and total surveillance. The hotel might be using this information responsibly, and actually creating value for the guest when this situation alone is studied. But there is an increasing number of IoT devices and surveillance equipment in our lives, cameras and other recording devices are used to ensure safety of establishments constantly at alarmingly increasing rates. Unfortunately, decision and policy makers sometimes have to act with the greater good in mind, even if it means sacrificing others. An example of this would be detecting congregation areas of homeless people and planting hostile architecture in these locations. This then becomes very similar to the classic trolley problem, is it ok for the public to be constantly monitored and who do the systems used serve in these cases? Who is benefitting and who needs to be sacrificed for this?

Name: P49
Informed consent: In a lot of cases, your face will be recorded upon entry in a certain perimeter. In general if you place the cameras exact enough there would be no legal problems. Recording people on your own property is not illegal. But the idea of having recordings of anyone who enters your property can be quite disturbing, especially if you would not know. Although most people would do their best to tell friends and family and most wouldn't mind, the one time it slips your thoughts to tell that person it can be an issue for someone for rather good reasons. When something is not illegal, it does not always mean its ethical.
Privacy: Going back on the concerns in Ethics. The idea is that certain images of people and their way of moving around in the home will be recorded. Of course the system tries to keep everything as locally stored as possible, but there will be always a chunk of data that is currently being reviewed by a cloud system somewhere out there. It can a quite big issue regarding privacy if leaks happen. All though I do think that there is a certain consent given by the user if they want to use such a system. But again the biggest issues that such systems can cause, is with unknowing visitors(not the ones that try to rob you, we do not really care about them).
Information security: There is balance here to be found between having just enough information for the system to work smoothly and personalized and having an overload of information sitting somewhere being a security threat. I personally think such systems that try and use as much local storage as possible will always be better, and connected as wired as possible. Than the threat of risk of security breaches will be minimized but also the user would be more responsible. The issue is, if you do not want every user to buy a how many thousand dollar system with enough processing power to run the programs, it is going to be quite difficult to do everything locally.... Than the costs come in and then we al of the suddenly see a lot people not minding the information risks etc...
Physical safety: This system doesn't include any possible harmful actuators, except maybe being locked out of your home. In general, the biggest risk is relying on the system too much for it to notify authorities while a security breach is happening and it not being able to communicate. Then again in that case, you should have a lot more options.
Trust: So in this case as said in previous concerns, it can be an issue to build a system wherein the users get very used to having their safety handled by an automated system. This trust in the system is something a designer would probably strive for by creating a great functioning system. But this trust can also backfire in life threatening situations.
Sustainability: In general the system should focus on sustainability. But in many cases the underlying technology such as the communication layer needs to be well done for it to be actually helping the home being more energy efficient rather than the system overusing energy. Also a big problem is that many electrical parts needs to be replaced when they break down as most chips are not worth it to repair. These electrical home systems can create a lot of e-waste down the road. Untill e-waste can be properly reused(and most of the people are actually doing it) such systems could cause the world more harm than good.

Name: P50
Informed consent: Guests have to register their voice with the hotel before the monitor system can recognise it when the guest is in their room. Therefore, consent should be asked beforehand (during check-in) (or when entering the booked room) since the registering cannot be activated without the user being aware of it as the system would have no basis to compare the voices to. A concern could be that the hotel does not inform the guest enough of what the system exactly does in fear of the guest feeling uncomfortable, and can therefore cause the guest to be unaware of the continuous recording.
Privacy: During the guest's stay in the room, data (in this case voice) will be constantly collected to compare whether it matches the guest's voice. However, this will mainly be focused on the voice features rather than the content (text). Still, a concern could be that this data can be used for another purpose or shared without the user knowing.
Information security: Concerns would be who can view the collected data (voice), whether people will be able to monitor guests in their room (through the IR camera) and whether this data's security is good enough to prevent access of third parties. Tightening security will increase the cost, which is difficult for lower budget hotels, so they might not do this. A partial solution could be that the data is deleted after the guests stay.
Physical safety: The location of the monitoring system is important as placing it close to the bathroom (or maybe even inside) and coming in contact with water could lead to the device breaking and cause dangerous situations such as a fire or electrocution. However, this means that the product from itself should be waterproof to prevent such things from occurring. Infrared cameras can be harmful to the eyes when stared at it for too long, however this is rare and the users would not have any reason to stare at the device depending on the placement of it.
Trust: For the monitoring system, the trust lies in whether the voice will be recognised properly and will alarm the hotel when there is an intruder (so whether the device is working as it should) or that it does not function properly causing frequent false alarms. A trust concern could also lie in whether the collected data will not be used for something the user did not give permission to (informed consent.
Sustainability: The components or material used into creating the components (of the device) should not be retrieved from countries where the working conditions are unethical. Another concern would be the disposal of the product by the user which should not negatively impact the environment.
