Name: P52
Informed consent:
According to the Nuremberg Code informed voluntary consent means that: ‚ÄúA person involved should have legal capacity to give consent; should be so situated as to be able to exercise free power of choice, without the intervention of any element of force, fraud, deceit, duress, overreaching, or other ulterior form of constraint or coercion.‚Äù (BRITISH MEDICAL JOURNAL No 7070 Volume 313: Page 1448, 7 December 1996). You could argue that the implementation of vaccine passport could force people to give informed consent when being vaccinated / using the digital passport, since if they don‚Äôt they are restricted in their freedom. Especially if travelling becomes impossible without the vaccine passport and if the ‚Äúpassport‚Äù will also be implemented in public venues such as restaurant, theatres etc. 

http://www.cirp.org/library/ethics/nuremberg/
Privacy:
The app that holds these digital certificates will have access to the medical information from testing and vaccination authorities which can be seen as a privacy concern. Especially if the app is owned by a third party that is not a health authority or the government. When they gather the data, there are a lot less restriction in place on how it should be used. If such a third party keeps the data, it might also become an easier target for hackers. The best steps to ensuring better privacy is the collection of only necessary data, encrypting the data before it is stored in the app  (which I also added to my architecture) and developing the app within the government/ health authorities, without third parties. 

Another concern would be that the passport could violate privacy, since people would have to share their status to visit places, even If they would rather not. You could argue that you are free to decide not to use that app, but this would restrict a user‚Äôs free-movement, which is a violation of human rights.
Information security:
Requiring users to store their test results and vaccination status in a digital format would expose private medical information to the danger of data breaches (which is of course also a privacy concern).

Furthermore, a centralised database is less secure than a distributed one and could be a big target for hackers.
Physical safety:
One concern, which can also be categorised under privacy but is formulated here, is that digital vaccine passports can be used to monitor user‚Äôs movements which in combination with their personal data can result in situation in which their physical safety is at risk (e.g burglary etc). Data might be collected about where they have scanned their certificate or, in the case of proximity tracking, their whereabouts during the entire day, especially when GPS is used. Using only Bluetooth to track proximity and changing users‚Äô personal Bluetooth keys regularly decreases the risk of a users‚Äô movement being traceable. 

Vaccine passports might strengthen a false feeling of safety, since proof of having been vaccinated does not entirely guarantee that a person cannot be infected with covid or infect others. Vaccines aren‚Äôt a 100% effective in all cases.
Trust: One concern is that the status shown when scanning the QR code can not be completely trusted, since forgeries might enter the market. Allowing people with forgeries to cross borders without meeting the restriction and rules in place.
Sustainability:
A concern regarding sustainability of the IoT system is that the national and international servers in the system require a lot of energy. In 2017 a single server produced more CO2 than a car. Furthermore, pollution of the environment also comes from chemicals used for data centres fire suppression and from the amount of electronic waste which is generated. Server equipment and technology already does not have an infinite lifespan, but for data centres to keep up with requirements and be competitive, equipment needs to be replaced often (Loøbrók, 2017).
Loøbrók, R. (2017, 16 feb). The Environmental Impact Of Server Hosts. Green Web Hosting Articles and News. https://www.orangewebsite.com/articles/the-environmental-impact-of-server-hosts/
Other: see question "Ethics concern regarding pandemic" above

Name: P53
Informed consent:
There are several ethics concerns regarding informed consent.

Frist of all, the information should be clear and understandable to the user. It is common to find informed consent forms to be written in a strange way, not going straight to the point, using technical terms that many users do not understand and being incomplete. In the specific case of the Nike t-shirt the user must be able to understand all the information easily, in combination with a complete overview, yet not overwhelming.

Second, social barriers to autonomy could also play an important role. Users could be influenced by other individuals, or social circumstances/ pressure. The desire of being part of a social group can lead to accepting terms one doesn't agree with or not reading through the informed consent form. The best way to fight against this ethical problem regarding design is to be able to show the most important risks or permissions the user has to be aware of in the first eye glance.

To continue, competence plays an important role concerning the informed consent. Practicing sports is for all ages, however, when tracking health information, a certain level of awareness is necessary. When using the Nike shirt some sensitive data is shared and minors might not know the impact and importance of giving certain permissions. This is why, an ethical challenge arises, if users under a certain age are suitable for this product

In addition, the presentation of facts and application of bias is an issue. As previously mentioned, all the information should be shown, but in a concise way. So, how much information is it required for a competent decision to be made? What can be exposed very briefly and what has to be presented in a more extensive way?


To conclude, the content itself of the informed consent can drive some ethical challenges. If it is stated that consent is needed to track location for example, but also there are permissions to connect to other apps (Facebook for example), these third parties also have access to that information. This is very common with apps or devices that are connected to social media, and the information in the consent form is not 100% true, giving room to ethical dilemmas.
Privacy:
The first issue of this section is derived from the last concern of the informed consent section. It has to do with data selling and or sharing with third parties. In this scenario the content is compromised by other third parties' policies and could result in the loss of privacy previously had. Therefore, it is important that if third parties have access to the data, this must be annonymous, encrypted and keep the same policies as the entity who shares it. Or, in the other hand, be stated that new policies are applicable when the data is shared in the consent form.

With the number of heartbeats, steps, and sleep history tracked, these types of ‚Äúhealth data‚Äù are not formally considered PHI (protected health information) unless it‚Äôs shared with a doctor, hospital, 3rd party vendors and therefore not subject to HIPAA regulations (national standards to protect individuals' medical records and other personal health information and applies to health plans, health care clearinghouses, and those health care providers that conduct certain health care transactions electronically). 

Another aspect to take into account is the ownership of the personal data generated with the wearable device ‚Äî you or the business that‚Äôs compiling your vitals? As the creator of your very own health data, you are not always the one handling the ownership of it. It can happen that some company‚Äôs privacy policy requests the possession of the data in order to access it, which can be dangerous.

To conclude, the ethical issue involving personalization is always there. When personal trainings and suggestions are part of the product, some learning algorithm is needed to be able to deliver this service. However, it is not a free trade. Access to sensitive data is needed and usually third parties are involved in the processing of the data generated to create a profile that matches your needs. All this traffic of information could be intercepted and the user could suffer from blackmailing, social exposure and more. This issue concerns the next section: information security.
Information security:
From the previous section (privacy), the first ethical concern comes by the hand of hacking. It is important to note that the more steps the information travels from, the more vulnerabilities can be found. This means that in the current IoT architecture (product, product to device, device to gateway, device to cloud and back end) there are 5 possible entries for attacks. In this scenario, there is no need for direct access to the product to be able to steal the data. 

1.Product and synchronization between product and mobile phone:
The T-shirt is meant to be worn; however, their owners may well take them off (when finishing the training). Therefore anyone with a compatible device and the corresponding app could theoretically synch with the device and gain access to the data it records. That data could potentially be delivered to the wrong phone without the user noticing it.

Related to this the phone compromised by fake apps. It is possible that users download apps that are created to mimic the real app. Once they introduce their credentials and log in, all the data could be stolen without the user noticing it. It is important that the app needed clearly states that they are the real ones, or that there is a certain control from the app platform download centre to get rid of the imitations.

Due to the tech used, the T-shirt can scan the user‚Äôs environment for other Bluetooth-enabled devices, like mobiles, smart bands, other smart T-shirts, etc. As well as gathering the addresses of these devices, it also passes them to the vendor‚Äôs servers via the phone application. This way, the vendor is potentially able to create a profile of each user‚Äôs infrastructure environment.

2. Synchronization between the mobile phone and the server:
The synchronization between the smartphone apps and the cloud servers is a neuralgic point, since the data stream comprises both the data gathered and the credentials to access the user‚Äôs account. This could be avoided by encrypting the data before leaving the device (device to gateway). By applying simple TLS/SSL encryption this ethical issue could be avoided in this stage.
Physical safety:
The major implication in physical safety concerns the proximity of the device to the body. Although there is not a direct contact with the skin there is only a layer of cloth in between. Because of no safety physical barriers between the product, a severe failure of the electronics (setting on fire) could result in a serious accident. Therefore, the product electronics must be designed with a safety margin to avoid that these kind of situations take place

In addition the danger could be aggravated because a t-shirt is not easily removable. In order to monitor vital constants the t-shirt must be tight, to bring the sensors closer to the body. A tight t-shirt, in combination with sweat can be hard to remove, and more in urgent situations. Thus, a solution to the problem could be designing the t-shirt with not-flammable textile, to avoid bigger damage if this situation takes place.

To conclude, not only the safety of the product itself has to be taken into account, but also the environment, to ensure physical health in case of severe failure.
Trust: The biggest ethic trust issue comes by the hand of not having clear policies regarding security, privacy and informed consent. When some information is not presented in a clear way, users lose confidence in the product as they think that some information is being hidden to them. In addition to this, the issue could grow even worse if some information is actually being hidden. But not only information regarding how the system works, the policies, security, consent and privacy, but also important issues the user might find when using the product. If this happens, the image of the company could be in risk as the customers will feel betrayed.
Sustainability:
The use of electronic circuits always comes by the hand of the sustainability issue regarding the end of life of those components. The difficulty of recycling e-waste, as well as the correct disposal draws several sustainability issues. Also the manufacturing, which normally takes place in places where the working conditions are not favourable and also the environmental impact is not taken into account. The productivity in these places is what matters, and usually it is achieved at a high humanitarian and environmental cost. 

Many social movements nowadays are arising to make people aware of this situation and urge them to change the mind-set, to apply some sustainability ethic filter that punishes those products that do not take care of the environment. This way the companies are forced by the customers to apply sustainability policies within their companies to grab the attention of the buyers. In the case of the t-shirt, materials play a big role and the recyclability of the components, as previously mentioned.

Name: P54
Informed consent: Passengers are the most vulnerable user group, since the information gathered is from them. And they might not be aware of all the features of the smart system since they might not travel verry often. The information that is tracked is the seat posture and their inputs on the IFE on meal preference, scene setting and other activities. People should be informed about this system and make a choice on this topic before information is used. This moment is before take-off, where the data is not yet important. Opting out should also be possible any moment. But the issue would be that it is hard to explain the whole data management briefly for the excited passengers.
Privacy: Peoples names are of course know for the cabin crew when needed, for security reasons (and optionally for extra service if user accounts are made). But the inflight data does not need to be personalised and is just referenced with the seat number. Only the cabin crew is able to observe scenes people are in, but never specific data they do on the IFE, that is only in the back-end system. Still, it can seem intrusive to passengers that their behaviour is being tracked, especially tracking physical activities is something we are not used to. The mechanic only has access to anonymous raw data of what happened during flight and only if something went wrong.
Information security: The system centralises most data to be able to make airplane wide decision on the scenes and for the crew to monitor everything. This could be a vulnerability if someone manages to break into the system. But the whole system is not connected the worldwide internet, only a system in the airplane. The physical data lines are probably safe since someone tampering with these wires, or the server, is easily spotted. Remaining are the Zigbee connections from sensors/actuators with the modems and the Wi-Fi connections of the IFE and crews tablets. Someone might be able to access this via a laptop. So these systems should be encrypted and use a different Wi-Fi network than passengers.
Physical safety: The smart cabin system could cause harm in a few ways, but it does not have to be dangerous. The system controls lighting, blinds, chair position and the bins, sometimes automatically. Lighting would only cause inconvenience if looked directly into when it would adjust brighter. The motorized actuators do not need much force but could in some way hurt passengers (or crew) by pinching. Therefore these actuators need sensors to sense obstructions and give a heads up before automatically controlled on the IFE and tablets. And even the actuators themselves have a indicator light or sound.
Trust: Trust is a topic that varies on a few scope levels. If the cabin crew is seen as the owner of the smart cabin system, passengers would see a real person‚Äôs face and might be able to trust them. To establish such a relation, the cabin crew should know the ins and outs of the system, be able to answer questions from passengers and explain the data risks. But on a broader level, these systems are of course owned by large airlines. People can have a strong relation with these companies, this is especially true for the premium airlines. The budget airlines on the contrary, have a bad reputation for lowering costs over passenger comfort. So for these airlines trust could be an issue, although I doubt if they would invest in such a system any time soon  for the same reason.
Sustainability: Talking about sustainability on an environmental level, which is the most relevant, there are a lot of airplanes in the air and therefore a lot of extra products. The system touchpoints (IFE, tablets) are already used in airplanes, but the added components for automation of the seats, bins, lights and blinds are not. This generates a larger carbon footprint for production, but disposal can be easily taken care of in a good manner since it is a closed system. And repair is also common in the airline industry. But maybe the highest impact is the added weight, on an airplane every gram costs more fuel which is a leading parameter in the design and use of airplanes.
Other: In general, automatically or forced control of someone‚Äôs seat can seem very intrusive, rude and direct. This could be something people are afraid of and against to. A possible solution would be to gradually move the chair, lighting and blinds over a time period (for example 10 minutes). This changes the interaction from intrusive to magical.

Name: P57
Informed consent:
For this "smart cabin" users must be able to give permission on using their data regarding: body posture, body temperature and noise registration for noise cancellation. Besides they should be well informed in a easy to read way how their data is used. For informing the passengers one of the regulatory frameworks as named in the paper can be used. 

I envision each of the passengers receive an easy to read statement on data usage when purchasing his/her tickets for the flight, so she/he is well informed already before the flight. 

During the flight when entering the seat, I foresee the passenger gives permission via the touchscreen, for the use of his data and can read the statement again if wanted.
Privacy:
Regarding the requirements of this assignment I opt for a local network, in which the passengers data is stored only for the duration of the flight. It is not needed to store data in an online server, because it is only needed to perform certain actions, by actuators or the cabin crew during the flight. 

Besides the data is anonymised, using a IR-camera which is only suitable of tracking posture and temperature but can not recognise a particular human being. Audio files of the flight are processed within the chair and act as a real-time inout for noise cancellation. Therefor they are not stored in any form for longer than a few (mili)seconds. 

For further expanding of the system I can image passengers would get insight in there comfort levels during multiple via an app or website. For this integration a connection must be made to an external server during or after the flight uploading the data. If this would be the case the airline would take measurements for data-protection and should be transparent about third-party enclosures. Beside they must give the user the possibility to easily delete their data. 

However for this case, I have left out this option regarding the requirements of the user scenario.
Information security:
As described above for future scenarios in which the user can connect with an app/ service after landing, data must be protected well when being stored at an external server. 

But also within the plane information must be secured. However the most 'sensible' audio data is not being shared by any form of wireless connection, or stored for a long period of time. Therefore it should be hard to reach for potential hackers or third parties. 

The data being transferred throughout the local network via Bluetooth such as body temperature and posture should be protected well taking measurements on the wireless network of the plane. 

Above all, all data should be deleted from the server immediately after the plane has landed.
Physical safety:
The actuators in this system are: buzzers in the seat, Cooling fan, speakers and the touchscreen. When looking at physical safety the first three could have an impact like buzzing to hard causing pain, cooling too hard giving a cold, make loud sound resulting in a hearing issue. 

For each of these actuators safety limits must be build in within the controlling software or make use actuators not powerful enough to cause any harms. Besides it must be enclosed in the informed consent what the risks are when using these actuators.
Trust:
Regarding trust the informed consent plays an important role:

All motives of the system should be made clear. -> the why of these "smart cabins" is to create a more pleasurable flight and data will be deleted after it. So data handing is also part of it. 

Besides the system should minimise the change failing of one of the components. If the fan is not working properly you won't trust the system knows how to handle your body temperature. If components fail, it should be notipficated to the users so it can understand why the system is not working.
Sustainability:
Regarding sustainability I would opt for a state of the art system, strongly weighting costs against the "how future proof" the system is. Looking at aircraft they've quite a long lifespan. For example choosing for uptown date wires could make it more future proof for coming data connections.

Besides it should be easy to replace integral parts such as the touchscreen design and different actuators as the speakers, fan and buzzer for replacement when broken. This should be taken into account in the design of the chair.

Name: P58
Informed consent:
In order to access the website, users must first accept the cookies. And to install the app, users must provide access to their location, camera and video usage. During this process there is the opportunity to read the 6-page general terms and conditions and 7-page privacy agreement. As Allhoff and Henschke (2018) describe in their research, these pages are often very detailed and extensive. In addition, they use technical and legal jargon that is inaccessible to many users. Because of the length and the difficult language, one can assume that most users do not read it, which raises the question of whether you have informed Felyx users well enough about the informed consent. For instance, only on page 5 of Felyx‚Äôs privacy agreement, it is mentioned that your ‚ÄòNAW‚Äô data (personal data) could be shared with third parties. In addition, there is no specific mention of which third parties your data might be shared with and what the consequences might be for you. This information could be confusing and could make users decide whether or not to agree to these terms and conditions. Furthermore, there is no alternative available if you do not want to accept the agreements but still want to use Felyx, something that is also an ethics concern according to Allhoff and Henschke (2018). There is simply an 'okay' button that you have to click to use the website or the app. 
To improve the informed consent concern, Felyx could, for example, focus more on making
the agreements understandable by putting the most important points for users in bullet points at the top of the documents. 
Another concern is that Felyx wants to include tailored advertisements on gender, age and estimated salary. However, it is not clear from where they source this data. In addition, users have not chosen to receive this tailored advertisement, which is questionable in terms of informed consent.
Privacy:
As mentioned earlier, the Felyx app requires users to provide access to their camera, video recordings and location. By sharing this information, it is claimed that your product experience improves. However, it is not clear how they use this information and for what purpose. Do they only measure your location during scooter rides, or all the time, or how long you are parked? Can they record conversations on the phone because they have access to your microphone or do observations while using your camera? This information can not only be exploited by hackers, but Felyx can also use this information to come to very specific conclusions about individuals. An example is analyzing the places often visited by scooter users. This may result in more scooters being placed in certain neighborhoods, something that enhances the product experience. On the other hand, Felyx can also use this detailed demographic information to identify preferences of places, such as certain shops or government agencies due to private problems. As Allhoff and Henschke (2018) illustrate in examples, seemingly innocuous information (such as scooter drop-off spots, spending at a shop) can lead to an exposure of extremely personal and sensitive information that may not be desired by the user. 
Another ethical concern regarding privacy is the fact that Felyx aims to use tailored advertising based on gender, age and income. The big question remains how they will obtain this information, especially income. 
Although safeguarding privacy partly falls under the responsibility of Felyx users for taken sufficient measures to ensure their privacy, part of the responsibility also belongs to companies by offering clear and well-established privacy guidelines. Given that users‚Äô privacy depends on their informed consent, as explained above users may not always realize what precisely they consent to. This implies that although in official legal terms their privacy may not be violated (though here we may wonder whether the informed consent lives up to GDPR standards), their privacy on a more moral level arguably is violated since we cannot reasonably expect users to have fully read the terms and agreements.
Information security: It seems that Felyx obtains user location data via 4G channels using the cloud, which is often not highly secured. The smartphones could possibly be in contact with remote and opaque data receivers, as Allhoff and Henschke (2018) also discuss in their research.To make this system more secure, the costs will also increase, something that is not desirable from a business perspective. Apart from that, the Felyx app does use a password to log in. However, this is set to default and is often not changed, making it easy to hack the users‚Äô profile. Adding more comprehensive security features decreases its smooth use, which is not desirable either. Overall, it remains a difficult ethics concern.
Physical safety:
Physical security in IoT, according to Allhoff and Henschke (2018), is '...being able to reason about the behaviour of IoT devices, especially actuators, and being able to detect and prevent unintended or unexpected behaviour' . To unlock the Felyx scooter, users scan a barcode using their phones. This is possible at all times of the day except between 1 and 6 at night, which means that the scooters are always locked at night. This is most likely set up to prevent unintended behavior: At night, there is a higher chance people are under the influence of alcohol, which could cause more scooter accidents. In addition, by preventing access at night there is also less risk of accidents caused by impeded vision due to darkness. Apart from the fact that this implementation limits Felyx to a lot of damage, the physical safety of the user is guaranteed to a certain extent by making the scooters inactive at times of higher risk of accidents. This system should also be implemented in the new e-scooters to ensure physical safety. 
Another safety aspect is the addition of a mobile phone holder to the Felyx scooter. It can be assumed that many road users use their mobile phones for navigation. By offering a mobile phone holder, it prevents dangerous driving behavior. In addition, a helmet is available with the felyx scooter to encourage users to wear it, which is legally required. However, there is no monitoring whether it is actually used. A user can therefore ride without a helmet, which endangers the physical safety of the user. A system that the scooter can only be used when the helmet is being worn would better guarantee the physical safety of the user. Or that you actively encourage the use of helmets by automatically opening the helmet-box when the scooter is activated. In addition, Felyx also wants to provide e-scooters that are not equipped with a helmet, mobile holder, mirrors, etc. Research shows that these aspects are an increased cause for accidents and that users often fail to understand the dangers associated with e-scooters (Warnick, 2019). So many physical safety aspects are not guaranteed with these new e-scooters. 
Furthermore, physical safety can be understood in public terms as well. Shared scooters create a lower sense of ownership. This can be expressed for instance by not reporting a malfunctioning scooter. If this is overlooked by Felyx management, or if they do not repair the scooter in due time, this may lead to unsafe use. In addition, a reduced sense in ownership could lead to more rule breaking that can place other people in danger, such as riding on the pavements.
Trust:
Immediately after a ride, the user can see how many minutes it has taken and therefore how much it costs (30 cents per minute). After an interview with three Felyx users, it emerged that they consider the costs to be fair and manageable. However, the amount is only debited from your bank account at the end of the month which can lead to the user having a distorted view of their spending and you possibly spending more than you really want to. 
People require trust in the system: that however long the ride for is indeed measured correctly. Efficiency/accuracy hence contribute to a user‚Äôs trust in Felyx‚Äô services. This relates to privacy concerns, as it requires Felyx to narrowly follow the user‚Äôs movements. It hence seems that a common value trade-off between privacy and efficiency/accuracy occurs here as well: less privacy improves accuracy which is necessary for users‚Äô trust.
Sustainability:
Felyx claims to offer sustainable transport in cities for short trips. If you compare it to the cars, the electric scooter from Felyx is probably more sustainable since it does not emit diesel or petrol. The question remains whether Felyx will reduce car usage or is essentially competing with bicycles. If we compare it with bicycles, Felyx is much less sustainable because of its energy consumption.Besides, a scooter does not contribute to a better health of the user: one gets less exercise with a scooter compared to a bicycle. On the other hand, it does ensure that the user breathes in fewer emissions. 
Also, the materials of an electric scooter are not sustainable. The Felyx contains a 2.5 pound lithium-ion battery that is shipped to Europe from the Chinese factory NIU (Deleger, 2020). Lithium batteries are very polluting to the environment and shipping them costs a lot of CO2 emissions (Deleger, 2020). In order to transport as many people as by car, many scooters are needed and therefore also many lithium batteries.
Another interesting aspect is that a scientific study by Moreau et al. (2020) shows that shared scooters have a shorter lifetime than private scooters. This is because more damage is done with shared scooters: people feel less responsible for the scooters and it is difficult to trace small damages back to the right person.
Other:
An interesting ethical dilemma is who is responsible in case of an accident with a Felyx scooter. Felyx holds the user fully responsible. However, it is difficult to prove whether the Felyx scooter contributed to the accident. After all, the Felyx scooter may have had some defects that could have contributed to the accident. 
It can also be difficult to determine who damaged the scooter. For instance, passers-by may cause damage to the scooters and the last user may be held responsible by Felyx. There is little evidence to prove that this user is not accountable. The possibility of taking a photo of the scooter after use could solve this. However, the question remains to what extent the damage can be properly detected.

Name: P62
Informed consent: The solution of having a smart camera to determine of roommates are at home has a major problem with Informed consent. The owners of the product are not the only users, even the guests of the people living in the house are not explicitly informed that they are filmed, and their face is processed by a face recognition program. This group of users could notice the placed cameras. For people in the public space this becomes even worse, they are not even aware of the fact that a camera is taking images of them and use it for processing.
Privacy: Depending on the system this video stream can be viewed live from all over the world. When the system only returns if the person is home or not it is already it is already a more privacy friendly option as the data to what someone is doing in your house can be watched. This information is sent all over the world to where the physical servers of the cloud services are located. It is possible to do this all locally, however the average user does not have the knowledge to set up a system of their own.
Information security:
In created prototype the processing is done on the device itself, this has the benefit of not having to connect to the internet to check if a roommate is home. This does mean that the speed and accuracy of the processing is lower as the chip in the camera is not powerful enough to check a lot of high-resolution frames per second. A cloud-based solution has the potential to do this, quicker however this does mean that the video must be sent elsewhere
. 
When the product is using the lower bandwidth Zigbee to communicate the present values this camera cannot be watched by others, only if a roommate is present. The user should have the option to turn the camera stream on or off that uses Wi-Fi or wired internet connections. This camera feed must be encrypted so that only the user themselves can view what is happening in their homes.
Physical safety: A smart home system cannot hurt the user directly as the actuators are not strong enough to bring damage to the user. A more efficient life however can decrease the health of the user by doing a lot of tasks for them. The user can control all the houses functions from their lazy chair. Walking to the fridge to check the supplies or to switch on the light gives you a bit of exercise. Especially when sitting for a long time in the same posture these small breaks help to prevent RSI (Repetitive strain Injuries) symptoms.
Trust:
When the smart system is controlling your house, the place where you are feeling safe the products must be trustworthy. A few examples of situations that should or should not be happening with the use of the smart products:  Smart locks should not open by others than you and your room mates, Lights should not fail when there is an internet outage and central heating should not fail when the power is out. 

Leaving this up to smarter product that must be connected to work is a challenge and the producers of these products should keep in mind that users are thrusting them to take over a part of their lives.
Sustainability:
Adding more functionalities and smartness to products does take its toll on the environment. The production of a smart product requires more electronics, and the cloud computing requires more energy than walking to your light switch and turning it on. 

On the other side the smart homes can manage devices to have more efficient and positive impact. For example, turning on the laundry machine at night when energy demand and prices are low without the users input. Other: Liability always presents itself as an ethical concern when using artificial intelligence. Name: P63
Informed consent:
A challenging part regarding the use of the QR code, and specifically identifying the user, is what the user gives informed consent for. In order for the system to work, the users will need to allow the test-result/vaccination status to be linked to your personal identity. If the user disagrees with linking this information, then they will be prohibited from travelling.

Therefore there is a dilemma, should people who do not give consent still be able to travel with a verified document regarding their status? Or is it all centralised through the QR code?
Privacy: Some might say there is no harm with collecting information about the status of the person, but a generation ago still remembers how the holocaust used good Dutch track-records to find and identify Jews (and other minorities) in the Netherlands. Taking this thought to the extreme, this data can be maliciously used by people who are anti or pro vaccination. For that reason the data contains very sensitive data that should be protected.
Information security: As the data is sensitive, high levels of data encryption is necessary. In todays age, intercepting and infiltrating systems is a common problem that will likely be attempted with a system like this: think about it, you haven't been vaccinated and don't want to get tested but you still want to travel and you're good with computers... As a precaution, the data should be encrypted with AES. RSA, or another form of encryption (experts will know what is best) to ensure only eligible people can acces the data.
Physical safety: The system itself will not harm people directly. On the contrary, the effectiveness of vaccines, negative tests, recovery are not reliable/known enough. Is allowing 1 million people where 10% may have a false negative/still sick despite vaccination and where 1% of these people might be sick and infectious (based on number of people infectious in the Netherlands 17 May: RIVM, 2021) safe? In that case 1000 people may be infectious and travelling. People might have a false sense of safety leading to new outbreaks with different variants in the future, leading to more deaths and health complications.
Trust:
In the case of travellers, it is to be expected that border control is rigid and can be trusted. Yet there might be very few cases where inappropriate control will allow people without a certificate to travel anyways.

When looking at using a system like this for events like a soccer match or a bar, then controlling is in the hands of the stadium or the bar owner. Especially in the case of the bar owner, it is in their interest to have as many clients as possible and may therefore control loosely or not at all. In this case private organisations with a personal interest/motive not to check should not use this system.
Sustainability: More data, more storage and more data centres is consuming an increasing amount of energy. Should we really keep developing more digital systems or should there be a cap? The benefits in this case outweigh the impact as it may save many lives and allow the economy to open. However, building these data centres will challenge our goals of reaching energy neutrality and reducing use of rare materials. Our increasing dependance will mean a cycle of building more and using more.
Other:
There are several other ethical implications that I will discuss in more detail during my presentation:

Privileging fortunate people who are able to get a vaccine as opposed to unprivileged people.

Penalizing religious or philosophical objections to vaccinations.

Governments will restrict physical access to essential settings such as workplaces, schools, and health care institutions, if this system is implemented in day to day life. Essentially this will indirectly force people to get a vaccination.

Name: P66
Informed consent:
For the sake of this discussion, I am assuming that the user is not hindered by any mental disabilities. In the final section, regarding other ethics concerns, this topic is briefly addresses separately. For now, it is assumed that the wheelchair is used by someone solely coping with physical disabilities.

When starting this assignment, I set out a goal, stating that I wanted to focus on meeting all requirements in a non-invasive way. This brings a lot of good things, as it does not hinder the user in the day-to-day activities. Additionally, people with disabilities do not want to stand out, they want to have a life that is as normal as possible, which also fully aligns with the vision set out by Dr. Per Uddén. 

However, this does come at a cost: the user might not even be aware of the measurement and tracking capabilities that the wheelchair is equipped with, as they are non-invasive. Hence, it is argued that these capabilities are not enabled when the wheelchair is first begin put to use. When the user gets to use the wheelchair for the first time and links the app to the wheelchair, the app will show instructions on how to use the wheelchair. Additionally, it will show the user which features the wheelchair has, how and what it measures and what happens with this data. Note that it shows the user these features, to prevent the user from skipping long terms of conditions texts. Once the user is aware of these features, he or she can determine to keep them all off, turn them all on, or to select a few specific features to be used. Of course, these settings can be changed in the app later. These steps ensure that the user is aware of what is being measured, and consents to this happening.
Privacy:
The topic of privacy might be one of the most difficult topics regarding this smart wheelchair. As the wheelchair is literally an extension of the person‚Äôs body, it is highly personal, and as such all collected data is too. During the design of the IoT architecture, it was emphasised that exact numbers, for example on heart rates, or accelerations, were not required, only an order of magnitude is sufficient to fulfil the requirements. Nevertheless, one could derive quite some information from the user based on this data, for example the combination of heart rate, respiratory rate, wheel rotations, and accelerometer data would be sufficient to fairly accurate predict if the user is resting and/or sleeping, or not, interesting data for people with bad intentions. Additionally, some of the data collected should be visible for the user and his caretakers, but not for the company. Permobil already gathers data on for example the battery status to perform fleet management, but does absolutely not need data on the patient‚Äôs health. 

A solution could be to only store health data locally, and communicate this to the user‚Äôs phone or to the GSM module in case of emergency. This would however not allow to either assess the user‚Äôs health remotely (for example for remote caretakers) or to run remote machine learning models, something that is often deemed desirable over running them locally due to computational constraints. Hence, the only solution for this is for Permobil to set high ethical standards, being critical in which data is stored and communicated where and to whom, to ensure the privacy of the user is protected.
Information security:
In the designed architecture, three communication streams are envisioned, as well as three data storage locations. These will be discussed separately.

The communications streams are the Bluetooth connection between the wheelchair and the user‚Äôs phone, the GSM connection to external caretakers in case of emergency, and the internet connection between the user‚Äôs phone and the Permobil servers to transmit data back and forth. The Bluetooth connection is relatively safe, as it is local and set up using the app, after which is not easy to access it remotely. The GSM connection is also quite safe, as it is only used rarely and does not provide any specific information apart from an alert call to caretakers. The connection between the user‚Äôs phone and the Permobil servers is expected to be the weak spot in the communication streams, as it is remotely accessible for persons with bad intentions. Many things need to be in order for a safe connection, e.g. the user‚Äôs home network‚Äôs Wifi password, but the data also needs to be encrypted, and Permobil needs to take care of their security as well, which leads us to the second topic: data storage.

The data stored at Permobil is probably most interesting for this discussion. Large databases are interesting for people with bad intentions, and it would not be the first time the news headlines mention that a large collection of personal information is leaked. Hence, it would be of importance for Permobil to store the lowest amount of data possible, and to do this safely. The data storage on the phone can be very minimal as well, as it can request the data from the wheelchair or servers whenever desired. The data stored on the wheelchair itself is probably safest, as people would need to physically access the storage device, which is likely to be hidden deep in the wheelchair itself. It is quite unlikely this data will ever be accessed by anyone not allowed to do so.
Physical safety: A major upside of the intended system is that it does not actuate the wheelchair, meaning that the data is only used for monitoring purposes, not for actuation. Even if the entire system were to crash, read false values, or fail in any other way, the user can never be harmed by this system. The only possible actuation is where the system turns off the motors in when the accelerometer detects a crash, major impact, or toppling. To ensure that the user will not get stuck at a dangerous location, such as a railroad crossing, a manual override button is added, which allows the user to suppress the emergency detection and reactive the wheelchair‚Äôs motors.
Trust: For the user to trust the added features of the wheelchair, it is of importance that it functions properly and that all other aspects of ethics in IoT are addressed well. When, for example, the implications of this system on privacy are a concern to the user, he will most likely not trust the system. Regarding the functioning of the system, it is of key importance to prevent false negatives and positives. When for example the user topples in a remote location, he expects the wheelchair to automatically trigger the alarm function and inform caretakers. If this does not happen, the user might be stuck for hours, waiting for someone to find him. This would be considered a false negative, as the system indicates the user is not in an emergency, while he actually is. One can imagine that only one such event is sufficient to complete destroy the user‚Äôs trust in the system. Additionally, false positives could also reduce the user‚Äôs trust in the system. When the wheelchair triggers an emergency, while the user actually is fine, it might be considered embarrassing when caretakers come rushing in. Also, if this happens a few times, caretakers will start ignoring the alerts, which could be catastrophic in case of a real emergency. Hence, ensuring that the systems triggers an emergency at the right moments is crucial to develop trust in the system.
Sustainability: The proposed additions to the system are marginal compared to the already existing wheelchair. The current products contain lots of plastics, batteries, rubbers for the tires and seats. Hence, the sustainability impact of the introduced architecture is neglectable compared to the other elements of this product. If sustainability would be a focus point of Permobil, they would be better of focussing on improving other areas, over the minor impact of a few added sensors and the electricity used by the system.
Other: As mentioned in te first concern, it was assumed that the user only faces physical disabilities, meaning that mentally he is well aware to properly evaluate the system and consent to using the newly introduced features. However, it is not unlikely that people using these systems might have mixed disabilities, meaning they are also coping with mental disabilities. In these cases, special attention should be paid to the topic of ethics, as it will be more difficult to ensure that the user is aware of what the system is doing. Most likely, this would require the legally assigned representative of the user to assess the system and to consent to using its features.

Name: P68
Informed consent: Informed consent is important because it makes sure the users have control over their personal information or data, which gives them autonomy. In a smart wearable device with the main purpose of monitoring the health and training status of an individual such as the ‚ÄòNike Gym Shirt‚Äô, the user would expect the device to measure biological data. However, at the same time, the biometrics of an individual is a highly personal type of data, almost equivalent to medical records. Therefore, it is especially important that the user understands what raw biometrics are being measured, how they are measured, how they are being used in the device, where/how they are stored, and what risks there are when using the device. Currently, ‚Äúend user license agreement‚Äù, or EULAs, are the standard solution for informed consent. However, there are many problems with the EULA that questions if it should be the standard for informed consent in the first place. First, EULAs are extensive and often too detailed, which discourages users to read it. Second, there is usually only one option, ‚Äúagree‚Äù, that enables them to continue using the service, rather than triggering a conversation about the benefits and risks. Even if the ‚ÄòNike Gym Shirt‚Äô implements the EULA for cost reasons, it is necessary to add a visual and informative explanation when the mobile app is first launched about the points given earlier, with even in-depth settings that determine which biometric measurements to take and to what extent that data will be shared to the company or will be used to create big data.
Privacy: Like the ethics concern regarding informed consent, privacy is also a relevant issue because IoT devices collect large amounts of user data to enhance the users‚Äô experience. Especially in devices that use machine learning, personal information the user does not want to share can be predicted. For example, with the ‚ÄòNike gym shirt‚Äô, the users‚Äô age can be predicted with the biometric data and the information from the GPS can also suggest where a user lives. These data can be used to help the company get a better understanding of the target group and shape strategic decisions however, this might not be what the user prefers. Even more relevant is the alert about the health issues. Although it is one of the main features of the device, health issues are a highly sensitive topic, and in some situations, the user might want to keep that information only to themselves. Lastly, since the ‚ÄòNike Gym Shirt‚Äô is wearable, it is likely that users where the shirt for the entire day, although they only workout for a few hours. The user‚Äôs privacy can also be invaded if the device keeps on collecting data for the whole day without the user knowing. Even if it is turned off, the user might feel like they are being ‚Äúwatched‚Äù, like the web cameras on laptop computers.
Information security: Every IoT device relies heavily on wireless connections to either another device or the cloud. This means that there are also many opportunities in which the information security can be compromised, and with the information security compromised, both informed consent and privacy can also be compromised. In the ‚ÄòNike Gym Shirt‚Äô, the real-time biometrics are sent directly to the smartphone. In the process, other devices may pick up signals leading to unintentional distribution of data, or a cybercriminal can try to hack in the system and obtain the information without the user knowing. Passwords and encryption technology can be used to minimize such security breaches. It is worth noting that more security means a decrease in seamless connections, which is the main reason IoT is becoming more popular. The minimum information security needs to be determined and there is no doubt that more IoT devices need to be designed to secure information from the start.
Physical safety: Physical safety needs to be considered especially for wearable IoT devices. In the case of the ‚ÄòNike Gym Shirt‚Äô, there are no actuators on the device mainly because the device needs to be small enough to not be in the way of a workout. This substantially reduces the risk of sudden or unintended behavior of actuators. There is however one sensor that can be used as an actuator in the ‚ÄòNike Gym Shirt‚Äô: the electrodes for the EMG. EMG measures the slight change in voltage between the two electrodes to estimate muscle activity. On the other hand, electricity between two electrodes on the body can stimulate muscles. This is called functional electrical stimulation or therapeutic electrical stimulation and it is often used by athletes to loosen their muscles. If electric current accidentally runs through the two electrodes, the user will feel some stimulation and, in some cases, contract muscles against the users‚Äô will. This can easily be prevented by putting diodes in the circuit. The temperature of the device also needs to be checked to make sure the device does not reach high temperatures that can potentially burn the skin.
Trust: The trust of the ‚ÄòNike Gym Shirt‚Äô is relevant to many aspects of ethics that were discussed before. For example, the user trusts the system that it will not let hackers steal the data, and it trusts the company that they will use personal data according to the informed consent. Another aspect of trust is trusting that the main functions work. In the proposed device, the main concern would be the durability of the device. In specific, electronics in general are weak against vibrations and rapid movement, and the wear of the sensor is something that needs to be considered. Finally, the health issue alert will be an important factor regarding trust. For example, if the app alerts the user of a health issue, the patient may take the decision to see the doctor and get checked. If the machine learning algorithm diagnosed the health issue correctly, the user would gain trust for the device. On the other hand, if no health issues were found at the hospital, the users trust for the device will decrease. This would also happen if the algorithm alerted the user of a health issue very often. The users will eventually disregard the algorithms‚Äô advice because they do not trust it, making one of the main features of the device useless.
Sustainability: Since IoT devices exist in the physical world, more resources are needed to make the device. Even in the development stage, prototyping IoT devices will certainly use more resources than prototyping a digital service such as a web app. Data centers, where the cloud servers are also account global greenhouse gas emissions, proving that even digital services have an environmental impact. Also, there are not many regulations or protocols regarding the end of life of flexible sensors embedded in textiles. The recyclability and/or toxicity to the environment of emerging sensors need to be explored. Regarding social sustainability, an indirect but important concern may also be the working environment of the electronic components inside the product.

Name: P69
Informed consent:
At the set-up of the system the user should be prompted with a list of easy to understand points to agree to and their should be a clear explanation how the system collects and stores data.

In theory the manufacturer of the smart home solution itself does not need to collect any sensitive information, but might want to do this to improve product performance and test out new features with OTA updates. In case this is done this could be option a user either accepts or denies and should not compromise the functionality out-of-the box.

Furthermore access to personal data should be locked to personal use in the default set-up, however in case users want to enjoy the benefits from a larger smart home platform like Apple's Homekit a user should te asked to give explicit permission in order to do so.
Privacy:
The data gathered by the smart home is highly personal and hence as private as could be. It should therefore be treated with outmost care.
And although many smart home products use cloud services to offer customers the best connected experience it is questionable what the data is used for exactly. Therefore it is advisable from a privacy point of view to keep the data local (as you be able to own your most personal data literally). Instead of a server hosted in the cloud, where a possiblility of fatal errors may occur (https://www.theverge.com/2021/5/17/22440298/eufy-privacy-glitch-server-error-camera-feed-exposed), the user's data could be processed at a local IoT hub and stored on a local NAS.
In case data is shared with a third party this should happen with anonimized data if possible.
Data captured by sensors such as PIR  should be processed at sensor/device level as much as possible to avoid unnecessary storage of data that could be traced back to an individual. However this could be in contrast to the personalization of the learning algorithm, where you would want devices to respond to people's individual preferences.
Finally their might be concerns at level of a family, does every individual in the family have access to their own personal data for example?
Information security: Less secure wireless internet connections (which could be tapped by hackers and governmental agencies) should be avoided. And the connection to the internet should be limited to the most vital information. If indeed data is shared to a personal phone or a third party application, the data should be shared using a encrypted connection or VPN. Also biometric authentification methods and two factor authentification should be part of the remote monitoring application.
Physical safety: The smart door lock can be a concern as the user might not be recognized and are not allowed into their own home. If power is out, vital functions like the smart door lock might not function if connected to a socket. A potential solution might be to use a battery operated system. Futhtermore if no back-up is made of the data stored on the NAS, the whole system might stop functioning, however there should be a manual override at all times (you could open the door with a physical key for instance). Lastly to ensure malicious parties can not take over control of devices connected to the smart home system it is advisable that the remote access to devices is off by default and access is restricted. This might come at the cost of decreased user friendliness of the application.
Trust:
Trust is mostly based on a companies' image and vision with regards to privacy and their business model. For this assignment I assume that the company is willing to provide users with a more closed system where user data is only shared with permission of the user. In this case the company does not have an incentive to have control over you data other than perhaps to learn how the product is used and test how it could be improved. This last point can also be done with a select group of individuals that give explicit permission to share their personal information with the company. If this message is carried out effectively trust should not be a concern.
Futhermore being aware hat your personal dat is stored physically at your home should invoke trust that you are the one that has control over your own data.
Lastly trust is based on the willingness that a system is supported and kept secure on the long term. By providing this as a promise a company could help shape this more trustworthy image.
Sustainability: The fact that everything has to be automated leads to very complex system where a lot of electronics and connections need to be added to the current dumb home. As producing electronics is energy intensive and the products need to be shipped from the other side of the world the energy cost (and correspong CO2 emmision) might outweigh the benefits of energy saving of the system (on the short term). Furthermore software systems might lose support before they reach their physical lifespan. This provides an incentive to upgrade the system which undeniably leads to more e-waste.
Other: By providing a localized system and not relying on a business model that harvests user data to make a profit, the system will inevitably get more expensive. This could mean once again that privacy is likely not accessible to or attractive to the general public.

Name: P70

Privacy: Who can access the wearer's data? Is it stored anonymously or is it (easily) traceable to the wearer?

Name: P71
Informed consent:
Informed consent means that granting permission in full knowledge of the possible consequences. If you want to make use of the product and/or services you have to agree with the terms of service and the privacy notice.

Research* has shown that less than 90% of people read the terms of service without reading them. So a 15 page privacy notice and a 55 page terms of service would not improve this number. In most cases there can be spoken of consent but not informed consent.

One could argue that consenting to these terms without reading them is the fault of the user, which is a fair point. However, in case of the Ring video doorbell, a product that is similar to that of the smart home assignment, the privacy notice consists of 15 pages, and the terms of service are an additional 55 pages. If one would want to read these documents for every IOT device that gathers data, they'd better have a lot of free time.

Next to the consent of the users themselves we need to take bypassers into account as well. All the people that come near a house that is equipped with a Ring video doorbell are not able to give informed consent to be filmed and have their footage shared. There are multiple examples of Ring doorbell videos of random visitors that went viral**. Without their consent.  

* https://eu.usatoday.com/story/tech/2020/01/28/not-reading-the-small-print-is-privacy-policy-fail/4565274002/ 

** https://slate.com/technology/2019/12/ring-smart-doorbell-camera-sign-consent.html
Privacy:
Smart homes generate highly sensitive and heterogeneous data of users most intimate and private moments. This resulted in privacy concerns. These concerns mostly range from the ability of the systems that use the smart home sensors as input, to effectively predict the actions of individuals. Another issue is the possibility of hackers to penetrate through Smart-Homes systems and retrieve confidential information relating to individuals. 94% Of Dutch people indicate that they have concerns about their privacy.* One of the major fears is that of identity fraud. If hackers somehow make use of facial data from the doorbell, they could use this for identity theft.

https://autoriteitpersoonsgegevens.nl/nl/nieuws/nederland-maakt-zich-zorgen-over-privacy
Information security:
The first concern is that legal regulation is slow and, thus, unable to keep up with the rapid developments of information technology. Legal solutions are usually at least one step behind technological developments. Data privacy by electronic means should, therefore, be based not only on traditional jurisdiction, but also on soft law, i.e., self-binding policies such as the existing data privacy principles. 

The second problem of data protection has to do with the fact that these regulations are not internationally harmonized, causing severe complications (especially between the United States and the European Union) on a cross-border basis, which is the rule rather than the exception in modern business.
Physical safety:
Sensitive data could be leaked, could have physical consequences. Burglary when facial recognition fails. Systems are often designed for one person with control and password for the services. This can be used to monitor family members when they do not realize it. An example of this is a man who has been found guilty on monitoring his wife with a wall mount smart thermostat. When he heard his wife say she did no longer love him, he drove over to the house and a fight broke out. The scale of tech abuse is largely unknown. In the UK, the women‚Äôs charity Refuge reported 920 cases of tech-related abuse from only January to August 2019. But that‚Äôs only one charity.

https://www.bbc.com/future/article/20200511-how-smart-home-devices-are-being-used-for-domestic-abuse
Trust:
Transparency in software design, insight in the stored information and the handling of this information can increase the chance of spotting biases and flaws in the software. Open source software would be perfect for this, however, this will not work in private organizations because of the IP protection. This is especially significant when a data collection process is established by the software and trust is required on behalf of the customer that no personal or sensitive data will be exposed. This is mostly a one way relationship since the customer has no leverage with the tech companies other than switching to another supplier. Luckily with the coming of GDPR, customers get the right to be forgotten and they get more power over their data.

https://www.project-sherpa.eu/the-internet-of-things-and-ethics/
Sustainability:
The smart home provides numerous ways to make the home more ecofriendly. The most important example being energy management. *‚ÄúWhile Ecobee and Nest, pioneers of the connected thermostat, claim their products can save up to 23% and10-15% respectively, you‚Äôre probably not saving that much. Most research puts the figures somewhere between 1% and 15%, and in a few cases energy consumption can actually go up‚Äù. It mostly depends on how the smart system is used. If you install a smart thermostat and don‚Äôt understand how the features should be used, you‚Äôre not likely to save a lot of energy. Next to this, there is little data available on the impact of using energy demanding products during solar peaks for example. 
https://www.the-ambient.com/features/do-smart-homes-save-energy-1232

Name: P72
Informed consent:
App should asks informed consent for the acquisition, retention, and sharing of personal data. BUT: Smart shirt only works together with the app, this comes with terms of services. If the user does not agree with the informed consent, he cannot use the service. 

The ‚Äúend user license agreement‚Äù (EULA) should be contextual and flexible, meaning even if the user changes his/her mind, they should always have the possibility to make changes in their consent. 

In general, the informed consent should be designed for easy accessibility and really make the user understand what they are signing. Not just saying we collect data, but explain what it means and what happens to that data.
Privacy:
Privacy in Europa is regulated by the GDPR. Therefore the system should have an updated privacy policy. In the policy it should be described how they collect and use health-related data. 

The system should nly collect relevant data (not registered adress e.g.). For example:  the app should only record GPS if user agrees. 

Do not share personal data with other companies or organizations without legitimate interest (However, what counts as a legitimate interest feels open to interpretation)!

Do notshare or sell your data for marketing purposes!

 De-identification of the data is not 100% sure because even encrypted data can be personalized again.

Downside of the system: phone's Bluetooth needs to be on all the time

Downside: System requires huge number of sensors and communications between devices and gathers big amounts of personal information to enable the desired service. That information can compromise on an individual's privacy
Information security:
Information security plays a key role to ensure privacy, informed consent, physical safety and trust. 

There are possible threads like hartware attacks, or distributed denial of service (DDoS) attacks. This is especially endangered if users use too easy passwords and do not change them regularely. 

Even if data is encrypted, metadata can nevertheless reveal pertinent details.

Smartphones are potentially in communication with remote and opaque data receivers. 

Data could be sent to producer's servers for analysis, without clear or obvious consent from the users.

Providing more information security is costly and often not considered. 

High information security also compromises effective and easy use.
Physical safety:
Tracking of location is dangerous, especially for famous people.  

The private adress or work adress can easily be found out. 

Every human being is different. If someone has already a disease or special medical conditions the shirt might not be accurate or even recommended. The app should encourage people to always double check with their doctor if sports and what kind of sports is possible. 

For some measurements, e.g. measurement of glucose in blood it is possible to do it on your own. However, these methods are intrusive and if people are not familiar with the procedure it is safer to let it be done by a Doctor.
Trust:
To build trust in the IoT system it should work seamlessly and safely. That means that individual components should work reliably, the networks of components and operators run as promised and the user feels comfortable that the overall system is providing the desired results.

Trust can be endangered if services in the past did not work out as expected or the company does not show motivation for the users best interest. 

There should also not be too much trust in a health device since there can always be errors and a doctor's advice should always be considered more important.
Sustainability:
The Nike Gym Shirt IoT system produces a lot of data which needs to be processed by servers. 

On top of that electronic waste is produced.

Name: P74
Informed consent: An important concern for informed consent is that it is difficult to establish where/when the user should consent. Is it when booking online? Or even before entering the building. When you enter the building the system will turn on and possibly record you. A way to inform the customer and ask for their consent is when they book the hotel. The hotel must be clear during booking that the customers will be recorded when they enter. This adds the problem that customers who want to stay at this hotel cannot book at the hotel itself because this would mean that they already will be filmed without consent when entering the building. Bookings for the hotel can be done online to omit this problem, with a clear message that the customers will be recorded at the entrance of the hotel. An example of a clear message could be a picture with some explanation or a short text. This info should not be too long because otherwise the customer will ignore it and simply click yes. Another option is to ask for consent at the door, this will cost extra time for the customer and cost an extra employee. Another problem arises when a customer brings a friend into the hotel, the friend cannot give the informed consent to being recorded since it did not book the hotel. The same goes for when people book rooms for more than one person. Usually one person is in charge of the booking and his/her name will show up on the booking number. But how do you make sure that the other people who share the room also give their consent? This could be omitted again by asking for consent when entering the hotel.
Privacy:
The privacy of the users is being protected by using sensors such as an infrared camera and a microphone. The users are only being recorded when dangerous words are recognized. The part of the conversation of the user is saved to the cloud and used to train the machine learning model. This can invade privacy when the customers do not know that their data is being used for training a model. Therefore, it is important to also notify this to the customers when they book the hotel.
The camera and the microphone are always on when motion is detected, this can still invade the privacy of the customers because they feel like they are being watched. The infrared camera minimizes the invasion of privacy but still is not 100% privacy proof, because you can still get an general shape of the customer. Voices can also be recognized and some parts of the conversations are also heard. This invades the privacy of the customers when they are talking in the entrance hall. This can be omitted by placing signs in the entrance hall that there are cameras and microphones, it is important to communicate to the customers what data of them is being used and for what purpose. This way intrusion can be minimized. It is also important that the customer can access the data if he/she wants to. Since the data is made anonymous, it can be difficult for customers to access the data that is saved of them.
Information security: The possible dangerous moments identified by the system are send to the cloud to be send to the operator. This means that the information is stored, since the data is also used to further train the machine. It is therefore important that the data is anonymously stored. So no names or identification numbers will be stored with it. Only a timestamp and date. Besides making the data as anonymous as possible, it is logical that the hotel stores the data on her own servers to keep the data of her customers save. For the hotel to gain trust it is also good practice to not sell the data, most of the revenue of the hotel is from customers booking a room. Another concern is data safety. By saving the data on hotel servers it is also important to design a good security system so the hotel can withstand hackers and attacks. A good security system is only half of the job, it is also important to keep this system updated and working. Therefore the hotel should hire an expert on this to maintain this. A concern with this will be that it can cost extra money, but if the system works it will save money in the end because they will need less employees in the long run. As mentioned in the privacy part, it is also important for the hotel to be transparent about what data they will collect and use to their customers.
Physical safety: There is not a lot of physical intrusion with this design, there is only a camera and a microphone so the customer will notice that the product but not actually touch it. The operator only has to respond to notifications of the system. The lock system is not completely part of the designed system. There is only checked if the lock is not forced/broken into to keep robbers out. A problem that can occur is if the card/key does not fit nice the customer may need some force to get it in. This can set off alarms and scare the customer or lock the customer out of the hotel. These are not life threatening problems, on the other hand they are a big inconvenience for the customer.
Trust: The users will only be recorded when dangerous words are spoken and detected. Since a machine will pick these, the context is not always considered. Therefore the system In the beginning might not be very trustful to use for the operator. Since a machine learning algorithm is added, the system will become more reliable over time. This will cost the operator in the beginning more time because he has to label the data. In the end this makes sure that the machine does not ring the alarm at a wrong time if it would work without an operator. The operator is the extra check. Another aspect of trust is that the hotel is really clear about the data collection and usage of the customers data. If this is not clear, the customer may think that the hotel does not have the best interests at hand.
Sustainability: The system does not make use of a lot of components, and since everyone has got a phone, tablet or pc no extra screens need to be bought for this design. This saves materials. Another concern is the use of precious metals since pcb‚Äôs are needed. It is best to use recycled metals but that is not always profitable for the company that sells these sensors and machines. The hotel could only get eco-friendly sensors and modules but this may not be cost-effective. Another big concern is if the hotel wants to go for a very green(more expensive) solution or for a cheap solution.
