Name: P76
Informed consent: The data that is collected is quite personal, since it is medical information. It has to be clearly communicated to the user what data is collected, and what will happen with it. When installing the Permobil App or when buying the Permobil connected wheelchair, users should be presented a list with all data that is collected, and what will happen with it, and they need to sign it before they can use this app.
Privacy: For this case it is very important that the data is encrypted. Medical information is always very personal, and we should make sure that this data can only be seen by the people who are allowed. The question is, What sorts of information becomes available for whom? Does the doctor need to constantly know the heartrate of the wheelchair user? Or what if you often see a high glucose level? Will caregivers and doctors make assumptions that this person is eating a lot of sugar? It is important to think about which information is crucial to share. In this case, for the safety of the wheelchair user, it is crucial to send a warning if there is something wrong with the heart rate. But the caregiver does not need to know all the other data that is collected. He/she only needs to know when the wheelchair user is in danger, so that he/she can come for help.
Information security: The Permobil app uses GPS only in cases of emergency, so that caregivers can see where they have to go. But it should be prevented that the GPS can also be used at other moments. Then criminals could maybe find out the adress of the wheelchair user. It is just very important that all information is encrypted, and cannot be hacked by someone. Not being able to walk makes people vulnerable, so you don't want criminals to know where these wheelchair users live.
Physical safety: In this case, there are no physical actuators that can harm the user. The only actuators are warnings on the phone of the user, the caregiver, or the doctors office. The warning could physically harm people if it is too loud. If it is too loud or too surprising, people could maybe be startled by it. In case of the wheelchair users, when they do have a high risk for a heart disease, maybe the loud noise can even cause a heart attack! But I think this chance is very small. Another issue with physical safety is that I included devices in the wheelchair where people can do their own bloodtests. People should know how to do this properly, because if they use it in the wrong way, things could become dangerous.
Trust: Trust is very important in this case, since the health monitoring algorythm can give you a diagnosis that is quite impactful on your life. Telling someone that they have a high risk on a heart disease is not nothing. Therefore the algorhythm should work very well, and should work more accurate than it is currently. You should also always warn people that the product is not 100% accurate, but this can also make people trust the product less. When people do not trust the product enough, people could ignore the diagnosis and be in danger. Also people should be able to trust that caregivers and doctors are warned on time! Because if they cannot trust on that, they will always be worried.
Sustainability: Regarding sustainability, it is important that all different monitors/sensors that are added to the wheelchair, can be replaced separately. It would be a pity if one sensor is broken, and that you need to replace everything to make it work again. Another concern is that this device needs to be constantly connected, which means that it is always turned on and uses quite some energy. More energy than a not-connected wheelchair.

Name: P77
Informed consent:
The application uses Bluetooth on the mobile phone of the user to connect to others in the environment. Apart from the enormous hazard of sharing medical data and files, the application can also be used to track the location of a user, track regular contact-people or points and other habits.
Firstly, to verify the authenticity of the application it will need various personal data such as name, birth, citizen service number and a picture. Furthermore, the app will track the covid-tests of the user and the result of these tests will be provided to other app-users in their immediate surroundings and a general platform used to predict the covid-trends for the following week.
Although the app will generally be installed by the user with the idea of sharing their data on covid-tests and vaccination, the consent asked by the application will have to very clear on precisely what information will be shared and with who. Any differences or personal settings of what will be shared could be implemented but would greatly influence the results of studies (prove them unrealistic).
Privacy: Mentioned in the Informed consent is that the app will collect and share data on the users‚Äô vaccination (yes/no), their covid-19 tests (positive/negative, date) and share this data with surrounding people also using the application. Privacy concerns with the use of this application is that the user can be targeted for certain status-happenings. Perhaps someone who is not yet vaccinated get targeted with more pro-vaccine messages. Or someone tested positive for Covid-19 suddenly receives medicine adverts alerting others of their status (think of the pregnant girl in the target-incident). If the app would for instance show the log of contacted people or visited locations it could show sensitive information such as someone having an affair or a secret meeting/location regarding national safety.
Information security:
Closely connected to the privacy concerns regarding meetings or contact with people is the information security. The application is installed on a phone and can be accessed as such. One might question whether the security of a phone (often a 4-number code of Face-ID) is enough to keep the privacy of the application. Only close family or friends might have the means to access someone‚Äôs phone. However, this again gives rise to the issue with tracking contacted people or frequent locations. Furthermore, in relation to other applications on the phone it is often unclear to users what information is allowed on which application which could allow the information of the user and other users to be leaked.
If the status of the user would be shared with 3rd party organisations to gain access to a country or to participate in an event the channels among which the information is communicated would require enough security as to not leak the personal information of the users.
The sensor will have to be quite precises to be able to determine people in the surroundings of the user in a radius of 10m.
Physical safety:
Because the application does not directly influence the Physical World, there are no concerns regarding physical safety. Only if the application were for instance to develop such that it would allow vaccinated users to influence the physical world (for instance open doors) while unvaccinated people are not allowed physical safety might be affected. (someone tested positive for covid-19 not being able to open their door because they have to stay home but there is a fire)
Concerning discrimination regarding the shared information (see other ethical concerns) physical safety could be compromised by a human acting on the information provided by the application.
Trust:
The users of the application will have to trust that the other users will not use the provided information for any actions that are not in their best interest. Trust that not more information that intended is shared with other users. Trust that the communication channels to 3rd parties is secure and that third parties have the best interest of the users in mind.
Another aspect of the application is that it takes away the need to trust another person in the immediate surroundings of the user. The information shared by the application can not be adapted and/or removed and might prove more trustworthy than another person telling them or having to trust the other person takes al laws and regulations in account.
Sustainability: Because the phone acts as a certificate and can be used to track contacts, it will have to be on the user at all times. Furthermore, Bluetooth needs to be active at all times. This will require a larger amount of battery than usual for the mobile phone and therefore more energy. Because the application uses regular mobile phones there are no other sustainability concerns.
Other: Showing the status of the user or for instance their choice on the vaccine might be cause for mild to heavy discrimination or even physical safety.

Name: P78
Informed consent: Due to overly detailed and lengthy End User License Agreement (EULA) users generally do not read it though, therefore the representation of Informed consent established by this agreement is arguably not ethically correct. This leads to private information of users being unknowingly collected and processed.
Privacy: IoT products are capable of collecting a huge amount of private data. While this might be useful for manufacturers to learn about consumers‚Äô behaviour and reach target audience more accurately, it also can very easily breach user privacy. Users are often not aware what type of information about them is being shared and analysed, and have very little control on securing their digital privacy. Shared e-scooter IoT system would have opportunity to collect information about usage behaviour such as ride frequency, specific times, locations.
Information security: Where there is a flow of information from one device to another, there is a possibility that it might be intercepted and retrieved by the wrong hands. The most sensitive data In the case of shared e-scooter IoT system is personal data of users, linked to their account in the app ‚Äì username, name, surname, gender, age, email address or even personal number. And since it is possible to pay for the renting service through the app, the account might be linked to the credit card information. The service providers are responsible that their user account information which is logged and saved in their servers would be kept securely, as well as during any stage of device-to-device communication. One of the possible ways to do that is by using hashed databases and secure communication protocols. Also, on their end, the users are able to minimize the possible damage by not using the same password many different accounts. Otherwise, in the case of data leak, cyber-criminals would have easy access to hack into other accounts like emails, social media and other services.
Physical safety: Failing to operate as intended, influenced by some overlooked factor, the IoT system may impose a risk for physical safety. E-scooter sharing system provides transport for the users, which participate in traffic and has to abide traffic rules. During the drive, users are responsible for other and their own safety. Felyx company, from their side, hold the liability for making sure the vehicles, in this case scooters, are safe to operate on the roads from the technical aspect, including the safety risks due to the flaw in the IoT system. Although Felyx could not be completely responsible for the actions of their users like intentional traffic rule violation or mistakes caused by the lack of skills, some safety implementations for risk prevention need to be done. Such as verifying whether the user has the appropriate age to drive an e-scooter, limiting the speed in caution areas, or recognizing whether the user might be intoxicated.
Trust: It is crucial to maintain a high user trust for IoT system, since trust determines if the user will want to keep using the system. In the context of e-scooter sharing system, the user trust will be positively influenced if he finds the system efficient, convenient and would not see any issues regarding privacy and safety ‚Äì both physical and in the cyberspace. For instance, the user can be satisfied that there are a lot of available scooters nearby available to rent at any time, which are constantly maintained with full battery charge, the renting process is effortless, and clear information is given about the reason and use of data collection and processing.
Sustainability:
Generally, growing number of IoT devices means larger amount of e-waste at their end of life, which is difficult to recycle and a large portion of used materials ending up in the landfills. But implementing a shared electric scooter option for the urban transport arguably has a positive overall impact regarding sustainability. The convenience for accessibility and mobility, can attract more car users to choose this far more sustainable alternative. The most problematic part in the electric scooter, from a sustainability point of view, is the battery, having relatively short lifespan, and recycling of li-on battery is still not a common practice.
But even compared to the current Felix e-mopeds, the next generation of scooters are considerably smaller, therefore require a lot less electric energy for the same distance, less power and less massive batteries. Also, the company currently source of energy to charge the batteries from sustainable source ‚Äì windmills. 
One of the concerns is that non-motorised transport e.g., bicycle, is still much more sustainable than e-scooter. The image of green, emission free transport might seem harmless enough and may be chosen over bicycle by people who don‚Äôt enjoy a little bit of exercise and a drop of sweat.
Other: The examples of electric scooter sharing system integration in the transport of many different cities in Europe show the same tendency ‚Äì other participants of the traffic including pedestrians tend to get annoyance towards this particular mean of transport or feel more at risk, to get in an accident with it. Scooter drivers move fast compared to pedestrians, and perform reckless manoeuvres to get in front. Even if the accidents and injuries might not occur be very often, it threatens the comfort of others. When the number of scooters increases their individual rapid movement and general lack of awareness can make the mobility chaotic. In places where scooters have gotten banned to be ridden on pavements, car drivers have difficulties sharing the same road. So, the change infrastructure or some additional traffic control implementations have to be considered when a large number of e-scooters are introduced to the urban transport, since the current situation can already be stress inducing and requires a lot of attention.

Name: P82
Informed consent:
Personally, I think that one of the main problems regarding informed consent in software currently is that people see it rather as an obstacle in their way to glory, which is installing the product or application. As a user you just want to install things and get it to work as soon as possible. If a 3rd party would install the system this would be even more challenging since it would be likely that this 3rd party would then usually need to accept all conditions before the system can be set up appropriately. 
A second factor that is of importance is the amount of information that is being displayed to the user, which is either a very long text written in a complex way, causing that a large part of the population won‚Äôt be able to understand what it actually says, or it has been adapted so that everyone should be able to understand it, but then it‚Äôs often oversimplified and it doesn‚Äôt show the full truth.
To overcome these problems, it might be good to make a short video about what data is being collected, why it is collected and if the data is being shared, with who it is shared and why it is being shared. If the user would like to see more info, he/she can click a button to be redirected to a more elaborate explanation. The user should have the option to decline, however this would most likely go at the cost of the functionality of the system. 
If you want to be sure that the user pays attention to this video and not just let it play and wait until it‚Äôs done, a quiz can be introduced, where the user can only continue after answering the correct answer. However with this, difficulties might arise about the level of these questions since the product should be accessible for everyone.
If in a later stage the user changes his/her mind about the data sharing, he/she should be able to EASILY (not hidden between 6 layers of menu‚Äôs) change or withdraw their consent.
Privacy:
Regarding privacy, it is usually best to keep the amount of data that is being collected low. However, especially in a smart home a lot of data will be collected, since this will be a combination of many different smart products that need to work together. 
Apart from having this data, even more important is what is done with this data, especially when it comes to making profiles of people based on data. Of course this could be a valuable addition for marketing related purposes, however I think you should be hesitant with using such tools, especially since everyone is unique (and wants to feel unique) and putting someone in a box might not have a beneficial effect.
If needed, data can be shared with 3rd parties, but that would only be the case if  these parties really add something to the system, meaning that they have a hardware product or software platform that uses this data in favour of the user and use experience. However for every party, a really thorough discussion should be held to find out what info is really essential and which is not. Important is that this sharing of data should be very clearly communicated to the user so that it‚Äôs easy to understand what it might ‚Äòcost‚Äô them in terms of data and what benefit it would give the user. Apart from that, the user should again have the possibility to withdraw consent for sharing this information at any moment in time. 
Although it might be a very easy way to get some extra income as a company, I think it should be preferred to not share data with other parties for marketing purposes. Although the price that will be charged to the user might become a bit higher then, I think with being open about it and branding it as not sharing data will also be an incentive for the user to buy. 
For all data that is being stored internally or shared to other parties, I think a lot of effort should be put into de-identifying data so that it‚Äôs not (so) easy to find out to who this data belongs. That would mean that if a hacker would be able to infiltrate the Smart Home Database, he/she still would not know which information/account is linked to which person. However, one of the downsides in this case of the internet is that everything is being stored, also the metadata such as the IP address and the timestamp from which specific actions happen. So when someone would be physically following or watching someone do something it might be possible to trace this information back to a specific account in the database for example.
For my project I decided not to integrate a smart assistant in the form of a speaker in the Smart house. Although it has many benefits in terms of usability and it could make your life a lot easier, you should be aware that this means that the system is always listening to what you say. Although most companies would not directly sell your conversations for marketing purposes, maybe it will record snippets to train its' own voice assistant? Do you know if it does? Probably it‚Äôs hidden somewhere in that very long informed consent document that you just agreed upon.
Information security:
More and more we are seeing news about people or companies getting hacked and with all these sensors coupled to communicators, not only might sensitive data about the user or his/her environment be exposed, but devices like a smart fridge and videorecorder are being used for DDoS attacks. One of the main reasons for this to be possible is the user itself.
In the ideal situation, a device would have a 32-character password containing upper and lower case, numbers and symbols that will be changed daily. However, often the opposite is true: default passwords like ‚Äúpassword‚Äù and ‚Äú123456‚Äù never get changed which makes such devices very vulnerable for hackers. However, having such long passwords that need to be changed so often is also not preferred from a use perspective. I would suggest that upon registration (when you are trying to link a new device to your smart home system) it will be required to change the default password to a stronger password (no name, smaller case and upper case, digits and symbols, minimum length).
Apart from the user, part of the responsibility is also at the company behind the technologies and the ones that are maintaining the platforms. Of course the user wants his products and services as cheap as possible, but that will most likely go at the cost of security. However,  people do start realizing more and more the importance of data security and therefore they also might be willing to start paying more to achieve this improved security. 
This improved security however will go against the core value of IoT, namely the ease of communication between devices. I think it is important to make the user aware of the situation and the risks involved. If you want a smart system that is very easy to connect, it might go at the cost of security and I think it will depend per user what sort and amount of information they would risk for such a benefit. However, many of the people that are buying smart systems will not understand how the product/system works, so I think it‚Äôs a good idea to invest more time/money/energy in that.
Physical safety:
When letting all those smart systems and devices have a place in our life, people need to be aware of the physical risks that come with it. The danger for your physical safety is not so much harmed when your printer is being hacked and it starts to print spam messages, however when a smart door lock gets hacked, the impact will be a lot higher. Again, people probably don‚Äôt see the dangers (just yet) but they will rather see it as a nice gimmick, but if things go wrong, they could go wrong quite badly. 
Apart from getting hacked there is of course also the case that the grid might be offline, what will happen in that case? Of course when you have a backup battery, you might be able to fix some things by yourself and make sure that not everything will be accessible for people with bad intentions. I think that it‚Äôs very important that when design these product or systems, to keep in mind these ‚Äòdisaster‚Äô scenarios. For a smart door lock for example, you should design it in such a way that when it unlocks, the door should stay in it‚Äôs rabbet(?).
Trust:
I think trust is about reliability, understanding and communication. As a user you should be able to rely on the product doing what you expected it to do (over a longer period of time) and if it deviates from this, it should clearly communicate this to the user, so he or she keeps updated and is able to understand (a simplified version of) the behaviour of the system. Once it starts leading it‚Äôs own life, I don‚Äôt think that there will be a very good relationship between the system and the user, and I think it would rather lead to frustration or disappointment since the system then doesn‚Äôt behave as the user would expect. 
When the system would make changes, it‚Äôs also important to not only say why it makes the changes but make sure that it displays what‚Äôs in it for the user. Really show that the AI and the user are in the same team and that the AI is not working against the user.
When it comes to decision making, especially in the beginning I would leave this to the user. No matter how bad the choice is, the user is right (at least he thinks). I think it would be acceptable if the system starts making suggestions which the user needs to accept and apply himself. If the user sees that it really works, it will start to trust the system more and slowly you can hand over control to the system, but the user should always be able to change these decision to his or her likings.
Sustainability: Whereas from a user experience perspective IoT can be a great tool, in terms of sustainability it can be both a tool or a harm. For all these smart products, sensors, controllers, extra power are needed, and later on, it will generate a lot of E-waste, which currently is not being recycled very well. At the same time, when applying these sensors correctly, they might be able to indicate in time when replacement or repair for certain parts are needed and therefore protecting the product as a whole from maybe even more negative impact in terms of sustainability. So it really depends on how these sensors and systems are being used in my opinion.

Name: P83
Informed consent:
For this system you have to make sure to properly explain what data will be collected from the users and what the risks can be that come with it. 
For example, the live data that will be collected are from the pressure mat in the seat, the IR-UWB radar sensor, and the accelerometer of the phone. The information from the pressure mat and the IR-UWB radar sensor will stay in the wheelchair so will be harder to get to as a third party. The data from the accelerometer will be collected from the phone, therefore needs to go through the cloud. This data should be encrypted in the app before sending it to the cloud, making it more difficult to interpret for others. Next to that, the name of the user can be kept away from the personal data at all time. It can just come with a customer number to send to the right wheelchair. This number with the associated name can be kept in a (digital) safe somewhere far away from praying eyes. 
Even if it might be difficult to obtain their personal data, the user should know what could be the harm in it. Accelerometer data can for example not be used to see the location of a person, but it shows how much the user has moved in a certain time frame.
Other sensitive data that is collected for the service are some test results of the user. This data specifically does not sound like very sensitive data. It is very abstract, and not something people are often ashamed about. However, if it falls in the wrong hands with a connection to your name it could be harmful. Insurance companies would of course love to know which of their customers have a bigger risk on heart diseases to up the price for these people. 
Because this data will be very difficult to obtain from the hospital directly, it would probably be filled in in the app manually by the users after every checkup. This also makes sure the users won‚Äôt just press another check mark but consciously know what they are giving access to. Maybe you can even allow the user to choose what they fill in and what they won‚Äôt. However, this will result in a less accurate prediction. So if that would be a possibility, that should be mentioned as well.
A final possible use for their data would be to expand the data set with it to further train the algorithms. I think it is best to leave this completely optional in the app and not even required to use the app. Giving them a clear option to choose for it and always being able to change it. Of course the data will be anonymised before it gets included in the data set. Before offering this service, more research should be done and the data set should be expanded. But after that, when the algorithm is trained well enough, it is not completely necessary to keep adding all the data to the data set.
So you shouldn‚Äôt try to get this data by hiding it or confusing the user so much that they will give this information because they don‚Äôt understand what they are signing up for. By giving them a clear choice and showing the benefits and the risks, it also adds to the transparency and therefore the trust in the company. (More over trust in the associated block.)
One final thing that should be taken into account when designing for this target group, is that there might be people with mental disabilities who are using it. So this informed consent is probably not only for them, but also for caregivers or relatives. So how do you make sure the user actually understands what they are saying yes to. The data could be something to be filled in together with the doctor, which can be nice because they have a personal bond and better understand the consequences. However, they are not allowed to influence the decision too much. So maybe it should be done together with the next of kin. So a informed consent isn‚Äôt as straight forward as with other users. (It‚Äôs never straight forward‚Ä¶)
Privacy:
I really liked the example of Wolf in the lecture about the camera on the toilet. Only capture the information you really need. For me this also transfers to: only compute the data on the cloud if you really need to. Most of the live captured data will stay on board of the wheelchair to make it much harder to retrieve it from a distance. 
Next to that, you can constantly measure everything needed to predict the chance on a heart disease for example. But I think it is sufficient to receive this information after the annual (or more often if the user would need it) checkup in the hospital. With this information you only receive for example the value for the resting blood pressure instead of constant measuring it and extracting the needed resting blood pressure.
Within privacy it‚Äôs important to look at the rights of the user to control what personal information is used and how it is used. This is a big part in the informed consent part as well. The users should be able to choose what information they want to share and making it available to incorporate in the existing data set to train the model is completely optional.

(Sorry if I mentioned more subjects within this part. But informed consent is so connected to privacy and information security that it is difficult to talk about it without talking about the other subjects as well.)
Information security:
If we look at the security of the information we collected, it is important to leave the name of the user out of the data. In the system of the company they can always be referred to as a number to make it harder to link certain data to a certain user. And the data that has to be transferred can be encrypted in the app and decrypted on the wheelchair, meaning it will never be unencrypted in the cloud. Only if it is added to the data set to be used for machine learning it can be placed in the cloud unencrypted, but if the data is anonymised this is less harmful.
If we look at the security challenges, one thing to take into account might be availability. We can see that there are several options in which the system can be made unavailable. By keeping all the important computing processes local, the data only needs to be updated once in a while. Therefore accessibility of the data is less crucial for the service to work. The predictions can‚Äôt be made at the moment of unavailability of a network, or at least not with up to date data. The biggest risk of losing connection is that the caregivers or first response team cannot be warned in case of an emergency. To reduce this risk, the system uses internet connection and connection to mobile network. If both would not work there is a backup alarm on the wheelchair itself to warn bystanders. Of course this is only the worst case scenario, but it‚Äôs interesting to still integrate in the design.
Physical safety: With this product physical safety is very important of course. The health and safety of the user is the main reason the product would exist. Including electrical components in a user product can always lead to shocks if something goes terribly wrong. One of the most dangerous parts of most electrical products with a battery is the Lithium Ion battery itself. In the worst case, this could explode. So this battery should be mounted on the back of the wheelchair as far away from the user as possible, and in a strong protective box.
Trust: If you are transparent as a company and show how you secure the users information, trust can be build. This is why proper informed consent is important. You need to show the users what they give away, what could happen if a third party might get access, and what you do to prevent that. I think that this system, with the proper safety precautions could earn the trust of the customers.
Sustainability: With all products with a battery, there is a sustainability issue with the recycling or disposal of the battery. This is why you should only use a battery if it‚Äôs really needed. In some wheelchairs it‚Äôs already included, but with others you still have to add it. Because of sustainability I tried to give sensors double purposes and not use a new sensor for every type of data you want. This decreases the environmental impact of the product service system a bit. Next to that it is worth thinking about the end of life of the product. If the user doesn‚Äôt need or want it anymore, can the sensors and electronics be reused in another product? I think this product can be made very modular. A pressure mat for example doesn‚Äôt need to be changed a lot to fulfill its duty in this product. So if you mount it in a smart way you can just reuse it afterwards.
Other: I don't think I covered all the ethical concerns. But that is also the main takeaway of this part of the assignment for me. It is very difficult to think of everything and predict all the possible situations. But I think it's important that we learned how to think about it and try taking it into account. And if I come in contact with it in the future, I know what I do and don't know. So I can consult a professional instead of thinking it will all be fine and just winging it.

Name: P84
Informed consent: Typically users aren't really aware of what data they hand over and how the data is treated. In order to be able to use the devices, you just need to accept the terms. In case of the smart house this would be contextual consent
Privacy: In the smart system not a lot of biodata is collected, therefor the data is not really sensitive. This changes if you think about security camera's, these might record private moments (e.g. nudity). By installing a camera users typically are aware of this risk. However, it is unclear whether also voice recordings are made. This is another form of biodata which can also be very private. Beside that, applications on phones might use geotracking. This allows the device to know where the user is, questions arise whether such precise data is really required for devices.
Information security: Especially storing data and encrypting data is a big concern. With the biodata individuals can be identified, also with geotracking patterns can be identified which also allows a system to find out who it is. This can be very private data and therefor it should be encrypted and stored in a way that only the system and trusted companies may enter. Also, sharing this data with third parties should be avoided
Physical safety:
The biggest concern with physical safety is if others have access to the data. Users might have opposing lifestyles which may lead to conflicting values and dangerous situations for the user. Therefore, the data should always be encrypted. 
Another important thing about physical safety is knowing who has access to the system, this way you avoid the possibility of 'hatecrimes'
Trust: The user needs to be able to trust the companies, this requires companies to stick to their ethical values and that they actively think about data security and update their policies.
Sustainability:
As a designer and system analyst it is possible to support sustainability by providing suggestions to users. E.g. washing moments (during sunny hours), automatic lighting that also switches off when a room is not used, thermostat that makes sure the house is only warmed when users are at home. This way data can support sustainability. 
Nevertheless, this also requires sensors and devices. These need to be designed for a long timespan to avoid being replaced too soon which would result is less waste.

Name: P85
Informed consent: The user has to agree to be able to use (certain parts of) the system. They have to know what data is shared to which parties. This is often found in the Terms of Service (ToS). Adding a simple bullet point list which explains the most important parts of the ToS could help people to actually understand the ToS.
Privacy: Only share data that is necessary within the system. The smartphone app should not share more data than needed. For example, skin thickness is a data point that is not relevant for predicting diabetes. Therefore this data should not even be shared outside of the phone app.
Information security: Make sure everything is encrypted and anonymized when possible. Think of securing bluetooth and wifi connections, and database storage.
Physical safety: Anonymize data and remove data after a predetermined period of time
Trust: Clear communication is key. Build a reputation with your brand to ensure your users of your posotive business practices. Perhaps make honestly part of one of the main selling points of your business (high transparency is not the norm).
Sustainability: How is the IoT system implemented? Does the whole shirt get thrown in the trash when one part malfunctions? Can we repair the product?
Other: Manufacturing in a more ethical way (wage, location, etc.)

Name: P86
Informed consent: Getting the hotel guest‚Äôs signature to give permission for recording their data that is needed for the IoT system can be easy, by just adding a clause to the contract that they are already signing. However, quite often people don‚Äôt fully read the contract they sign. Therefore we can say that in this case the guests give assent, but it is not informed consent as they are not aware of what they give consent to. In order to make sure that the users know what they give their consent to, an additional form can be asked to sign which clearly and shortly states what data is collected, how it is collected and what it is used for.
Privacy:
Privacy is a general concern in any IoT system, as they all center around collecting user data. This case is no different. People should be free to do what they please, so long as they don‚Äôt break any laws or rules posed by the hotel. By integrating four sensors in each room, data is collected about the area of the users on 4 different parameters, at all times. Now, the smoke sensor is most likely not going to infringe on privacy, as they only collect data that is not directly generated by the user and they are considered standard in (hotel) rooms all around the world. The same arguments are true for the temperature sensor, as they can be found in any room that has airconditioning (= most hotelrooms). Although the movement sensor does collect data that is generated directly by the users, it only detects IF there is movement, not to where or who moves. Beside, movement sensors have been used in hotelrooms before, so I don‚Äôt see a privacy risk in them. 

The sound sensors do raise privacy concerns. They collect data that is directly generated by the user, and this type of data (i.e. sound) is especially sensitive. In order to not infringe on the privacy of the guests, two options can be implemented. The first is that the sensor must be used in such a way so that conversations cannot be understood, yet the parameters that are needed can be collected. I am not a sound expert, so I don‚Äôt know if that‚Äôs an option. The second option is following the protocols of for instance Google Assistant or Amazon Echo. They are in principle always listening, but (say that) they are not recording and storing the conversations they hear. 

To further mitigate the risk of infringement of privacy, I have chosen to only show the staff members status updates, instead of seeing the values for each sensor in each room. This way, only if the computing hub detects a problem the staff can see what it is.
Information security:
As said before, some of the data that is collected might be sensitive, and thus could be bad if it were used for purposes other than intended. To mitigate this risk, the sensors in the system I propose only sends out the raw and unrecognizable data, through technologies like LoRa or RF, which cannot be hacked through the internet. Only the computing hub is connected to the internet, but that will only send status updates, not the data from the sensors. 

Like almost all internet-based IoT systems, hacking is also a concern in this system. Malicious hackers could get access to the computing hubs and then have acces to the (raw and unrecognizable) data. I think that the implications for such a hack would be limited, as the hackers wouldn‚Äôt know what the data means.
Physical safety: The proposed system is in part designed to let staff know if any possible dangers arise in a hotel room (for instance fire or a fall). This means that the system will actually be benificial for physical safety. The system does not contain any (moving) actuators like door locks, so that also drasticallt decreases the risk of physical harm to the guests and staff.
Trust:
If we look at trust in the sense of reliance, concerns are present but not very great. The proposed system in principle replaces a human staffmember. If the system would (partly) fail, then it can be replaced by a staffmember until it is fixed again. Although it might result in extra cost for the hotel, it does not change the experience for the guest. 

We can also look at trust in the sense of goodwill: if we believe that other people are treating each other with goodwill (Allhoff & Henschke (2018). This raises some concerns that might possibly come from guests. They are aware of the system and are aware what data is collected and how it is stored (through the extra form, as explained in ‚Äòconcerns regarding informed consent‚Äô), but do guests believe that? Or do they feel that they have been lied to, and that other people (i.e. staffmembers) will use the information gathered in their room maliciously? A way to mitigate this can be by creating a sort of transparency in the system. What if guests could see what data from their respective room is being sent to the cloud? Maybe this could increase their trust in the system and show them that their data is secure.
Sustainability:
The main issue regarding sustainability in this system is energy consumption. Each room has at least five active components (four sensors + one computing hub), which all consume electricity. Modern communication technologies like BLE use significantly less energy than their predecessors, and the PIR sensor could also be used to lower energy consumption by turning off lights when there is no movement. But, the hotel will probably still use more energy than one without such an IoT system. 

It also has to be noted that the electronics might use critical materials, which can drastically increase their environmental footprint.
Other: The functional prototype can distinguish a female voice from a male voice. While some might say that this can be used to ‚Äòguard defenseless women from bad angry men‚Äô, I think that this is an overly stereotypical view on society, and I don‚Äôt think it is ethical to use the distinction for this purpose. There are many more instances where male and female guests get along perfectly, than instances where female guests are intimidated by male guests. Of course this is still a great concern and actions need to be taken to lower this risk, but I just think that using a voice detection is much too superficial to detect these situations.

Name: P87
Informed consent: Informed consent deals with two parts. 1. The user confirming or affirming and 2. them being informed. The degree of information they should be the why and general how but not go as far as the answer to the deeply technically how-question. In the system I designed the user should be informed of the type of data which is collected (seat pressure sensors, general cabin sensors (the data can not be linked to people), and the necessary data of the device connected to the smart cabin > user‚Äôs phones). Explaining the why should be easy. The moment at which this information is presented to users and they are asked for their consent can be done at two instances: 1. When the user buys a ticket online. 2. When they are on board and connect their phone to the smart cabin network. If at any point they do not consent the user/seat specific data is not gathered and used.
Privacy: As with many IoT systems, privacy is the largest ethical concern, so much so that, just like I mentioned in the presentation, I did not include many sensors which monitor users personal data or even biometrics. Only pressure sensors are used which give a real time update on how long the user has been seated and an estimating whether they are asleep or not. Connecting a device to the smart cabin does give the system information on the type of device which is connected. Theoretically this information could be used in estimating a passengers income and be used to scale prices differently but I have no such intentions. Lastly, the passenger manifest contains quite a lot of personal data. This data has been supplied by the passenger to the airline, however this data is quite personal and is used to try to align passengers with certain passenger profiles. Benefits include: In flight entertainment automatically selects the right language, gives you certain recommendations (does not recommend kids movies for adults for example). Users might however not like that this system knows quite a lot about them, more than I initially expected.
Information security: The smart cabin concept allows users to tap their phones to quickly connect to the smart cabin system. No passwords are required. It is often the case that making something more information secure creates more barriers of use. I have opted to lower these barriers considerably when designing the system. Other passengers could easily tap their phones on another seat and take control of that seat's lighting, air conditioning and order drinks to that seat. However, this is about the extent I expect this to be able to go. When a passenger is logged in to the smart cabin from their seat their personal information from the passenger manifest should not be visible, not even to themselves, that way no one else can access the information. Only when the plane has landed will the data from the flight be sent back to the airline cloud server to improve the model and the new passenger manifest will be loaded in. This data should naturally be encrypted as I expect all data from these already existing servers to be.
Physical safety: Ethics concerns regarding physical safety in regard to the context of the smart cabin have more to do with the broader context rather than individual physical safety. One of the aircraft wide concerns would be what would happen in the event of a system failure. If systems are too interdependent this might be disastrous, therefore I have advised that flight control systems must be in no way connected to the smart cabin just like in flight entertainment is not connected. These concerns are not strictly ethical but rather proper design. I can not identify purely ethical concerns regarding physical safety.
Trust: The smart cabin concept system should try to gain users' trust by clearly showing the passengers its intentions, for instance via the informed consent information. The passengers should have no doubt that the system is primarily designed to increase their well being aboard. This vision should be made very clear to them. I do not see any clear concerns regarding trust as long as Boeing does not commercialize the system too much. If the goal of the systems seems to be to increase in-flight passengers purchases I would immediately lose trust as I would see their intentions as insincere, this should therefore be avoided.
Sustainability: My implementation plan calls for several NFC readers/sensors to be implemented into the seat tables, multiple pressure sensors into seats and a lot of data being sent to and from servers. Electronics are unfortunately not easily recyclable and have bad end of life potential and sending huge amounts of data cost a lot of energy. Both are quite negative however
Other: None that I could think of. The article on foundational ethical issues with IoT was quite interesting.

Name: P88
Informed consent: It is important, once a vaccination passport is going to be used, to inform the users about the relevant information that is shared and with what parties. The users must know what they are assenting to. For example, the extent of protection conferred by vaccination, particularly against new variants, is not yet well understood, nor is the potential for viral transmission by people who have been vaccinated. This has more to do with the virus itself, but inevitably leads to the same questions when considering getting a vaccine and thus a vaccination passport.
Privacy: Antagonists mainly see problems when it comes to privacy. Such a vaccine passport could, for example, divide Europeans into people who are healthy and who are not. It could also lead to more health checks. The medical data will be stored in a secured way according to the GGD.  Recent dataleaks show otherwise, which questions the privacy.
Information security:
The data that is used to alarm people when an infected person was in their presence for a longer period of time is encrypted in codes. These codes store limited information and are randomly generated, the application doesn‚Äôt know who or where you are. This is a great form of security, using only Bluetooth to define the distance between people. 
However, looking at the bigger picture, there are some problems regarding information security.
The private data of millions of Dutch people from the IT systems used at the GGD were illegally traded on the internet. Fast implemented measures may lead to weakened security.
Physical safety:
No physical (moving components)  actuators are used in the described system so this is no potential form of harm. 
The app might give a false sense of security and make health agencies and policy makers over reliable on them, which could lead to a lack of setting up strict rules.
Proving that you are vaccinated leads to a safer environment, since gathering of people will be safe again, without the risk to spread the virus.
Looking at it from the other side, antivaxers  still issue the safety of the vaccine and believe that it consists of substances that might hurt the body.
Trust: On political level, lots of promises were not kept regarding the relaxation of the measurements. This harmed the trust of a big part of the Dutch society. Not only the government, but also the GGD caused trust issues.
Sustainability: Regarding sustainability, vaccine passports will allow the majority to travel again, go back to work, and re open their businesses. Traffic levels are down,  resulting in a huge reduction in air pollution and emissions of gas caused by the economic disruption caused by COVID-19.

Name: P89
Informed consent:

Information security:
I think the two biggest concerns regarding security are confidentiality and access

Confidentiality:

Access control:
Maybe it is a good idea to force users to lock their data. When they would want to access, biometrics would be needed (which most phones have). This would be to protect your sensitive data in case that your device would be stolen.

Other: none

Name: P90
Informed consent:

Name: P91
Informed consent: With the Nike shirt (I chose a shirt for my case), there is personal data that is being collected and that is being used for an algorithm. The user in question thus needs to be aware of the data that is being gathered and for what purposes. When being aware and knowing the purpose of the gathered data, it could be said that giving the user the choice for this data being collected, would be the right thing to do. Of course, the more data that is gathered, the more the algorithm would be able to help people but this should not stand in the way of personal choice.
Privacy: Concerning Privacy, it would of course be important that the data would not be readable by more parties than the user in question consents to. This is why in the IOT, I mentioned that a private cloud would be recommended). Next to this, the user should be able to change the Privacy settings any time. Privacy is not only about giving away data but also where it is being stored etc.
Information security: In order to ensure Information security, encryption can be used to prevent an information leak. In the paper survey there has been mentioned that encryption is not the ultimate solution to ensure this because of metadata. For this case, authorities need to put the clear boundaries to ensure Information security is being kept from a legal point of view since technically it seems harder to achieve because of for example metadata.
Physical safety: The Physical safety of the product is not as serious as autonomous vehicles but if the motion sensor would be included and it accidently would register a hand movement for turning up the volume, this could have some effects in the sense of not hearing a motorcycle coming when you are crossing the street without looking etc.. So there are some concerns but the only sensor in my IOT that has an effect on the physical world is actually the motion sensor since I intended to design the Nike shirt as an 'information gatherer' rather than a 'regulating unit based on the gathered information'.
Trust: The user could lose its trust if certain parts/ promised functions/... would not work. If their expectations are being crushed. In order to prevent this, transparency can be a helpful factor. Sustainability: The ethicality concerning the Sustainability of the Nike shirt, electronics would create a lot of E-waste which is a big aspect to be concerned about. Not only on planet aspect, but also people aspect, electronics are not collected in the most ethical ways.
Other: The lack for a clear direction. Ethics are complicated but when applied to IOT or a system in general, it becomes way more complicated and certainly the causal networks. Name: P92
Informed consent: Due to the presence of many interconnected smart systems, the user must be informed that their data is being collected to increase their comfort and that the data collected will be used to train the machine learning models. The user must also sign a User License Agreement (EULA) and must be given an option to opt out of the service.
Privacy:
‚Ä¢	Personal information such as body temperature, body movement, heart rate and sounds are collected to monitor the comfort of the passenger, and they are linked to the passenger‚Äôs personal details. This makes the data personable and identifiable. 
‚Ä¢	Body temperature, muscle movement and heart rate data are extremely valuable to big pharma companies in creating their own products and services using the personal data of the passengers and their health data. When using the AVIATAR data system, there is a possibility of this data being sold to make profit.
‚Ä¢	Even if these issues could somehow be circumvented, when the user connects their smart devices and wearables to the system, there is very little done to prevent those devices from accessing the collected data as well. These pose a major issue regarding privacy.
‚Ä¢	Data that is collected and processed must follow the GDPR and other local data privacy laws. As they can change when you leave Europe, this makes it very complex.
Information security:
‚Ä¢	Since the proposed IoT architecture uses LAN and has a central server on board, the security protocols must be robust and comprehensive to ward off any attacks to the network. 
‚Ä¢	If a device with virus and other malware is connected to the network, it could affect the entire system.
‚Ä¢	Devices which are connected to the system can be used to breach the firewall to collect the data that is being gathered and hack into the flight systems.
Physical safety:
‚Ä¢	Photoplethysmography can potentially cause burns or itches in some people and cause physical harm.
‚Ä¢	The sensors could overheat or malfunction physically and set fire to the upholstery of the seats.
‚Ä¢	The sensors can malfunction and recommend the wrong intervention such as give tea to a guy who is suffering from a heart attack.
‚Ä¢	Since there are no actuators in this system, there is no cause for alarm from that.
Trust:
‚Ä¢	The machine learning model offers predictions and suggestions based on the processed data. Since the data is acted upon by the flight attendants, the user must trust that model has a high degree of accuracy.
‚Ä¢	The passenger must trust that the best sensors are being used to monitor their comfort levels, and that they have a low failure rate.
‚Ä¢	The passenger should trust that there are enough cybersecurity protocols in place to ensure that the system can withstand cyberattacks.
‚Ä¢	All the information processed is being done by both the airline and the data processing server. The passenger must trust that their data is in good hands and will be processed and disposed responsibly.
Sustainability:
‚Ä¢	The hardware components could be manufactured in third world countries where there is no labour protection and safety. By using components sourced from such countries, we support this discrimination.
‚Ä¢	Software experts and customer service people work long and arduous hours. If the system is not good, they would need to spend more time working on it thus leading to bad physical and mental health.
‚Ä¢	All the hardware components are electronic. They should be responsibly disposed so that they don‚Äôt end up in a landfill and contribute to the global carbon footprint.
‚Ä¢	It takes power to run these systems. We must ensure that this power comes from renewable sources as much as possible to protect the environment. 
‚Ä¢	In the name of saving costs, substandard components must not be used in the system.
‚Ä¢	To increase the company‚Äôs profit, the company must not sell user data to other companies or use them for other means.

Name: P93
Informed consent:
An informed consent is certainly needed. The data collected is very personal and a lot of information can be extracted from it, telling you something about the user‚Äôs behaviour, even though the user might think of the data collected to be not that interesting. Permobil should therefore be transparent about this and clearly explain what all the data collected means. Doing this in text might however be an issue as the text might be using too many difficult medical terms or jargon. 

Although, with the informed consent, both the patient and Permobil each play a role formulating an acceptable plan for the medical care, Permobil cannot subject patients to certain perioperative tests without informed consent. They should therefore engage the patient in a discussion about the patient‚Äôs medical condition and ways of monitoring using the wheelchair. The goal of this discussion is then that the patient makes an informed decision about his or her use of the wheelchair. The most important thing then is that the informed consent is not a signature on a document; it is a dialogue with the patient that occurs before the patient signs a consent form.
Privacy: As also stated in the previous answer, the data collected is very personal data. This nevertheless is required as it is also a very personal problem. The trade of needs to be made between functionality and privacy as the system needs a lot of data to function properly. The patient must be convinced that the benefits are worth the data he/she is giving the system. Possibly the vital and medical data can be anonymized; Peter could just be customer #2253. Data privacy for the wheelchair furthermore refers to freedom from unauthorized access to private data, inappropriate use of data, accuracy and completeness when collecting data about the patient by technology, availability of data content, and the data subject‚Äôs legal right to access the rights to inspect, update or correct these data. All these are issues that should be carefully investigated.
Information security: There can be treats at 3 different levels: device, network & cloud/server. The principle of information security: CIA (Confidentiality, Integrity and Availability) all have to be clearly addressed and possible attacks should anticipated, this should be transparently communicated to create trust.
Physical safety: Obviously all the electronics used should be safe for use in different environments; the movement sensors on the wheelchair should not catch fire if all of a sudden it starts to rain. Also, the influence of the radiation from all the devices constantly being around the patient should be investigated as some patients might question whether or not that is healthy.
Trust:
‚ÄòA trustworthy party is one that will not unfairly exploit the vulnerabilities of the other party in the relationship.‚Äô There should therefore be honest, transparent and clear communication as the patient might doubt about whether or not the companies interests are aligned with his/her own. Permobil is however an established brand that is competent to deliver on commitments. Nevertheless, the further removed individuals are from the locus of the relationship, the complicated trustworthiness becomes. So although the caregivers might be close and might create trust, also Permobil, operating the system, should stay close and aware of their patients. 

Lastly, Permobil should make use of European chip and sensor manufacturers. Asian ones are expected to be a lot less secure than those produced in the EU, because the EU sets high standards and has compliance mechanisms in place to make sure sensor producers measure up to these standards.
Sustainability: The next generation connected wheelchair makes great use of sensors. These sensors however require rare metals. The wheelchair furthermore also requires quite a lot of energy and lastly the electronics are packed with toxic chemicals such as arsenic, lead and poly-brominated flame retardants and are incredibly costly both to extract as well as recycle; the latter especially because there are not enough recyclers. These are all concerns that should be investigated carefully to see where they can be improved and otherwise minimized.

Name: P94
Informed consent: The informed consent must be kept as short and comprehensible as possible to be sure that the user will read a fully comprehend it.  It should be more focused on the use of the user's data rather than technical aspects. The users should be informed that their data can be stored and used to predict customer behaviour and improve the service within the flight. However, it's also important that the user is informed that the data are anonymous and cannot be sold to third parties or linked to the individual to know, for instance, how much she/he slept or drunk.
Privacy: I decided to use alternatives systems to cameras in order to avoid unnecessary acquisition of images. It is important that all the data collected should be anonymous, and not sold or used by third parties. However, should be noticed that can be easy to date back to an individual using the flight's ticket.
Information security: Since the data are anonymous information are not particularly sensible and requirement about information security are not high. Using an ethernet connection to communicate from the single seat to the central system of the plane should be more secure than WiFi, if the data are later stored and elaborated in an external server it should be secured and accessible only by the company. If the company in the future wants to provide a personalized service, for instance, an app to make the user able to select his/her favourite seat preset, another layer of security should be added and the user should be reminded of change often sufficient complex password for the app. The security should be well balanced and should not compromise the user experience.
Physical safety: All the actuators in the system should avoid any physical hazard for the users, for instance, the servomotors to put in position armrest should use just the sufficient force necessary to move their weight stopping their movements if someone interferes with them, this is particularly important for children. A plane is a complex system with and any type of interference should be avoided for this reason it's better to avoid when possible WiFi connections.
Trust: The reliability of the system in synergy with the human sensibility of the crew's member can create for the users the feeling of having their need anticipated and fulfilled, building trust in the system and in the company.
Sustainability: I decided to use an ethernet connection instead of WiFi to avoid additional interferences in the plane's network. However this choice has some drawback from the sustainability standpoint, in particular, it will create more electronic waste at the end of life of the plane.

Name: P95
Informed consent:
Since the wheelchair architecture not only collects medical data from the user but also uploads it to the cloud and shares it with the healthcare facility, having the patient's informed consent is essential. However, in this case the patient might be more vulnerable due to existing health conditions. The question as to how capable the patient is to decide upon this might arise, and the decision could be delegated to the caregiver.
Furthermore, as the patient's condition evolves, it is very likely that their privacy preferences do so too. It is therefore essential that informed consent be given in a dynamic way, and not just required once, at the beginning of the service.
Privacy:
As for the data collected from the user and the privacy implications. For the wheelchair, it becomes even more complex, since the data is of medical nature. Medical privacy is of special importance for most patients and this kind of data is considered sensitive. There is a large debate on Big Data sharing in health, on how to balance the patient's privacy rights with the  benefits of data sharing for science and health.
The privacy of the patient is also important in the patient-caregiver relationship. How much information should be shared with the carer and how much should remain private? Who decides this? The same for the healthcare institution.
Information security: In this case, the proposed system architecture uploads the data periodically to the server of the corresponding healthcare facility, making it vulnerable to a cloud server attack . It is strictly recommended that this flow of information be done following the regulatory guidelines for a medical app and not a ‚Äúwellness app‚Äù. Since medical apps follow stricter legislation as they handle sensitive data.
Physical safety: One of the main goals of the connected wheelchair is to increase the patient's safety. However, it does so by monitoring the healthy status without the constant supervision of a healthcare professional.  Lack of accuracy or a biased algorithm could therefore result in a wrong diagnosis or an overlooked health risk. This poses the question of, who has the responsibility if the algorithm fails?
Trust: The same can be said for trust. An overreliance of the patient in the system architecture could lead to a misdiagnosis or a wrong judgment without the opinion of a healthcare professional.
Sustainability: The IoT of the connected wheelchair increases social sustainability. By monitoring the patient's healthy status and predicting potential dangers, without the constant need of a carer, the patient's independence increases. This is positive in that not all users can afford the cost of a carer, therefore allowing the monitoring of a larger scope of individuals that potentially couldn't access this kind of care otherwise. Furthermore, the increased independence can lead to larger inclusivity of physically impaired users.

Name: P98
Informed consent: With a smart seat, the comfort/wellbeing of passengers are monitored and based on their state, a decision is carried out on the passenger. It can be equated that the health of the passengers are monitored. In the same vein, when doctors treat patients, patients must have informed consent which includes documentation of consent, competency and decision capacity. Informed consent is important to avoid unethical bias and to promote autonomy.
Privacy: As cameras may be used as part of the new smart system, passengers are understandably concerned about their privacy. The faces of the passengers should be blurred and their personal information should not be stored. The airline should also ensure that the cameras are marked clearly and display warning signs so that the passengers are informed. The concern is that there might be unauthorised access to the data and the data may be used inappropriately. However, an airplane cabin is a public space. Therefore, it can be monitored without any privacy violations.
Information security: Expanding on the previous concern on privacy, information security is closely tied to it. The smart seat would be collecting a lot of personal data on the passenger. The data would be uploaded onto a remote server so that analytics can be run and improve future experience. This data should be encrypted or protected, and should not be stored. Any data that is on a remote server (on the cloud) is open to potential cyberattacks.
Physical safety: When designing anything, the physical safety of the users is of paramount importance. Though the design may be spectacular, if the safety of the users is not considered, then it should not be implemented at all. The question is whether the design is worth harming any human life. If companies were to ignore physical safety, ethical concerns aside, they may face legal and social trouble.
Trust: Trust is an ethical issue because it guides decisions within organisations. With trust, everyone (customers, companies and other stakeholders) are able to decide more ethically. As IoT can be considered relatively new, many consumers would naturally be wary. Their trust should not be abused in order to launch IoT to a next level.
Sustainability: More technology has to be used to implement smart seats in an aircraft. For the sake of improving customer experience, the amount of potential e-waste generated has been increased. Air travel already contributes a big carbon footprint and this could be adding to that. Care must be taken to ensure that the new system contributes positively or as least negatively as possible to the environment and people.

Name: P103
Informed consent: Since the app collects personal data and shares this with a server, it is important that people sign an informed consent. This is the case for Corona Apps. However, as Allhoff and Henschke explain in their paper 'The Internet of Things: Foundational ethical issues', it is also important that people understand what they are giving consent for, instead of just ticking a box. Therefore, the user needs to understand in a brief way how the app registers to whom you have been close to via Bluetooth Low Energy. I think the Dutch government does a great job in explaining this. The CoronaMelder (Dutch Corona Tracing App) website has infographics to explain how the app works. They even provide a video with the app explained in very simple words. Moreover, the app itself contains answers to a list of questions people might have. So I think that with this video and appealing infographics, the CoronaMelder app encourages people to understand what they're giving consent to.
Privacy:
An ethical privacy concern of Covid tracing through apps is that people's privacy could be compromised because their location and movement would be monitored. To ensure that the government, Google, Apple and hackers cannot track the movement and locations of people, Bluetooth was chosen with encrypted keys instead of GPS or location data to measure if people have been close to each other. These keys are changed randomly every 15 minutes, to ensure that they cannot be connected to a specific person (CoronaMelder, 2021). 

However, there are also ethical concerns to another function of the Corona App: the vaccine certificate. What if you are against vaccinations and do not want to share this information with other people. But if you have to show your vaccine certificate to be able to travel, you are forced to show other people that you decided not to get vaccinated. This way, people have access to your personal medical data, which could invade your privacy.
Information security:
Even though the keys that the phones exchange through Bluetooth are encrypted and change every 15 minutes, the app still has access to data from the Central Vaccine Register and the Central Register of the GGD. And this is exactly where it went wrong in January of 2021. Names, addresses, phones and social service numbers of millions of Dutch citizens were sold online, after employees of the GGD downloaded them from the system. Since there was a button in the system to export all data, it was relatively simple to steal the data. This data leak put millions of people at the risk of getting their identity stolen.

Moreover, in April 2021 it turned out that the encrypted data from Corona Tracing apps was not as well protected as we thought. A flaw in Google software has allowed data Corona Tracing apps to be accessed by the pre-installed apps on Android phones. This allows the apps to determine whether the phone is in the possession of someone previously reported as infected in CoronaMelder and what encounters with infected people have taken place.

These examples show that it is extremely important to think of what data you are collecting and how you protect it. However, this brings challenges related to usability, since more information security could limit the ease of communication between devices (Allhoff, Henschke, 2021).
Physical safety:
The argument used by people that are pro-vaccine certificate is that it ensures other people's physical safety by only letting vaccinated people go to for example a festival. 'Forcing' people to show a vaccine certificate is accepted if it ensures that other people do not get infected with Covid. Privacy is neglected for the 'common good'. This way, physical safety is used in an ethical debate about the vaccine certificate and corona tracing apps. 

However, corona tracing apps could also give people a fake sense of safety, since they expect to be warned if they have potentially been infected with Covid-19. This could make people more reckless and adhere less to the covid-regulations, thus potentially spreading the covid virus faster and harming other people's physical safety.
Trust:
Trust is extremely important for Corona Apps. If people do not trust the app, they will not use it, thus people will be at risk of not being warned when being in contact with an infected person. Moreover, if people do not trust the app, they might not follow the recommendation to quarantine after a warning.

According to Allhoff and Henschke (2018), in relation to the IoT, "trust can apply to the IoT in terms of whether the components will reliably serve their function if we can expect other users and developers to act in predictable ways, and if we believe that other people are treating each other with goodwill." The answer to all of these three parts could be 'no'. No, the components will not reliably serve their function, since data leaks proved that the encryption does not always work. However, trust could be regained if the information security is restored by experts while notifying the users. Moreover, we cannot expect other users to behave predictably, since some users will not quarantine after getting a warning of a possible infection. Lastly, the selling of personal data by GGD employees shows that other people are not treating each other with goodwill. All these incidents could make the user lose trust in the app. Therefore, it is extremely important to be transparent about these flaws and communicate with the users what is happening, so users feel involved in the process and understand that it could be safe to use the app again.

However, another ethical concern could be if we want AI to be responsible for our physical safety and health. Do we want to rely on AI to inform us about possible infections? This question is less important for corona apps since all warnings are double-checked by employees from the GGD, thus minimizing errors.
Sustainability:
If a vaccine certificate will be required to travel to Europe, this will disadvantage poorer countries even further. Since the European Union has bought large amounts of the vaccine, thus making them less available for developing countries, fewer people in developing countries are vaccinated.

Making a vaccine certificate obligatory to travel to Europe will disadvantage residents of poorer countries with limited access to vaccines further. This could result in residents from developing countries to not be able to work or follow education in Europe, thus limiting their wealth (NRC, 2021).
Other: Another ethical concern is about privacy and discrimination through data. Is it guaranteed that governments will not link vaccination data to other personal data, such as age or ethnicity? The recent scandal of the childcare allowance from the government shows that things go wrong with the collection of data. Last January, the cabinet fell due to a scandal that the government had discriminated against parents and untruthfully marked them as frauds based on their ethnicity (Government, 2021). There is a risk of such discrimination happening again if the government would link vaccination certificates to ethnicity.
