Name: P106
Informed consent:
Informed consent is of primary importance when medical data is involved, such as in the case of a connected wheelchair that monitors the health of the user. The user should consent to the acquisition, retention, and sharing of personal data, and should be made aware of what happens to their data.
Providing stakeholders such as the company, medical researchers, or doctors with appropriate data could help better inform the user regarding safety concerned with the operation of the wheelchair, by identifying potential risks of using the wheelchair under certain circumstances. Therefore, it should be clear to the user what data is being collected, where and how it will be stored, whom the data will be shared with, why the data is being collected, and the known side-effects and risks of using the wheelchair for prolonged periods.
Users should also be made aware of what personal data will be shared in the app to caregivers or third parties.
The cheaper option for the company would be getting the user to sign an end-user license agreement (EULA). In such cases, care should be taken to make the agreement sufficiently detailed and non-generic, without be too long that users are discouraged from reading through multiple pages of an extensive document. A more responsible option, although more costly, could be to adopt practices from the medical industry and allow potential users to interact with a representative from the company to clarify any questions they have regarding the use of their personal data, as well as any risks and side-effects, so that an informed decision can be made by the user when consenting.
Privacy:
Privacy is paramount when medical data is involved, such as in the case of a connected wheelchair that monitors the health of the user. Care should be taken to securely collect, store and share only the data that is necessary, based on what the user has consented to. 
Stakeholders, such as the company, or medical researchers, may want to access anonymous medical data to better understand and cater to the needs of the user or patients with specific medical conditions. A doctor, for instance, could use data collected from the sensors to view the medical history and activity levels of the patient, to aid in their diagnosis of a medical condition. In such a case, the user would need to be identified. Alternatively, a researcher could study the relationship between sensor data related to the user‚Äôs health, and sensor data related to the user‚Äôs activity levels whilst the data remains anonymous. Users should be able to opt out of sharing data they do not wish to disclose.
In the case of the wheelchair‚Äôs microphone which is used for communication with caregivers, as well as for the control of certain devices, users should be given the option to push a button to allow the microphone to be activated, so that it does not continuously listen to the user and collect more data than necessary.
Information security:
Increased information security hinders the seamlessness and ease provided by IoT, while adding to the cost of the product as well. However, a failure to ensure adequate information security could have grave consequences for privacy and physical safety and can easily invalidate informed consent and undermine trust. Therefore, the trade-off between different ethical values should be recognised when dealing with information security.
If data from sensors are intercepted by hackers, this could further expose potentially already vulnerable users to physical harm or other risks. For instance, hackers could misuse location data to predict when and where the user will be at a particular time, and blackmail, attack or steal from the user. Personal information could also be vulnerable if hackers listen to audio communications between the user and their caregiver via the wheelchair‚Äôs microphone. This would violate the user‚Äôs privacy and informed consent, while potentially compromising the user‚Äôs physical safety. 
To reduce such risks, communication between the wheelchair and cloud server should be encrypted to ensure information security is maintained. Users, caregivers, and any other authorised people with access to personal data should only be allowed to access the dedicated app using a sufficiently strong password that needs to be periodically changed. On a broader scope, the minimum set of necessary information security features required for handling sensitive medical and location data must be identified.
Physical safety:
The connected wheelchair has actuators and sensors that could compromise the physical safety of the user under certain circumstances. For example, if components related to the motion of the wheelchair (such as the wheel motors or the brakes) malfunction, the user and those surrounding it could suffer physical harm because of an accident. In such a case, it would be complicated to determine if the company is liable for the damages caused if the fault is not just due to the manufacturer‚Äôs complacency, but also if the user failed to routinely maintain the wheelchair as instructed by the manufacturer. Alternatively, a third party could also share the blame in contributing to the accident. It is the company‚Äôs responsibility to mitigate such risks by adding safety features, such as emergency brakes, and facilitating routine maintenance on the wheelchair. The remote control of the motion of the wheelchair should also be prevented to eliminate the risk of it being digitally hijacked by hackers.
The electronic components and battery may also pose a fire hazard to the user, who may not be able to leave the wheelchair depending on their physical ability. The physical inability could also impede their ability to protect themselves from physical threats. Hence, users should also be given urgent support in case of a medical emergency, accident, device failure, or in case the panic button is pushed, by sharing their location.
Trust:
Users rely on the wheelchair to function as intended and trust the company to deliver a product that performs the desired functions reliably. If the wheelchair malfunctions, (e.g., in case the wheel motors do not stop or the brakes fail and this causes physical harm) users could lose their trust in the company, which is vital to a company that caters to users with potentially more medical needs than the average person.
Users also trust that their data will be handled responsibly by their caregivers and others whom they consent to accessing this data. A breach of this trust could have serious implications for such a company.
Alternatively, the company should be open with its users about security breaches or vulnerabilities to show that the company has the user‚Äôs best interests at heart and maintain the trust users have in the company. If the company can guarantee the user‚Äôs privacy, information security and personal safety, then users will be able to rely on the product and trust the company. If the company fails in any of these aspects, the users may not only lose trust in the company, but also in IoT solutions as a whole.
Sustainability:
End-of-life and recycling of components is an important consideration to be made when dealing with the sustainability of a product. The manufacturer allows components, such as the battery, to be replaced, and recycles old batteries. This helps extend the lifespan of the product and improves its sustainability. A trade-off when employing IoT is that adding features involves more electronic components, which contribute to e-waste. Hence, it is important to ensure that durable components are used, which are as recyclable as possible. Adopting a circular business approach could help with sourcing materials as well as responsibly dealing with the product as it reaches its end-of-life.
Additionally, components should be responsibly sourced from suppliers that pay their employees fair wages.
The product should also be sustainable to the company from a financial point of view, without incurring high costs to the user. Governments or authorities could help users cover the cost of such products, whilst ensuring that the company charges a fair price that allows it to run its business.
Other:
Accessibility is an ethical concern that is relevant to this product. Its high cost means that only certain individuals will be able to afford it, which raises the ethical issue of access to a product that is essential for providing independence and mobility to individuals who may otherwise be limited. However, for those who can afford it, it restores their mobility and allows them to regain a sense of equality with an individual who has no physical impairment. The responsibility to ensure that everyone has the right to this level of independence corresponding to their mobility could therefore fall on governments, which could subsidise or fund patients who require access to such products for their mobility.
Designers or the company are also responsible for ensuring that the design is inclusive, and suitable for different genders or demographics, as well as customisable for persons with various disabilities, depending on regulations for inclusivity.

Name: P107
Informed consent: Since the main purpose of the Nike Gym Shirt is to collect a users data, the informed consent is probably not a major issue for this product. When a user buys an IoT product like this, he/she should be aware that Nike is collecting the user's tracking data and communicating it with the cloud. It should however be communicated what exact data is collected and for what reason. Some data is obvious, like tracking ones heartbeat, but if the shirt can also track a person's conversations with the microphone that was initially meant for tracking breathing rhythms, the user should be well-informed. This could however be solved by not integrating any voice recognition in the system architecture. Another concern that I can come up with is when the shirt detects abnormal data for this user. E.g. I can imagine the Tech shirt could predict that a woman is pregnant because of an odd data change, or that someone is very sick because of a rise in temperature. Should Nike ask for consent to even check upon these possibilities? A good option would be for the user to be able to check or uncheck the option to take these possibilities into account.
Privacy: This product will have a large influence on your privacy. Some unwanted data that can be collected by the device are: Conversations, pregnancy predictions, sickness, mood. Conversations shouldn't be able to be followed by the tech shirt. This can be done, as mentioned before, by not integrating any voice recognition in the system architecture.The data should be communicated to the user (via bluetooth) before being transferred to the cloud.
Information security: When Nike would leak live data of your location and your activities, you can be a vulnerable target to robberies or attacks. Since joggers/runners generally want to carry at little stuff as possible with them, they might even want to hide their key close to their door before they leave the house. Therefore a leak of data that results in burglars knowing when you leave the house or where you are, could result in dangerous situations.
Physical safety: As the tech shirt will only collect data and has no direct actuators, the only possible physical harm done is through the connected mobile device. The mobile device can alarm (buzz, ring, display) which could frighten or distract the user while participating in traffic (e.g. when crossing a street), which could result in a traffic accident. The chance that this really  happens is rather small though.
Trust: A first concern is that of the alarming function; The user trusts it's shirt to notify and alarm his/her when he/she is overtraining, overheated or has other potential health problems. If the user relies on the shirt, one might feel 'invincible' during a training because the user knows he/she will be alarmed when something goes wrong. If the alarming function fails, this might cause overheating which can result in nausea or passing out.
Sustainability: I'm mainly concerned regarding end-of-life and recycling. How are the trackers and sensors integrated in the shirt? If something breaks, can it be easily repaired? Or should the whole shirt be replaced? If the sensors are very invisible, chances are high a shirt will be discarded as textile. This might mess things up the recycling plant and it could break the electronics (e.g. the battery) which can results in a release of harmful substances.
